```
Michael Wojcik wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> Michael Wojcik wrote:
…[109 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://java.sun.com/applets/jdk/1.4/demo/applets/GraphLayout/Graph.java

Good examples.

I completely agree that long lines are difficult to read and generally 
unnecessary (I use the concatenate operator ( + ) in C# to break literals if 
they are likely to run "out of view")

So, I think we agree on what is undesirable... :-)

That just leaves the issue of C# "attracting people who are disdainful of 
whitespace".

I don't think it does; you think it does. You showed some good examples and 
you admit to being sensitive on the issue of  code formatting. I actually 
agree with you and, as I can't produce evidence to support my position (that 
is not subjective), I shall withdraw and leave you the field. (Nevertheless 
retaining my own opinion... :-))

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Avoiding Java (Was: And you ask why I hate OMVS?)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-02T12:59:48+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7b2f46F20prdpU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<IBM-MAIN%200906232300039262.08A0@BAMA.UA.EDU> <7ao3utF20hlf1U1@mid.individual.net> <h2db5j02v65@news1.newsguy.com> <7avrf0F2m4f8U1@mid.individual.net> <h2fpvk$5ot$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <7avrf0F2m4f8U1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

No, Doc, you didn't misread.

Sometimes even a reasoned argument is not entirely enough to persuade or 
convince. While it may be a good argument, presented correctly, perhaps the 
evidence on which it is based is suspect or, as in this case, it really 
doesn't matter and is being argued "in passing".

Michael produced some solid cases to support his position. For me to do 
that, I would need to comb through web sites collecting examples that suit 
my position. (It isn't like they don't exist, it is just that it is work to 
collect them.) If this were something seriously important and there were 
fundamental disagreements in our respective positions regarding how code 
should be formatted, I would do the work and stand my ground. Such is not 
the case, so I'm happy to acknowledge his case is better (on this occasion) 
and withdraw. However, my opinion has not been changed.

I guess someone for whom everything is about winning arguments, and only 
that, would find this difficult to follow.

There IS a "bigger picture" which is how much time I can afford to spend in 
CLC. I enjoy my time here and I've always said that when I don't, I'll stop 
posting. But that time is not unlimited and it would be silly to simply 
waste it on unimportant debate for the sake of debate. (There are enough 
important things to debate without doing that.)

Yes, as a general Rule, of course reasoned argument must trump opinion, but, 
like many important "Rules" there are qualifiers to it:

1. The issue has to be important. (It is perfectly fine to believe whatever 
you want about unimportant things.)
2. The evidence has to be rock solid and irrefutable. (In this case, it 
isn't. I know I can produce equivalent cases to Michael's, but why bother? 
Whatever I collected would be based on my opinion and thus, subjective...)
3.  Winning arguments in Usenet may be a doubtful use of one's time and 
energy, UNLESS the issue is important. (See 1, above.)

If you have cleared out your cubicle and taken a hike over issues where you 
felt Reason was not being paid due deference, I can only suggest two things:

1.  Never quit unless it is a matter of the utmost import. Doing it as a 
"matter of Principle" is questionable. Sometimes a "Principle" is just a 
fossilized prejudice.
2.  If Reason lost the argument it was because one of the parties wasn't 
persuaded. In order to persuade people it is necessary to reach them. In 
order to reach them, it is neccessary to communicate. You cannot communicate 
if you are angry or the person you are trying to reach is angry.

I can relate to what you're saying because I too have cleared my cubicle and 
taken a hike... (Not during the last 20 years, though :-)) But I am much 
older now and wiser and, given the same situation again, I would handle it 
much differently.

Bottom line: Pick your battles.

Pete.
```

```
In article <7b2f46F20prdpU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <7avrf0F2m4f8U1@mid.individual.net>,
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>No, Doc, you didn't misread.

Quick... someone mark the calendar.

[snip]

>I guess someone for whom everything is about winning arguments, and only 
>that, would find this difficult to follow.

Paying greater heed to actual examples and Reasoned Argument than to mere 
opinion is not about 'winning arguments', Mr Dashwood... it is about how 
one sees and deals with that series of complex phenomena sometimes called 
'The World'.

[snip]

>Yes, as a general Rule, of course reasoned argument must trump opinion, but, 
>like many important "Rules" there are qualifiers to it:
>
>1. The issue has to be important. (It is perfectly fine to believe whatever 
>you want about unimportant things.)

I wonder what the quantum of 'importance' is and how one measures it... 
but in the end it seems to boil down to the ever-popular IF PROGRAM-RUNS 
PERFORM NEXT-ASSIGNMENT ELSE PERFORM CODE-LIKE-HELL UNTIL 
DAMNED-THING-WORKS.

>2. The evidence has to be rock solid and irrefutable. (In this case, it 
>isn't. I know I can produce equivalent cases to Michael's, but why bother? 
>Whatever I collected would be based on my opinion and thus, subjective...)

No, what you collected would be a set of examples which might then be set 
into a Reasoned Argument out of which could fall... a Conclusion.  One 
measures, one tests, one alters circumstances, one tests again and one 
builds up - one might hope - a series of Reproducible Results.

Reproducible Results are the hallmark of a Science, or so I was taught, 
e'er-so-long ago... remember when it was called 'Computer Science'?

>3.  Winning arguments in Usenet may be a doubtful use of one's time and 
>energy, UNLESS the issue is important. (See 1, above.)

Winning or losing arguments, in UseNet or elsewhere, can both result in 
Learning Something New.  Some folks I've met don't like Learning Something 
New... takes all kinds to make a world, I guess.

>
>If you have cleared out your cubicle and taken a hike over issues where you 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>fossilized prejudice.

There were times when I left of my own accord, Mr Dashwood... and there 
were times when I was Fired.  I've worked on enough sites to have found a 
few where Managers will terminate a consultant who has *dared* to counter 
the Manager's opinion with examples, Reasoned Arguments and Reproducible 
Results.

(I've also worked on sites where a Manager has said the equivalent of 'It 
is my way or the highway'... in fact, that's kind of what happened at 
General Motors two-decades-and-change ago when they bought EDS, gave H 
Ross Perot a seat on the Board of Directors... and then gave him US$1 
billion to Just Go Away because he said Bad Things about them.)

>2.  If Reason lost the argument it was because one of the parties wasn't 
>persuaded. In order to persuade people it is necessary to reach them. In 
>order to reach them, it is neccessary to communicate. You cannot communicate 
>if you are angry or the person you are trying to reach is angry.

You cannot communicate with someone if you speak only Suomic and their 
only tongue is Togalog... but Reason does not lose here.  The important 
thing, as I mentioned earlier, is that in winning *or* losing one can 
learn... and Learning is, to my mind, paramount.

[snip]

>Bottom line: Pick your battles.

Battles?  So... big and sloppy and consuming of resources, Mr Dashwood.  
There might be less need for battles were a few more well-trained, 
properly-deployed assassins kept amongst the troops.

DD
```

```
In article <PaednYsRmLOExdHXnZ2dnUVZ_qydnZ2d@giganews.com>,
Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@att.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <zcOdnZRAvY6oadTXnZ2dnUVZ_sOdnZ2d@giganews.com>,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> to be overridden.

[snip]

>> In one of my earlier messages in this thread I asked something about the 
>> conditions which would cause the Powers That Be to disable the screen... I 
>> don't recall getting a direct answer but from the above it seems that 
>> their condition is 'Any time We want to, for any reason We want to.'

Notice, Mr Trembley, that the change in what screen is used seems, in 
effect, to be arbitrary.

>> 
>> Have you thought about doing it by DFHCOMMAREA (Linkage), a flag and *two* 
>> maps?  I assume that changing the maps is something that is not done 
>> without Executive Decree... so instead of digging out source and doing 
>> gooey things with CEMT change the code in the program that sends the map.

[snip]

>> That way... the code is always there, when word comes down from On High 
>> that the map is to be disabled... a FileAid change of a single byte does 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>update access to a production file using FileAid.

Yes... and no.  

>The Auditors might 
>object to that.  It might be better if there were a special program and 
>MAP (with restricted security access) to update the byte switch that 
>turns the application on and off.

Bingo.  The Original Poster was looking at a change in processing (which 
MAP to SEND) based on change in data ('Word From on High', as it were) and 
decided on a certain code-based solution which would have to be performed 
twice (turn off The Usual, turn on Keep Out and then reversed) when Word 
was given.

Instead of giving a complete and off-the-shelf solution (for a couple of 
reasons, one being that I am not sufficiently familiar with the 
environment to do so and another being that I like getting paid for Doing 
My Job) I suggested that instead of concentrating on the code the 
concentration be changed to data... code the program and MAPS once and 
then let the data do the driving.

If Auditors - folks whom an Auditor once described to me as 'having the 
job of going across the battlefield and bayonetting the not-yet-dead') - 
are of concern then I would assume that they are already involved, that 
when Word comes the program-change gets documented and all are kept 
satisfied.

The FileAid solution was purposefully given as being quick, dirty and 
out-of-Audit compliance... just start thinking *about the data instead of 
the code*, that's all, and what could be changed in the data so that the 
code need not be changed any more.  How this gets done in a fashion that 
satisfies all parties involved... well, I'd like to start discussing 
rates, first.

DD
```

<a id="t-eec3e5f813"></a>
## Thread: What is executable file ext for Micro Focus COBOL/2 Ver 1.2.29 L2.2 rev 003 on old DOS PC ? — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### What is executable file ext for Micro Focus COBOL/2 Ver 1.2.29 L2.2 rev 003 on old DOS PC ?

- **From:** Mel_3 <myemaillist@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-04T11:54:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<207e1839-3d68-41ab-8b79-2c97c4544414@j32g2000yqh.googlegroups.com>`

```
THE SITUATION:
- I'm trying to resurrect an old custom COBOL ap that ran on an old
DOS PC.
- The program used a DOS GUI App HI-SCREEN Pro II by PC Soft as the
interface to the Ao.,
- I'm having trouble getting HI-SCREEN Pro II to work...
- and want to search for the actual compiled COBOL file(s) to get the
darn'd thing to work.

But I'm not a COBOL programmer.

The QUESTION:
What file extension is used for a compiled Micro Focus COBOL program
that was running on an old DOS 6.2 PC ? Would I look for a .COM
or .EXE file type? or what?

Thanks for any help.
```

#### ↳ Re: What is executable file ext for Micro Focus COBOL/2 Ver 1.2.29 L2.2 rev 003 on old DOS PC ?

- **From:** riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-04T14:00:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<33782f77-a97b-443b-95a8-c6e059616d31@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<207e1839-3d68-41ab-8b79-2c97c4544414@j32g2000yqh.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Jul 5, 6:54 am, Mel_3 <myemaill...@gmail.com> wrote:
> THE SITUATION:
> - I'm trying to resurrect an old custom COBOL ap that ran on an old
> DOS PC.
> - The program used a DOS GUI App HI-SCREEN Pro II by PC Soft as the
> interface to the Ao.,

I used that (well I played with it) and have a copy here.

> - I'm having trouble getting HI-SCREEN Pro II to work...
> - and want to search for the actual compiled COBOL file(s) to get the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> or .EXE file type? or what?

Most binary executables are .EXE but the compiler can output .INT
'intermediate' code which is a byte code that is executed by the run-
time or .GNT which is 'generated' and has much machine code in it.
These are equivalent to Java byte code and the output from a Java JIT
respectively.

Or the .int and/or .gnt can be linked and encapsulated into a .EXE
which is what I usually did.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is executable file ext for Micro Focus COBOL/2 Ver 1.2.29 L2.2 rev 003 on old DOS PC ?

- **From:** Mel_3 <myemaillist@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-04T14:22:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c1b91c64-ed22-4dad-b189-99ffc920936b@q11g2000yqi.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<207e1839-3d68-41ab-8b79-2c97c4544414@j32g2000yqh.googlegroups.com> <33782f77-a97b-443b-95a8-c6e059616d31@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com>`

```
Ah Ha... you have a copy of HI-Screen Pro II ???
```

```
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Screen Pro & the copyright holder PC Soft

DISPLAY.COM loads as a TSR

The hs_beg or hs_end would be .COM files that set up and tear down the
TSR environment.

RUN is the Cobol runtime environment. I would have expected MSTRMENU
to be a .INT or a .GNT file and the .MNU to be a data file perhaps or
perhaps a HS screen definition file.

>
> RUN MSTRMENU should, I think, run the HI-Screen Pro II file

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> know... I need a little help on this.

Do you not have manuals ?

Are you running this on a real MS-DOS or similar. Note that NT/XP/
Vista console is not MS-DOS and probably won't run the DISPLAY.COM
TSR.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is executable file ext for Micro Focus COBOL/2 Ver 1.2.29 L2.2 rev 003 on old DOS PC ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-05T12:52:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<827b23a7-49e1-426b-92d0-ec763d0c87a8@d7g2000prl.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<207e1839-3d68-41ab-8b79-2c97c4544414@j32g2000yqh.googlegroups.com> <33782f77-a97b-443b-95a8-c6e059616d31@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <c1b91c64-ed22-4dad-b189-99ffc920936b@q11g2000yqi.googlegroups.com> <f837b4af-57b2-41c7-b978-4c807bca11d0@o18g2000pra.googlegroups.com> <e64ec78f-eea7-42a7-b5c3-3cae021b076b@p29g2000yqh.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Jul 6, 1:39 am, Mel_3 <myemaill...@gmail.com> wrote:
> Thanks for the great help !
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> MstrMenu.INT

.CBL is the Cobol source file.  .INT is the byte coded compiler
output. .IDY is the debug references. .GNT is the generated program
(ie JIT compiled). It is the .GNT that will be run.


> To review... the last few lines of MstrMenu.BAT are...
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Do you know what this error message means?

I don't have your version of MS Cobol but it looks like a message from
Cobol because it can't find the run-time libraries. These would be
similar to COBENV.DLE and should be on the COBLIB= environment.

> FYI I think hs-beg and Display.com are executing correctly because...
> ..Once MstrMenu.bat aborts with the error message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> .. and Display.com got installed as it should... right?

After running hs-end the DISPLAY.COM TSR should _no_longer_ be
loaded.  'begin' puts a marker in memory, 'end' restores the free
memory back to that marker and resets the interrupt vectors.


> I'm running a DOS 6.2 as a Virtual Machine - on an Windows XP machine
> - under Sun Micro's VirtualBox
> And DOS seems to be working fine as a virtual machine... I've ran a
> number of other app's no problem.
> Hopefully I can get this one to work as well.


> No manuals at all. This was a custom Ap and the developer can not be
> found.
>
> Thanks again for your kind assistance on this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is executable file ext for Micro Focus COBOL/2 Ver 1.2.29 L2.2 rev 003 on old DOS PC ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Mel_3 <myemaillist@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-06T06:49:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a1a41e50-4283-4d22-a964-449133a22516@d9g2000prh.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<207e1839-3d68-41ab-8b79-2c97c4544414@j32g2000yqh.googlegroups.com> <33782f77-a97b-443b-95a8-c6e059616d31@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <c1b91c64-ed22-4dad-b189-99ffc920936b@q11g2000yqi.googlegroups.com> <f837b4af-57b2-41c7-b978-4c807bca11d0@o18g2000pra.googlegroups.com> <e64ec78f-eea7-42a7-b5c3-3cae021b076b@p29g2000yqh.googlegroups.com> <827b23a7-49e1-426b-92d0-ec763d0c87a8@d7g2000prl.googlegroups.com>`

```
Got it working!
Somehow when files were copied... file names that contained a dash
changed to an under bar so...

my-file.abc became my_file.abc

But in the batch files that setup and launch the system... the dash
remained in the file name...

So nothing worked.

One of your comments led me to very carefully review the batch
files... and find each file they referenced... including the files
referenced with the SET command... finally, just like finding a syntax
error when debugging code... I saw it and the light went off... I
fixed all the file names and da da... the darn thing worked.

Now I have DOS 6.2 running as a virtual machine on a Windows XP box
using Sun's VirtualBox application... and I have the very old Micro
Focus COBOL application up and running... just like it was on the old
486 PC... only now it is on modern hardware... very cool indeed !

Thanks again for all the fine help!

Best.

Mel_3
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thoughts on refactoring COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-06T08:55:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On Sun, 28 Jun 2009 09:29:27 -0500, "Michael Mattias"
<mmattias@talsystems.com> wrote:

>I have found that applications have a finite number of times they can be 
>enhanced before it's time to just start over, desiging the application to 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>mind, this does not occur for many years; but it wasn't.

My experience has been very similar.

Furthermore, we tend to hold onto a process long after the job that
that process was designed to do should have changed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thoughts on refactoring COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-07T10:42:57+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bfcviF22rp2rU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7a979kF1thpfnU1@mid.individual.net> <NYK%l.242$kA.39@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <7aahtjF1tqaksU1@mid.individual.net> <B8L1m.10804$aX1.1832@nlpi067.nbdc.sbc.com> <104455dsavfl15m48fdorf9ofo1hhkffmf@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Sun, 28 Jun 2009 09:29:27 -0500, "Michael Mattias"
> <mmattias@talsystems.com> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> that process was designed to do should have changed.

I believe it comes down to "granularity".

Even leaving OO aside for the moment, modular systems comprised of called 
modules are easier to maintain and have a longer shelf life generally, than 
processes that are implemented as large monolithic blocks of code. Small is 
beautiful. It costs less to maintain and it runs more efficiently in online 
environments. ("Big" is good for batch...)

Certainly, there will be cases where the cost of refactoring is not less 
than the cost of rewriting, but there are many more cases where it is much 
less to refactor and salvage. (Especially if there are tools available to 
automate some or all of it...). If you can salvage say, 60% of the 
functionality, even (sometimes you can get considerably more than that),, 
then that 60% that you didn't have to rewrite can get you new enhancements 
for free.

I'm not suggesting that refactoring should be applied unconditionallly or 
exclusively; all I have ever said is that it can be an option and can go 
into the mix with other options.

I have one client who simply COULDN'T afford to rewrite his application from 
scratch. He'd go broke. The time and cost of rewriting would be more than 
the business can support. And even if he did, he'd just have a new COBOL 
system which really gets him no further ahead. By refactoring to objects he 
can use those objects with ANY language he decides to implement (including 
COBOL). His migration can be phased at a pace he can support. There is time 
for training and coming to grips with a new environment.At the end of it he 
has a component based system that can be easily reconfigured and enhanced.

For smaller businesses the option to redevelop everything simply may not be 
viable. Migrating to a component based approach keeps options open and 
salvaging exisiting code into components makes sense for that environment 
(provided it doesn't cost a fortune to do it; that is why I am looking at 
automating it or at least assisting the process with tools...)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thoughts on refactoring COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-07T08:28:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rfm655dcmgs8hv6ga206vpdi6cfcmlbdph@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7a979kF1thpfnU1@mid.individual.net> <NYK%l.242$kA.39@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <7aahtjF1tqaksU1@mid.individual.net> <B8L1m.10804$aX1.1832@nlpi067.nbdc.sbc.com> <104455dsavfl15m48fdorf9ofo1hhkffmf@4ax.com> <7bfcviF22rp2rU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Tue, 7 Jul 2009 10:42:57 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>> My experience has been very similar.
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>environments. ("Big" is good for batch...)

Or maybe big is beautiful:    
Small is machine language, 
bigger granularity is assembly language, 
then compiled language, 
then tool rich languages that have us drag components together.   

...

>I have one client who simply COULDN'T afford to rewrite his application from 
>scratch. He'd go broke. The time and cost of rewriting would be more than 
>the business can support. 

If one can't afford to write your application around your business
needs, one should consider using an off-the-shelf solution that one
can adapt one's business to.   These come in all sizes and costs. 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thoughts on refactoring COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-07-07T15:23:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h2vpa1$5n0$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<7a979kF1thpfnU1@mid.individual.net> <104455dsavfl15m48fdorf9ofo1hhkffmf@4ax.com> <7bfcviF22rp2rU1@mid.individual.net> <rfm655dcmgs8hv6ga206vpdi6cfcmlbdph@4ax.com>`

```
In article <rfm655dcmgs8hv6ga206vpdi6cfcmlbdph@4ax.com>,
Howard Brazee  <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>On Tue, 7 Jul 2009 10:42:57 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
><dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>>I have one client who simply COULDN'T afford to rewrite his application from 
>>scratch. He'd go broke. The time and cost of rewriting would be more than 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>can adapt one's business to.   These come in all sizes and costs. 

I'm glad someone else caught this one.  If the business had changed to the 
point where the old application no longer 'fit' then there are various 
possibilities for the reason.

One is that the amount of business had increased to the point where 
transaction flow was too large to be handled by the old system; if there's 
a nightly batch window of (n) hours and (y) transactions/hr could be 
processed then the business is limited to a maximum of (y * n) 
transactions and more than that exceeds the window.  This can sometimes be 
solved by throwing hardware at the situation; more core, faster chips, 
more disks to distribute the load; such business expenses are (in the USA) 
taxably depreciable over time.  Some new 'toys' for the gang in Ops could 
bring up processing speed to, say, (2y) transactions/hr.

Another is that the type of business had changed to the point where the 
'all ya gotta do is' patches just... don't do it.  If that's the case then 
new software, home-grown, COTS or COTS with source is a way out... if the 
hardware can still handle the transaction flow (see above).

Another possibility is for the owner to realise that the cost/benefit 
ratio of new customers cannot be made to justify hardware or software 
changes and just hold where he is; in certain professions (medicine, law, 
architecture, construction, etc) it is not all-too-uncommon to hear 
'Sorry, we're not taking on any new customers at this time.'  This is also 
called 'Turning Down Profit' and goes against many folks' grain.

So... what to do?  More information, of course, is needed, the most basic 
being 'what has changed so that what-used-to-work no longer does?'  If it 
is growth then... well, unless it was *very* sudden growth (the town has 
two widget-makers, one gets hit by a bus) then someone wasn't keeping up 
on the run-statistics.

If the kind of business changed... well, that's kind of like the 
widget-maker-hit-by-a-bus scenario.  'Janet just gave birth to a boy but 
we don't have to worry about new expenses for clothing, he can just wear 
his older sister's hand-me-downs'.  This might work in the short run... 
but what one saved on clothing might be outweighed if one finds one later 
that one has a 14-year-old boy in need of a bit of psychotherapy.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thoughts on refactoring COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-07T11:29:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<181755dtmq2j2s7at667t05rsq7eusj58c@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7a979kF1thpfnU1@mid.individual.net> <104455dsavfl15m48fdorf9ofo1hhkffmf@4ax.com> <7bfcviF22rp2rU1@mid.individual.net> <rfm655dcmgs8hv6ga206vpdi6cfcmlbdph@4ax.com> <h2vpa1$5n0$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Tue, 7 Jul 2009 15:23:45 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>Another possibility is for the owner to realise that the cost/benefit 
>ratio of new customers cannot be made to justify hardware or software 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>called 'Turning Down Profit' and goes against many folks' grain.

Medicine is an interesting case here.   Physicians were latecomers to
automation, as lack of automation didn't cost money.   It cost lives,
but not money.    But when the insurance industry changed, physicians
had to adjust to get paid in a timely manner.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thoughts on refactoring COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-07-07T17:50:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h301sn$aau$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<7a979kF1thpfnU1@mid.individual.net> <rfm655dcmgs8hv6ga206vpdi6cfcmlbdph@4ax.com> <h2vpa1$5n0$1@reader1.panix.com> <181755dtmq2j2s7at667t05rsq7eusj58c@4ax.com>`

```
In article <181755dtmq2j2s7at667t05rsq7eusj58c@4ax.com>,
Howard Brazee  <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>On Tue, 7 Jul 2009 15:23:45 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>had to adjust to get paid in a timely manner.

I'm not sure what fits into the category of 'automation' here (anyone for 
fluoroscopy?) but I am reminded of a conversation related to me by a 
physician.

It was in the mid-to-late 1960s and he was having trouble getting paid for 
an approved procedure by one of the Blues (Blue Cross or Blue Shield); 
after many fruitless calls and transfers he finally was told 'I'm sorry, 
but the girl who cuts the checks for reimbursements is on vacation this 
week, you'll have to call back after next Tuesday.'

His response was 'That's very interesting... tell me, when does the girl 
who deposits the checks for the premiums go 'on vacation'?'

The woman he was speaking with involuntarily laughed and said 'Oh, that 
*never* happ... all right, let me see what I can get done here.'

He received his check a mere four-to-six weeks after that.

DD
```

```
Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thoughts on refactoring COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-07-08T13:26:03+00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thoughts on refactoring COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-08T11:34:20+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bi4bsF23k8nnU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7a979kF1thpfnU1@mid.individual.net> <NYK%l.242$kA.39@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <7aahtjF1tqaksU1@mid.individual.net> <B8L1m.10804$aX1.1832@nlpi067.nbdc.sbc.com> <104455dsavfl15m48fdorf9ofo1hhkffmf@4ax.com> <7bfcviF22rp2rU1@mid.individual.net> <rfm655dcmgs8hv6ga206vpdi6cfcmlbdph@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Tue, 7 Jul 2009 10:42:57 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> then tool rich languages that have us drag components together.

No, I'm talking about executable size, not instruction execution.

The size of a module or component.

In online environments there are three factors which govern performance:

1. Queue wait time.
2. Load time.
3. Capture time.

Smaller modules load faster, allow better memory management, and are less 
likely to have long capture times.  For online processing, small is 
beautiful.

For batch processing, queue wait and load time are irrelevant and all that 
matters is capture time. In these instances the larger something is, 
probably, the better.

Granularity concerns the degreeof "modularization" in a system. How many 
"atoms" is it composed of? If it needs to be maintained and there are many 
"atoms", it is less likely to have knock on effects than if the granularity 
is larger. Again, small is beautiful.  Components are encapsulated and carry 
out specific defined functions.A given component will seldom be more than a 
couple of thousand lines of code (in COBOL) and usually hundreds of lines, 
rather than thousands...)) Component based systems therefore tend to have 
smaller granularity. This means a component can be easily replaced without 
knock on effects on the rest of the system.

>
> ...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> can adapt one's business to.   These come in all sizes and costs.

I can't discuss this in too much detail, but I can tell you, you are way off 
the beam.

D'you seriously think I wouldn't advise someone to buy a package if that 
made sense, Howard?

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thoughts on refactoring COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-08T08:57:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5gc955djnoged4k2bnjvvquuckln24o18s@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7a979kF1thpfnU1@mid.individual.net> <NYK%l.242$kA.39@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <7aahtjF1tqaksU1@mid.individual.net> <B8L1m.10804$aX1.1832@nlpi067.nbdc.sbc.com> <104455dsavfl15m48fdorf9ofo1hhkffmf@4ax.com> <7bfcviF22rp2rU1@mid.individual.net> <rfm655dcmgs8hv6ga206vpdi6cfcmlbdph@4ax.com> <7bi4bsF23k8nnU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Wed, 8 Jul 2009 11:34:20 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>>> Even leaving OO aside for the moment, modular systems comprised of
>>> called modules are easier to maintain and have a longer shelf life
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>No, I'm talking about executable size, not instruction execution.

OK.   When you said "It costs less to maintain", I figured you were
talking about code modularity, not run efficiency.  

Mainframes can afford to have run-time speed optimized at the expense
of larger executables.   Distributed environments bog down with I/O,
so offloading "small" commands to do a lot is efficient.   But in my
experience, this doesn't make maintenance cost less.

In my experience, the most effective distributed computing
efficiencies though have to do with being better at extracting a
minimal amount of data off of the server.   I wouldn't describe this
as a smaller granularity either though.   It is just adjusting the
computing and data transfer in the right places.

Lots of times, much of this adjustment is done by DBAs and other
systems people.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thoughts on refactoring COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-07-08T17:39:38-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3k0a55928rj9ckjenhuv8v7bv6i0i434ml@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7a979kF1thpfnU1@mid.individual.net> <NYK%l.242$kA.39@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <7aahtjF1tqaksU1@mid.individual.net> <B8L1m.10804$aX1.1832@nlpi067.nbdc.sbc.com> <104455dsavfl15m48fdorf9ofo1hhkffmf@4ax.com> <7bfcviF22rp2rU1@mid.individual.net> <rfm655dcmgs8hv6ga206vpdi6cfcmlbdph@4ax.com> <7bi4bsF23k8nnU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Wed, 8 Jul 2009 11:34:20 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Howard Brazee wrote:
>> On Tue, 7 Jul 2009 10:42:57 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>beautiful.

It ain't so simple.  Interface logic can be expensive.  IBM came out
with XPLINK to handle C/C++ better by reducing intermodule overhead.
In many systems such as CICS, a module may be loaded on first request
and left resident in memory with each user getting its own data areas.
Various weird and wonderful optimizations can be done when all of the
source is in the same compile unit (C includes, COBOL nested programs,
etc.).  I know that I was surprised at the size of CICS COBOL modules
that I found in many vendor packages.
>
>> rest snipped
>
>Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Thoughts on refactoring COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-07-12T20:43:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbx6m.102982$K24.54410@newsfe19.iad>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3k0a55928rj9ckjenhuv8v7bv6i0i434ml@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7a979kF1thpfnU1@mid.individual.net> <NYK%l.242$kA.39@nlpi068.nbdc.sbc.com> <7aahtjF1tqaksU1@mid.individual.net> <B8L1m.10804$aX1.1832@nlpi067.nbdc.sbc.com> <104455dsavfl15m48fdorf9ofo1hhkffmf@4ax.com> <7bfcviF22rp2rU1@mid.individual.net> <rfm655dcmgs8hv6ga206vpdi6cfcmlbdph@4ax.com> <7bi4bsF23k8nnU1@mid.individual.net> <3k0a55928rj9ckjenhuv8v7bv6i0i434ml@4ax.com>`

```
Clark F Morris wrote:
> On Wed, 8 Jul 2009 11:34:20 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Well, to my mind OO is simpler. In effect you were making a reference to 
the old term TSR (Terminate and Stay Resident). It looks a bit kludgey 
above, but nevertheless I can see it is doing the job.

I don't recall what my quesiton was, but in the M/F Forum, around 2000, 
Steve Biggs told me they cached methods. (Period). Any more information 
would have been proprietary.

So my guess, and solely my guess, this is what they possibly do. (I'm 
talking only Micro Focus; something parallel might happen with Fujitsu).
They have a memory area to store methods, (perhaps even the whole of a 
class).

Using a Pareto analysis, '80% of the current processing comes from 20% 
of the current active methods'. (That's a given. Ever dabbled with 
Pareto - doesn't matter whether you are talking about your shopping 
basket you are dragging around a supermarket, types of counter bank 
transactions during the course of a day, a League Table of 
Vendors/Customers/Inventory items, or in this case, OO methods).They 
aren't using either FIFO or LIFO - but a descending League Table. For 
convenience let's refer to it as their TSR. The TSR League Table is 
constantly expanding/contracting, and adding/subtracting based on a 
count they keep for each active method in a List (Dictionary), using 
their own Collection/Dictionary classes. As a method becomes moribund, 
it is removed from the TSR League Table, making room for a different 
method to be added

The key to this is keeping methods 'Small and beautiful', assigned to 
one particular requirement - not a multi-option method.

I qualified above that they may even be keeping whole classes in the 
TSR, but I somehow doubt it.

The criteria above,

 >>1. Queue wait time.
 >>2. Load time.
 >>3. Capture time.

No doubt it is there in M/F but I don't see it. The speed is TERRIFIC.

An example : When VISOC first came out, followed by Net Express, the 
Dialog Editor had icons to create dialogs, but there was never an icon 
for Listviews, and there still isn't. So I confused the terms Listbox 
and Listview as being one and the same, when they are very different 
animals. (I was not aware of the Dialog System until it was released 
with N/E Version 1 or 2. the Dialog System gives pre-packaged solutions 
to creating dialogs).

Primarily I needed a 10 x 24 table; Columns = 10 dates, and rows, 
between 1 to 15 items on average; it would be extremely rare for the 
active rows to get above 15. Using the Dialog Editor associated with GUI 
classes. I built the two-dimensional table but there were also other 
entry/display fields, plus labels indicating if the data displayed was 
in Imperial or Metric.  A total of 320 objects (i.e., 240 for the table 
plus the rest).

First time I displayed it via program, half of my dialog was missing. 
Not documented but there is obviously a limit of 256 objects per dialog 
using the Dialog Editor. Damn ! So now reluctantly I had to create the 
dialog dynamically, figuring out the width and height of the table entry 
fields and a formula to slot them next to one another per row. (Not bad 
but not as elegant as a Listview - but to recap, I didn't know that 
existed when doing this).

Select the data entry program from the menu and with the merest pause, 
perhaps 10 seconds, that dynamically created dialog appears on your 
screen. Certainly not sufficient time to go and grab a coffee before the 
display.

My suppositions above maybe completely out of whack, but whatever M/F 
are doing it ain't kludgey :-)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

---

<a id="t-36e8f7149b"></a>
## Thread: Windows Start Menu — 26 msgs

### Windows Start Menu

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-07-06T21:24:36-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tdz4m.16991$Wx7.10819@newsfe04.iad>`

```
Not COBOL per se, but something we are all interested in.

Still with security problems, (which I will summarize in a separate 
message), I downloaded Ad-Aware. Gave me a full scan approx 2 hours 30 
mins as opposed to AVG V 8, approx 3 hours 30 mins. Didn't appear to 
improve performance and the machine was sluggish. Sure enough, do a 
Right Click on an icon and the resulting dropdown menu shows two options :-

	Scan with AVG
	Scan with Ad-Aware

As we know from what Pete wrote these two are hogging the machine 
competing against one another. I wanted to retain both, (for 
comparison), but get Ad-Aware out of my Start-Up. I did a search on the 
Start Up using the obvious phrases "Remove from Startup Menu", "Delete 
from ...", etc.... No dice, have to search on "ADD to Startup", which 
gives relevant procedure and as a footnote it mentions REMOVE. Getting 
into Startup I have 6 items, but no list of icons nor are AVG or 
Ad-Aware listed.

As a last step I looked at Ad-Aware itself and got to a screen showing, 
way at the bottom a check-box "Add to start Menu" which showed a check 
mark/tick which they have as a default. Cheeky buggers. I was never 
prompted to fill that check box. Needless to say switching the check box 
off resolved the problem.

But there surely has to be an easier way without going to individual 
applications, to Add or Remove them from the Startup Menu. I either 
misunderstood what MS were saying, or their Help is badly worded. Any 
takers ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
I think the single MOST IMPORTANT thing for the average user is NEVER 
download or run a so called "optimizer" that promises ot improve 
performance. Most of these are simply Registry cleaners that have little or 
no effect on anything and can be dangerous. If you are experienceing serious 
performance problems, the best course of action is to post to a MS forum 
describing exactly what happens.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:
>
> Recently, my company laptop had some performance problems, and the two
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.microsoft.com/windowsxp/downloads/powertoys/xppowertoys.mspx

De-fragging an NTFS disk is almost worthless. There ARE situations in which 
defragging can help, but they are rare. NTFS is heavily optimized for 
searching and fetchin.

Still, if you want to defrag an XP disk, run Disk Cleanup first. No sense 
defragging IE history, temporary files, and assorted other dead stuff.

As to the START menu, open a command prompt and enter MSCONFIG. Disable the 
stuff you don't want in the Start Tab.
```

```
wrote:

>Arnold Trembley wrote:
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It is not worthless.  NTFS may be optimized as you say, but if
everything you want is at the beginning of the drive instead of being
scattered throughout, then searching and retrieving are significantly
faster.  I defrag my hard-drive on a semi-annual basis and I always
see a boost in performance afterwards.  It also doesn't hurt to do
full disk scans on at least a bi-monthly basis.


>Still, if you want to defrag an XP disk, run Disk Cleanup first. No sense 
>defragging IE history, temporary files, and assorted other dead stuff.
>

Good tip.

>As to the START menu, open a command prompt and enter MSCONFIG. Disable the 
>stuff you don't want in the Start Tab. 
>

Also a good tip.

Regards,

imagine my disappointment when you came along."
```

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> Concerning "MSCONFIG" and clean up stuff, I have been using CCLeaner recently 
> and found it pretty good in cleaning up "temp" stuff AND it has a fairly decent 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Thanks Bill. Took my life in my hands and gave CCLeaner a go 
specifically the Registry Cleaner.

But before that I used RegCure which I had downloaded and subsequently 
paid for because it was specifically marketed as a cure for 
Windows32\services.exe problems - but see below.

EACH time I run Regcure, and as Pete observed, perhaps a non-starter in 
terms of end-user help, it lists errors by 11 categories. First runs, 
there are errors all over the place; gradually they get reduced by 
subsequent runs. However, consistently there are errors for Path 
Filenames and Empty Registry Items. I have never got the errors down to 
ZERO even when I immediately did a second scan.

So before trying CCleaner - Registry

1. Run Regcure - Path Filenames 18, Empty Registry Items 97, Total 115; 
Clean-up = Zero
2. Run CCLeaner - Registry - big list but not a count that I saw. Delete 
whole list.
3. Repeat Step 2 - roughly 10 items perhaps - Delete whole list.
4. Back to Regcure - 28 Empty Registry Items - Fix All
5. Back to CCLeaner - zippo - no entries.
6. Thought OK - give Regcure another go. Step 4 above (Regcure) informed 
me it got rid of 28 entries - whereas Step 5 (CCLeaner) said zero. So 
with Regcure still loaded I did another scan. No surprise, we still have 
a figure of 5 for Empty Registry Items.

That's the sort of thing I was referring to when I said I don't trust 
any of them totally. At this point I favour the following FREE Versions 
- AVG V 8, Ad-Aware and CCLeaner from what I have achieved so far.

One NOT to try is Registry Cleaner. A Free Download, (they really mean 
of course a Free Trial). Without knowing, I wouldn't mind betting they 
hike the numbers. They plague the life out of you if you don't complete 
the sign-up. Can't remember sequence, but I was filling in their info 
box to <Submit>, had entered my e-mail address - noticed something about 
the price and quit the dialog. Smart boys :-

a) On event TabKey store entry-field contents, if any
b) On event Cancel (and perhaps Quit) -
	check if the TabKey entryfield contains what looks like a valid
	e-mail address then pester the living daylights out of the
	potential sucker with a host of e-mails enumerating disasters
	that can occur by not using their software.

Not sure which checker I was using - but for screen display it was 
listing items for the category, "Checking potential areas where 
viruses/Trojans can be placed", with pathnames of course.  Well how 
about that. Could it be that hackers know exactly where not to place 
their 'bombs' and place them somewhere else.

SERVICES.EXE :

I wont write separately about Security as I intended, so I'll cover 
Win32\services.exe here.  I should mention I no longer try and use Trend 
Micro Housecalls - it has lousy communication screens and I found that 
it was telling me it was going through phases, but leaving it alone for 
some 30 minutes I got the message it was in a loop. (To be fair to 
Housecalls if may have been negaged by other machine problems that it 
couldn't get around. But I satill don't like their screens/dialogs).So 
far as I could tell, initially using AVG and TM Housecall it looked like 
I had cured my problems. Then keeping the Windows Task Manager active I 
found performance was just plain awful and established I was getting 
"Not Responding" on Mozilla Firefox (Google), and kept highlighting 
different lines in the Task Manager until it signaled Firefox was running.

One of the checkers suggested Firefox had been hijacked - that I could 
understand very clearly from performance. So I switched to IE, which I 
don't normally use and there was a prompt to download Google Chrome, 
which I did. They claim it's a cut down version of the full Google in IE 
or Firefox and at this stage somewhat experimental, so they will 
occasionally give you a chirpy message like "Oops ! We are sorry for the 
inconvenience but we have a problem. We need to shut Chrome down and 
Restart - Yes or No".

For info - I can access the Prima Computing site from Google Chrome.

I didn't get any "Not Responding" messages from the Task Manager on 
either IE or Chrome. BUT......... the next one that came up, and I don't 
have screens to quote, "Application Sharing Error (or whatever the title 
is) followed by some text with a <OK> or <Cancel>. Don't know why they 
bothered with the <Cancel> - you get the same result - a small square 
Dialog telling you there are errors blah, blah and the error is 
something like "Error -5896743". And we will shut your machine down in 
60 seconds and Restart.

Sorry for being a pedantic former Systems Analyst - but why tell me you 
are shutting down and restarting when the machine will contain the same 
error ? That informative error number above - go check the Microsloth 
site - you gotta be kidding. You get some help from MS on services.exe, 
some KB articles devoted to specific problems and a caution to only use 
with your problem - how come fixes to services.exe are not cumulative? 
Yes Richard, obviously I'm being naive again.

I tried Safe Mode but without selecting 'Safe Mode with Networking' I 
couldn't e-mail or browse. Devices like the Lexmark printer aren't 
loaded for Safe Modes and AVG does only a short hand version scan with a 
DOS screen white on black, top line only. So I experimented on recovery 
in Safe Mode and when the different types of services.exe error dialogs 
came up dragged them to the bottom right out of sight so I could carry 
on working. Occasionally, but not on a daily basis, I responded to an MS 
Dialog 'Send Error Report' or 'Ignore'.

Avast3 is one security checker I haven't tried yet (info passed on by 
our MS Excel expert - "List of Invokes"). Avast3 is a Freebie but their 
latest is Avast 8 - Free Trial then pay. I used a mix of the stuff above 
and eventually Ad-Aware seems to have done the trick - but probably a 
combination with the others. I still haven't been brave enough to 
re-load Firefox as Google Chrome seems adequate. Still getting those 
services.exe errors so repeatedly as one occurred I triggered 'Send 
Error Report'. I did sometimes quickly see the word 'Updating.....'  in 
Start-Up - so I'm assuming that's our friends in Bellingham, Wa. God 
bless their little hearts the services.exe problem completely 
disappeared.(No doubt they were pissed off that some clown in the Great 
White North was continuously complaining).

Now I'm home and dry, and back to using Windows in normal mode, or so I 
thought. I think somebody here, years ago, referred to it at the 
'Dreaded Blue Screen'. "You'se got serious problems buddy and we are 
shutting down to avoid damaging your machine" while at the same time 
doing a memory dump to disk - but they don't tell you why or where the 
dump will be filed.

 From memory (Give me a break - how many screen shots of exactly what 
happened or was displayed do you think I could possibly make while all 
the above nonsense is going on). They suggested the Safe Mode routine, 
which I had already discovered - tapping F8 while still in initial 
Start-Up. Second suggestion was Restore. Quite painless. Comes up with a 
calendar dialog which lets you select an earlier date when things 
appeared to work, prior to your current misfortune.

It APPEARS to work now - but I await the next disaster. Having loaded 
the desktop and icons there is a definite pause. Left clicking on icons 
seems sluggish and I have better performance with Right double-click and 
select 'Open'. From that point on performance seems to improve - but you 
never know............

If I have to utilize my time I would much rather do gardening in our 
short summers rather than try and become an expert in computer 'innards' 
technology. The latter turns me on as much as the thought of servicing 
my own car !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> William M. Klein wrote:
>> Concerning "MSCONFIG" and clean up stuff, I have been using CCLeaner
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Windows32\services.exe problems - but see below.

Jimmy, they ALL say that. Any cowboy trying to flog a RegCleaner will pick 
up your search terms and insert them into his ad so it looks like he has a 
specific solution to your problem. General rule is NEVER download these 
scams.


>
> EACH time I run Regcure, and as Pete observed, perhaps a non-starter
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Probably not worth worrying about.

> That's the sort of thing I was referring to when I said I don't trust
> any of them totally. At this point I favour the following FREE
> Versions - AVG V 8, Ad-Aware and CCLeaner from what I have achieved
> so far.

They are all good. So is Avast!.

I hope you backed up the Registry before running any of this...:-)

>
> One NOT to try is Registry Cleaner. A Free Download, (they really mean
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> their 'bombs' and place them somewhere else.

Fortunately for us, they cannot just place them anywhere because they'd 
possibly never get executed. Certain locations are favoured for this reason.

>
> SERVICES.EXE :
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> couldn't get around. But I satill don't like their screens/dialogs).

It is a useful tool and it is free. However, I agree with you that the User 
Interface could be better.



>So
> far as I could tell, initially using AVG and TM Housecall it looked
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Firefox was running.

Did you have multiple instances of it running?


>
> One of the checkers suggested Firefox had been hijacked - that I could
> understand very clearly from performance.

Jimmy, as mentioned previously, download and run "HiJackThis". It will 
produce a log that definitively shows whether you are hijacked or not, along 
with suspicious signatures, startups, and general malware detection. The log 
can be used by people who know what they're doing to give you a clean bill 
of health. The same people can give you specific actions and cures for 
things revealed by the log. There's a user community who do all this for 
free. I strongly recommend it. Over years I have become reasonably 
proficient at analysing these logs (I don't claim specialist expertise in 
it; there are people availabe who do have that expertise and they are 
usually glad to help), and I still run it every six weeks or so and check 
the log produced. It's a bit like keeping the "official" guardians honest.



>So I switched to IE, which I
> don't normally use and there was a prompt to download Google Chrome,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> and Restart - Yes or No".

>
> For info - I can access the Prima Computing site from Google Chrome.
>
Thanks for that. I'm glad it renders OK. Have not tried Chrome myself.

> I didn't get any "Not Responding" messages from the Task Manager on
> either IE or Chrome. BUT......... the next one that came up, and I
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 60 seconds and Restart.

This is almost certainly caused by a conflict between Chrome and IE. Try 
removing Chrome and see if it still happens.
>
> Sorry for being a pedantic former Systems Analyst - but why tell me
> you are shutting down and restarting when the machine will contain
> the same error ?

If you were really a pedantic former Systems Analyst you would know that 
this causes .dlls to be reloaded and the sharing conflict to be resolved... 
(all currently marked share flags are reset to unlocked ), so it WON'T have 
the same error (unless, of course, you exactly retrace your steps and cause 
the same conflict to occur again... :-))


>That informative error number above - go check the
> Microsloth site - you gotta be kidding. You get some help from MS on
> services.exe, some KB articles devoted to specific problems and a
> caution to only use with your problem - how come fixes to
> services.exe are not cumulative?

Because a specific fix may apply only to a specific configuration.

It may be a workaround, pending a proper update (which IS cumulative).

>Yes Richard, obviously I'm being
> naive again.

Well, you're certainly being something... :-)

> I tried Safe Mode but without selecting 'Safe Mode with Networking' I
> couldn't e-mail or browse.

What did you expect from Safe Mode? Why do you suppose it is available?

It is INTENDED to be a minimal config. (So most of the things you "take for 
granted" won't work.) The idea is that you add drivers back to it until you 
identify the one causing the problem. Safe mode is a diagnostic mode 
intended for running down and fixing problems.


>Devices like the Lexmark printer aren't
> loaded for Safe Modes and AVG does only a short hand version scan
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If you are having trouble with Services.exe this can be quite serious. You 
don't tell us what the actual error message pertaining to this said. This is 
the service that executes other services, so if it isn't running there will 
be loads of things that don't work correctly.

If I were you, I'd find out what was choking it as a matter of priority. 
Resolve that and you'll probably find that most of your other problems 
disappear.



> Avast3 is one security checker I haven't tried yet (info passed on by
> our MS Excel expert - "List of Invokes"). Avast3 is a Freebie but
> their latest is Avast 8 - Free Trial then pay.

It is excellent. I used it for a while a couple of years back and preferred 
it over AVG. In fact, I believe I have recommended it here in this forum.


>I used a mix of the
> stuff above and eventually Ad-Aware seems to have done the trick -
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Great White North was continuously complaining).

It would be nice if there really were people there taking a personal 
interest in each of our systems. As a general rule it is good to send the 
error messages to them. They have servers dedicated to analysing these 
messages and the most frequent ones get looked at pretty quickly. It looks 
as if a Windows update was pending and wasn't applied. (There are any number 
of reasons why this might happen).

I'd still want to know what it was that killed Services.exe. Have you 
checked the system logs for clues?

>
> Now I'm home and dry, and back to using Windows in normal mode, or so
> I thought. I think somebody here, years ago, referred to it at the
> 'Dreaded Blue Screen'.

That is the famous "Blue Screen of Death" (BSOD). Fortunately rare under XP, 
provided the system has enough real memory. My current machine has only ever 
had one in nearly 4 years. It was caused by a video camera driver 
incompatibility with a previous version of Skype. The VAIO camera built into 
the system was running a SONY driver. Skype wanted a different generic 
driver and the result was a BSOD. I restarted in Safe Mode and tracked it to 
the video driver. Switched to the Skype driver and it was OK, but definition 
was less than with the SONY driver. The latest edition of Skype (the 
so-called "Business version") has an updated driver that has restored full 
resolution and doesn't BSOD. I use Skype video all the time so it is a 
relief to have it working reliably at last.

Most BSODs are caused by driver incompatibility or malware. Resolved by 
updating the offending driver or removing the offending malware.

>"You'se got serious problems buddy and we are
> shutting down to avoid damaging your machine" while at the same time
> doing a memory dump to disk - but they don't tell you why or where the
> dump will be filed.

Sure they do...

"By default, the memory dump file is written to the %SystemRoot%\Memory.dmp 
file. If there is not enough free space on the %SystemRoot% drive, you can 
redirect the dump file to another location that has enough free space. You 
can do this by changing the Dump File path in the Startup and Recovery 
options in Windows 2000 and Microsoft Windows XP, or by changing the Write 
Debugging Information To path on the Startup/Shutdown tab in Windows NT 
4.0."

...or you could just do a search for *.dmp

>
> From memory (Give me a break - how many screen shots of exactly what
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> things appeared to work, prior to your current misfortune.

However, if your system was infected at that time and has since been 
cleaned, you will re-infect it if you restore. When doing a thorough malware 
removal it is best to shut down system restore, then restart it fresh with a 
clean system.

>
> It APPEARS to work now - but I await the next disaster. Having loaded
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to improve - but you never know............

Try using Hibernate instead of rebooting every day. This can save a lot of 
time, but it does take extra disk space. If you can't see "hibernate" on 
your system close options, go to Control Panel > Power Options and click the 
"Hibernate" tab. Tick the box for "Enable hibernation", then reboot.

Run a full virus scan once a week followed by a cold boot. The rest of the 
week you should be able to hibernate. (I have my virus scan scheduled for 
Friday nights. It takes several hours as my notebook has over 1.3 million 
files on it... :-))
>
> If I have to utilize my time I would much rather do gardening in our
> short summers rather than try and become an expert in computer
> 'innards' technology. The latter turns me on as much as the thought
> of servicing my own car !

We are all different and enjoy different things. I enjoy working on my car 
and fixing my computer, but I'm hopeless in the garden and the only reason I 
can enjoy my garden is because I pay someone to look after it...
>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<Tdz4m.16991$Wx7.10819@newsfe04.iad> <1PWdnVpn8_rgS8_XnZ2dnUVZ_rednZ2d@giganews.com> <aZJ4m.11256$aX1.302@nlpi067.nbdc.sbc.com> <xbOdnSqNPeddusnXnZ2dnUVZ_u-dnZ2d@giganews.com> <MMSdndIuRcoAaMnXnZ2dnUVZ_qadnZ2d@earthlink.com> <ob7c55lo9g3nm15t43634ep7cu6apr32b6@4ax.com> <Kdy5m.310704$Xo1.220460@en-nntp-07.dc1.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> Concerning "MSCONFIG" and clean up stuff, I have been using CCLeaner
> recently and found it pretty good in cleaning up "temp" stuff AND it
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The problem with CCleaner is the TEMPTATION to use the "registry cleaner" 
function.

Absolute Law:
No good can come from any use of any registry cleaner but great, 
irreparable, and catastrophic harm can easily result.

No registry cleaner will appreciably (or even detectably) shrink the size of 
the registry. Since the registry is not read sequentially, removing moribund 
entries will not speed things up. At all.

Now Microsoft does offer a free registry cleaner. Tests done by experts 
reveal the Microsoft registry cleaner doesn't actually DO anything.

We think Microsoft is offering a registry cleaner for its placebo effect.

Even if registry cleaners were able to safely perform their main function, 
deleting unused entries, this activity is roughly the equivalent of waxing 
the underside of your automobile's fenders.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> HeyBub wrote:
>> William M. Klein wrote:
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yep. I monitor the microsoft.public.windowsxp.general (and other) 
newsgroups. At least once a week some soul blunders in with the lament that, 
after "cleaning" his registry, his machine won't work. And by "won't work" I 
mean it won't even boot!

You raise an interesting issue when you say you "back up the Registry 
then..." Many say that's what they do.

If, however, the registry cleaner has so scrambled - or deleted - the 
registry, what do you do with the backup?

Oh, you can mount the problematic hard drive in another machine and attempt 
to restore the backup registry. This technique often succeeds. Of course the 
possibility remains that the "cleaner" instigated some "hidden" damage that 
may not be apparent for long periods, if ever. The "backup" will, in this 
case, be useless.
```

```
HeyBub wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> HeyBub wrote:
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have some sympathy for these people because there are really misleading 
ads out there that appear to promise specific solutions (based on what the 
victim was searching for) as Jimmy noted recently.


> You raise an interesting issue when you say you "back up the Registry
> then..." Many say that's what they do.
>
> If, however, the registry cleaner has so scrambled - or deleted - the
> registry, what do you do with the backup?

The cleaner I use doesn't do that. But, if it did, I'd restore the Registry 
and investigate (just like with any backup). I take a full image copy of the 
current working registry, before it is cleaned, the cleaner is configured to 
save a before image of anything it changes, and I usually have a system 
restore point set before cleaning as well. Never had a problem, and only 
used the cleaner's restore once in 4 years. Caused by me OKing something for 
deletion which I should not have. It was restored without problem. I have 
never had to resort to either the System Restore or the physical images.

>
> Oh, you can mount the problematic hard drive in another machine and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> periods, if ever. The "backup" will, in this case, be useless.

That's why you need to be very careful about which cleaner you use. I can 
see every change it made and I can undo any or all of them. It works.

Pete.
```

```
On Jul 11, 11:31 pm, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
> > HeyBub wrote:
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> registry, what do you do with the backup?

You run any Live Linux CD (or DVD) and this can give full access to
the Windows drives so that you can copy the backup file back into
Windows.

Or a specialized recovery Linux CD, such as SystemRescue.
http://www.sysresccd.org. Download and put it to CD now, before you
need it.


> Oh, you can mount the problematic hard drive in another machine and attempt
> to restore the backup registry. This technique often succeeds. Of course the
> possibility remains that the "cleaner" instigated some "hidden" damage that
> may not be apparent for long periods, if ever. The "backup" will, in this
> case, be useless.

Use SystemRescue to copy your files off to, say, a USB Flash Drive so
that reformatting the drive and reinstalling Windows doesn't lose too
much.
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows Start Menu

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-07T23:13:34+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bgouuF23gshrU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<Tdz4m.16991$Wx7.10819@newsfe04.iad>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

>
> But there surely has to be an easier way without going to individual
> applications, to Add or Remove them from the Startup Menu. I either
> misunderstood what MS were saying, or their Help is badly worded. Any
> takers ?

Jimmy, there are a number of ways in which things can start at start-up on 
your system (not just via "startup"on the start menu), and yes, you don't 
have to go to each application to enable or disable it.

MS provide a tool called msconfig which, as you might expect, allows you to 
configure your Windows system.

Click Start>Run ...  then type: msconfig

You will see a tabbed display. Click the "startup" tab to see every 
executable which starts when your system does. Untick boxes to prevent them 
from doing so. Some of them are system tasks and you won't know what they do 
or whether they are essential. Don't untick anything you are not sure about.

For more information I would strongly recommend downloading "HijackThis" and 
running it. It explains each of the areas it logs.

Running both Ad Aware and AVG is not a good idea, and you should default to 
one of them (as you are trying to do...). Both of them are good packages. I 
have a number of anti-virus systems which I can activate on demand, but only 
one which runs at startup.

Of the free ones I have found SuperAntivirus to be best, although none of 
them are actually bad (any of them is better than no protection at all).

HTH,

Pete.
```

<a id="t-ea997f0933"></a>
## Thread: Numeric move of alpha?? — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Numeric move of alpha??

- **From:** "William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-08T01:22:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5xS4m.2831$9l4.1271@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
I just discovered a production code problem which fails when run on a 
Microfocus platform, but is running without error on an IBM platform. Here's 
the edited code:

VAR-1   PIC X(08).
VAR-2   PIC 9(09) COMP.
...
MOVE '123456bb' TO VAR-1  (bb is two spaces)
MOVE VAR-1 to VAR-2

Under microfocus, this gives a runtime error of  something like "Invalid 
character in numeric field."
Under the IBM, it moves 12345600 to VAR-2. As it turns out, this is exactly 
the data we wanted, so I guess no one noticed.

Is there a complier option which would account for this difference? We tried 
this on the IBM with InterTest, and forced 12345678XX into VAR-1, and it 
moved 12345677, In EBCDIC, an X is hex 'E7' so it appears to be just 
stripping the zone.
```

- **References:** `<5xS4m.2831$9l4.1271@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
>I just discovered a production code problem which fails when run on a 
>Microfocus platform, but is running without error on an IBM platform. Here's 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>the data we wanted, so I guess no one noticed.

MOVE '123456bb' TO VAR-1  (bb is two spaces)
INSPECT VAR-1 REPLACING ALL SPACES BY ZEROS
MOVE VAR-1 to VAR-2
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric move of alpha??

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-07T23:03:47-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rJWdnbqmw7IWkcnXnZ2dnUVZ_tWdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<5xS4m.2831$9l4.1271@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <O0T4m.3574$vO4.3412@flpi145.ffdc.sbc.com> <qgT4m.1426$P5.777@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`

```

"William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:qgT4m.1426$P5.777@nwrddc02.gnilink.net...
> "Doug Miller" <spambait@milmac.com> wrote in message 
> news:O0T4m.3574$vO4.3412@flpi145.ffdc.sbc.com...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The PC and mainframe are two different architectures.

On the mainframe to convert DISPLAY format data to COMP data, the DISPLAY 
format data is first PACKed into packed decimal/COMP-3 format.  That PACKing 
changed the X'F1F2F3F4F5F0F0' to X'1234500F'.  All of the zone digits are 
removed except the rightmost zone.  That one is used as the sign and an F 
sign is considered positive.  After all that the PACKed data is converted to 
COMP/BINARY via a CVB (Convert to Binary) instruction.

On the PC there is no packed decimal format.  A different process is used 
and in this case the result is a different format from the mainframe.  There 
may be compiler options that can change this, if so I am sure someone will 
respond with them.  I am not that familiar with PC COBOL as I am not willing 
to spend thousands of dollars for a COBOL compiler. I hope this helps.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric move of alpha??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@Nospam.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-08T12:34:44-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mbi9559ml7ndjpgelrreppqlq5hisn61hb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5xS4m.2831$9l4.1271@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <O0T4m.3574$vO4.3412@flpi145.ffdc.sbc.com> <qgT4m.1426$P5.777@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <rJWdnbqmw7IWkcnXnZ2dnUVZ_tWdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
On Tue, 7 Jul 2009 23:03:47 -0400, "Charles Hottel"
<chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:

>
>"William Bub" <fathafluff@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't believe that is true.  The VAR-1 data in hex after the MOVE
the OP described would contain F1F2F3F4F5F64040.  When the data is
PACKED into a temporary storage area in COBOL, the sign is taken from
the final byte or a "4" in this case.  So the temp storage field would
look like 0123456004.  The system will happily convert that to binary
in VAR-2 and all would be fine unless or until you try and perform
arithmetic on it.


>On the PC there is no packed decimal format.  A different process is used 
>and in this case the result is a different format from the mainframe.  There 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"I married your mother because I wanted children,
imagine my disappointment when you came along." 

-- Groucho Marx
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric move of alpha??

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-07-08T17:31:52-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ua0a559gvtor4e7gaq569tj2rlfg2n0nf5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5xS4m.2831$9l4.1271@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <O0T4m.3574$vO4.3412@flpi145.ffdc.sbc.com> <qgT4m.1426$P5.777@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <rJWdnbqmw7IWkcnXnZ2dnUVZ_tWdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <mbi9559ml7ndjpgelrreppqlq5hisn61hb@4ax.com>`

```
On Wed, 08 Jul 2009 12:34:44 -0400, SkippyPB
<swiegand@Nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

>On Tue, 7 Jul 2009 23:03:47 -0400, "Charles Hottel"
><chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>arithmetic on it.

Not quite. I suspect the move would fail if VAR-2 were S9(9) COMP.  By
defining it as 9(9) the code becomes PACK, OI (or immediate) of an F
sign nibble, CVB (convert to binary).  When VAR-2 is signed the zone
isn't forced and if the sign zone isn't valid a data exception will
occur. 
>
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric move of alpha??

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-08T22:27:37-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6IednQGWJ9wSyMjXnZ2dnUVZ_vudnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<5xS4m.2831$9l4.1271@nwrddc01.gnilink.net> <O0T4m.3574$vO4.3412@flpi145.ffdc.sbc.com> <qgT4m.1426$P5.777@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <rJWdnbqmw7IWkcnXnZ2dnUVZ_tWdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <mbi9559ml7ndjpgelrreppqlq5hisn61hb@4ax.com>`

```

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@Nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message 
news:mbi9559ml7ndjpgelrreppqlq5hisn61hb@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 7 Jul 2009 23:03:47 -0400, "Charles Hottel"
> <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
> arithmetic on it.

<snip>

You are right. I realized I had made that mistake after I was already in 
bed.  Of course '4' is not a valid packed decimal sign and any attempt to 
use it in actual packed decimal arithmetic would result in an abend. 
Generated code may vary with compiler options so use the LIST option and 
look at the generated code.  Sometimes VS COBOL  used to do an OI (OR 
immediate) with mask X'0F' to fix the sign to positive, but I believe the 
current compiler does something different.  Something I always have to look 
up but the book is a work.

Anyway the general process I gave was correct if not all the details.
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric move of alpha??

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-07T22:32:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<frU4m.299634$fo6.160368@en-nntp-09.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<5xS4m.2831$9l4.1271@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
When compiling under Micro Focus, what compiler option do you have set for the
  DIALECT
compiler option?

If you are using IBM Enterprise COBOL on the mainframe, then
  DIALECT(ENTCOBOL)
with Micro Focus should give you mainframe compatible results.

If you are using Enterprise COBOL on the mainframe, what are you using?
If you do not have a current (supported) version of the Micro Focus compiler, 
then you may need to look at the "WB" directive instead of the "dialect" 
directive.

If
  DIALECT(ENTCOBOL)
doesn't give you mainframe compatible results *and* you are running a currently 
supported Micro Focus compiler, then you should contact "support" and this may 
be treated as a "bug".
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric move of alpha??

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-08T20:11:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ctb5m.311524$fo6.298138@en-nntp-09.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<5xS4m.2831$9l4.1271@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`

```
I didn't ask in my first post what compiler option for ZWB/NOZWB is in effect 
for
  - COBOL on the mainframe
 - Micro Focus COBOL

Make certain that this compiler option/directive has the same value for both 
compiles.  This does (for some statements) impact the handling of spaces vs 
zeroes (i.e. high-order nibble)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric move of alpha??

- **From:** slade <jnjsle1@optonline.net>

```
On Jul 8, 9:11 pm, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
wrote:
> I didn't ask in my first post what compiler option for ZWB/NOZWB is in effect
> for
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Show quoted text -

SkippyPB wrote: "When the data is
PACKED into a temporary storage area in COBOL, the sign is taken from
the final byte or a "4" in this case."

As I recall, a MOVE to an unsigned numeric (no "S" preceeding the 1st
9) IBM MVS-like systems results in the low order nybble being forced
to "F" via an OR immediate Assembler instruction.
```

## Thread: Reading file error on AIX 5.2, microfocus cobol 4.0 SP1 — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Reading file error on AIX 5.2, microfocus cobol 4.0 SP1

- **From:** hamilton <hamiltonhall@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-09T07:19:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c98ac76-25d4-4af9-8926-b10b4579d1b7@h31g2000yqd.googlegroups.com>`

```
We have a cobol program to process EDI file , It is using following to
condition end of file .
           READ INPUT-FILE AT END
           MOVE 'Y'                TO  WS-END-OF-FILE-SW
           GO TO 150-EXIT.

For some reason ( run-time ,environment , etc... ) , a large fiel only
be read partially  , but did move 'Y' to the switch . It caused the
file onle be processed partially , but no error logs . The same file
can be re-processed sucessfully . It happens very randomly , 3 times
since last 3 months.

Help please !
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading file error on AIX 5.2, microfocus cobol 4.0 SP1

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-09T10:36:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I7o5m.290706$Lo1.116455@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3c98ac76-25d4-4af9-8926-b10b4579d1b7@h31g2000yqd.googlegroups.com>`

```
How is the file defined (Select/Assign) and what is your compiler option for the 
default for sequential files - RECORD SEQUENTIAL or LINE SEQUENTIAL?

If this is being processed as a "line sequential" file and there is any chance 
that it has "EOF" type bytes in the data, then that could explain this behavior. 
Can you "dump" the actual records just before and after what the program 
processes as the EOF?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reading file error on AIX 5.2, microfocus cobol 4.0 SP1

- **From:** hamilton <hamiltonhall@gmail.com>

```
Complier was configured as "line swquential" and there was no EOF in
the middle of the file( I did dump the file ). I can re-process the
same file on another try. It should not be the file's issue. Any idea
about run-time, environment which could affect this?

Thanks !
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading file error on AIX 5.2, microfocus cobol 4.0 SP1

- **From:** riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-09T11:38:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9bc45c05-6fd0-420f-9e7a-73d712f9e41b@a37g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3c98ac76-25d4-4af9-8926-b10b4579d1b7@h31g2000yqd.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Jul 10, 2:19 am, hamilton <hamiltonh...@gmail.com> wrote:
> We have a cobol program to process EDI file , It is using following to
> condition end of file .
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Help please !

On Unix a file can be opened for reading while it is still being
written. If the file is arriving by, say, ftp and your program is
running from a cron job, or otherwise cycling looking for files that
have newly arrived, then it may be trying to process the file before
it is completely written and thus reaching the 'end' at that time.

If this is the case then try doing a sleep() or somehow mark the file
and do it next cycle.
```

