```
Jimmy, I have to tell you this is almost certainly coincidence. MS certainly 
DO supply "updates in background mode" but never to a specific machine in 
response to mailed error reports. The error reports (which most people DON'T 
send, sadly...) are processed by specifically assigned servers, not by 
humans. Nevertheless, your problem was solved, possibly by a pending update 
which hadn't been applied, possibly by something else.

>
> As to Regcure, it has one of the nicest designed displays and is
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Microsoft.

I would change that...
>
> Further I have had to run Regcure several times, one after the other
> to even get down to only ONE Error in the Registry.

Iterative processing is pretty usual for most registry cleaners.

I have done the
> following a number of times -
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Can you please spell out the inconsistency above ?

They will probably claim that the system is being immediately re-infected 
and that their product is simply a Registry cleaner, not an anti-virus. If 
you get any response at all...

Or, maybe they'll seize the opportunity to sell you an anti-malware 
product... :-)


> -------------------------------------------------------------------------
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> through menu choices etc.

Yes, I've been there :-)
>
> I think Pete's right about the defragging, (CHKDSK and SCAN) -

Neither of those two actually defrag, and I was talking about defragging the 
Registry, not the system in general. In fact I know a number of people who 
defrag their systems far too often... Like Registry cleaning, the benefits 
are in their heads.  It is all about perception; there is a kind of 
satisfaction in thinking you did something very technical and it improved 
your system. On very rare occasions it will actually improve things, but, 
generally, the risks are simply not worth the gains.



> forgotten their correct names. MS recommends using one instead of the
> other, or one before the other. Elsewhere Pete wrote 'Small is 
> beautiful' - me,
> I would write 'Big is UGLY !' - that applies to the corporate world or
> whether you are 'slinging' data around a machine.

I said it in the context of online processing, where it is generally true, 
for the reasons I noted at the time. Sometimes (batch processing, for 
example, where there is no load time or queue wait time constraint) big can 
be good. Things are very seldom black and white, even on computers, where 
the binary paradigm is fundamental.

>I know from the
> Norton images that a clean-up gets rid of my fragmented files and the
> graphic representing your disk shows, after a clean-up that I only
> ever use 25% of available space.

Yes, that's probably true for many of us.

>I haven't now got Norton, but it hasn't changed, I
> still only use 25%. If I archived my COBOL source programs, (sets of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> cured),

I WAS miffed over it because I should have known better... :-) It took me 
around three days to investigate and fix, and I learned much about CSS in 
that time. Iwas thinking about this yesterday as I worked on some more 
material for the cobol21 site.

Things have come a long way in the last 15 years. A few days ago, 
Compuserve, the first online internet service available to the public, 
pulled their web hosting. The first web site I ever built (using Notepad, no 
tools available...) has gone and it made me reflect on the years in between.

In the mid 90s with around 16 million Internet users daily, the Internet was 
not well understood by the public in general. I subscribed to Compuserve 
while living in the U.K. and decided to publish a Web Site. I taught myself 
HTML in an afternoon (it really isn't hard) and was delighted to see my 
first pages appearing on the web. I decided to experiment with animated 
graphics and music and these duly appeared on the site, all built from 
scratch, lovingly by hand. As there was no concept of blogging at the time, 
I posted pages for various trips and activities and received delighted mails 
from friends who had seen the stuff. When my Mother died in 1997 I was able 
to post a dedicated tribute to her which actually helped me at the time. 
Tools started to appear (Dreamweaver...Front Page...) which could generate 
HTML, but I couldn't afford to buy them and, besides, I was quite happy 
cobbling HTML together by hand... :-)  I jumped into web scripting and 
learned Javascript and VB script... when digital cameras first appeared I 
bought one and posted images on the site... it was all very interesting. The 
site gradually became larger (it ended up with around 30 pages) and I kind 
of outgrew it so that from 2000 onwards it received very little maintenance. 
(I think I loved that site because it broke many of the rules regarding web 
design which have since been established... and yet, it worked.)

Compuserve was acquired by AOL and they gave us all a month's notice and 
pulled all the "home page" sites that were part of Compuserve's "Ourworld". 
I'm still getting used to my home page being Google instead of my own home 
page. I could republish it, as I have an image of it on an old machine that 
is retired now, but it wouldn't be the same.

I found out that a lot of stuff I have written has been republished on other 
web sites and they set links to my home page. As a result I had a top Google 
ranking (which people pay a lot of money for) acquired over time and didn't 
cost me a cent. The site was visited from 83 countries and took over 12,000 
hits... it is really quite mind blowing.

These days I use tools for web development. Publishing web pages is now a 
programming exercise and most of my current stuff uses server side 
precompiled C# (these are called "code-behinds"... I love the name :-)), 
with a smattering of client side Javascript. I'm very fond of ADO.NET  and I 
find Visual Studio to be every bit as useful for Web development as it is 
for desktop development.  As I generated revenue from web development for 
others, I re-invested it into tools and these days I don't cobble HTML by 
hand any more. VS 2008 and Dreamweaver both feature large in my current web 
development.

I mourn the passing of the old Compuserve site. It was part of a journey of 
exploration for me which lasted for years and brought me a great deal of 
pleasure (and frustration... :-))

With the skills I have now, I could obviously build a much better site, but, 
like most things, it involves time and committment, and these are scarce 
commodities for me at the moment. I think, in a few years time when I really 
AM retired, I will do something and maybe attach the old original site to it 
as a portal to nostalgia :-)...

> being the man he is Pete wasn't going to let us get away without
> comment. To ensure nobody got cocky/complacent he pointed out that IE,
> FireFox and Opera were all susceptible to attacks.

Yep. You can rely on me to not make no comment...:-)

>
> Perfectly true. Non-technical but a piece in the local paper back in
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> attention.

They are. The reality, however, is not necessarily what the Press might have 
you believe. We all need a Browser. But even if you sat down and wrote a 
super watertight one (assuming that was even possible...) it has to be 
backward compatible. So it has to support things which we know are flawed. 
Not as easy as you might think...


>MS's credibility is on the line.

Do you really think so...? Kind of assumes they HAVE some credibility in 
this area to begin with :-)

I like IE8 and it serves me well. I run Firefox during development to check 
page rendering is compatible. Neither of them are perfect, but they serve 
their purpose. I am pretty much resigned to the fact that if I want to surf 
the Net (and I do...) there are risks involved. I don't expect my Browser to 
keep me safe; it is merely a "first line" of defence. My defences are 
layered like an onion and I don't look for trouble. I KNOW that certain 
"attractive" sites (Bit torrent, P2P, porn, etc.) are almost certainly going 
to carry infections and malware, and I generally avoid them, or put extra 
layers in place if I'm going there... You wouldn't go to the orgy without a 
condom :-). I have also become adept at detecting and removing infections 
that are not known to various AV software... (There is NO substitute for 
port and packet monitoring if you want to know which ET is phoning home from 
your computer... :-))


> Meanwhile these bent minds
> who are hacking are costing the world $ ZILLIONS.

As the global economy hasn't collapsed yet, I guess we can afford it...:-)

Seriously, I'm not so sure it really costs us so much. A thriving industry 
in computer security is MAKING $ZILLIONS from something that we have all 
been told to fear greatly, when the reality is that the threat may not be as 
real or as terrible as we are led to believe. Remember the world was 
supposed to go into computer melt down on New Year's Eve 2000, and Michael 
Angelo's birthday, and a number of other dates significant in the mind of 
various anti-social geeks. I'm still typing this and the chances are very 
good that you will read it...(or, at least, have the opportunity to read 
it... :-))

Malware infecting home computers is something of an "intellectual exercise". 
The world won't stop if you are denied service for a few hours while you 
hunt down and remove the offending beastie. If you are intimidated by the 
thought of malware infection, don't go to places where you are likely to 
encounter it. Irrespective of where you go online, what if you have a hard 
disk crash or motherboard failure? Can you deal with it? If you can, then 
perhaps worrying about malware is not such a big deal.

Sure, malware infecting systems which are critical to our daily lives is a 
much more significant problem, but much more significant measures (and 
amounts of money) are being taken to prevent it.

I think it is important to maintain perspective on the whole subject. As far 
as I know, no-one has yet died from a computer virus....

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (OT) - Registry cleaner

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T13:57:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3qekj0db7@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<7c0ev8F25bnv0U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6P26m.341329$4p1.237589@en-nntp-03.dc1.easynews.com> <xxu6m.5271$3b7.2981@newsfe08.iad> <7bvnlgF24u195U1@mid.individual.net> <4a5aba10$0$9721$5a62ac22@per-qv1-newsreader-01.iinet.net.au> <7c0ev8F25bnv0U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> Paul Richards wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> substitute words and have an alias. I had a 1200 baud dial up also.

Actually, you probably didn't. I don't think there were any standard
async 1200 baud modem protocols, and there weren't any 2400 baud ones
until V.32 in 1989 - and that's almost certainly not what you mean.

What you had were, respectively, 2400 bps (bits per second) and 1200
bps modems. Both of those were 600 baud protocols.

The baud unit is symbols per second, not bits per second. This was a
common error in hobbyist publications of the 1970s and 1980s, and it
stuck around until most people had migrated away from async modems
over conventional telephone lines.

Standard 1200 bps async modems used the Bell 212A, V.22, or V.23
protocols, which were 600 baud with 2 bits/symbol, using PSK
(phase-shift keying, for 212A and V.22) or FSK (frequency-shift
keying, for V.23). With FSK, you send one of four tones (frequencies),
600 times a second: the four tones give you the four possible
combinations of two bits. PSK is similar except you shift the phase of
the carrier rather than the frequency.

Standard 2400 bps async modems used 600 baud with the V.22bis
protocol, which used QAM (quadrature amplitude modulation). QAM uses
two carriers, which are 90 degrees out of phase, and modulates the
amplitude of each; that lets you carry four bits per symbol.

The first standard 2400 baud async modem protocol was V.32, which used
2400 baud and allowed up to 9600 bits/second.

I did some hobby stuff with a 300-baud, 300-bps Bell 103 modem, but
that speed was painful for real work. When you took async framing and
other overhead and delays into account, you'd often end up typing
ahead of the remote system's echo (if you weren't using local echo).

1200 bps was fine for most remote programming work. Full-screen text
editors were reasonably responsive at that speed; even a full-screen
refresh would only take about 8 seconds. (Assuming 80 columns, 25
lines, 7-bit ASCII, 2 framing bits, etc...)
```

```
so easy. Firstly because Windows (both 95/98/ME and NT/..) was
designed before MS noticed the internet, and also because MS software
did and does silly things, like open an email attachment just by
reading the email. It then does whatever is 'appropriate' for the type
of file, for example execute the program.

'HannaMontanasKnickers.jpg' is actually
'HannaMontanasKnickers.jpg.exe', which then gets executed.

Like executing code on a CD or FlashDrive just because it was put into
the computer.

Like files that were downloaded can be executable without any
additional action.

Like starting the network up before the firewall has started leaving
the machine exposed on the internet for a time.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: (OT) - Registry cleaner

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-14T18:55:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3j2d71m7l@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<xxu6m.5271$3b7.2981@newsfe08.iad>`
- **References:** `<6P26m.341329$4p1.237589@en-nntp-03.dc1.easynews.com> <xxu6m.5271$3b7.2981@newsfe08.iad>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Now I did take a look at Regcure from ParetoLogic - the sole reason was
> that in advertising (through googling), they were receiving 'Wows!" from
> ecstatic users; it cured windows32\services.exe problems. Just what I
> wanted.

Unfortunately, this is pretty much meaningless. services.exe is a
grab-bag of code that implements dozens of Windows functions that
happen to run as system services. Thanks to combinatorial explosion,
there are thousands (at least) of potential Windows problems that
might involve services.exe.

So even if they have a few hundred such testimonials (and they're all
genuine, and accurate - which is unlikely, since such users typically
don't know what caused their problem even after it's fixed), there's
little chance that they're relevant to whatever your problem might be.

I'm afraid that saying there's an issue with "services.exe" is like
saying there's an issue with the Windows GUI, or with Windows
security, or with the filesystem. It's still just too broad a
diagnosis to help find a solution, except by pure luck.

(Personally, I think services.exe is a big mistake - the same sort of
mistake Microsoft made when they introduced the Registry. The
granularity of what's visible to the end user is far too coarse. If
you implement each system service in a separate executable, at least
the end user can say, hey, I got a message indicating that
"DComLauncher.exe" died, and someone else can start to narrow down the
problem. Grouping lots of unrelated functionality in a single black
box is rarely a good idea.)
```

```
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Lord, that brings back memories!! 

You might know, and probably Richard in NZ knows - did ICL get Harrods 
going on the Singer POS. I believe my DPM, the ex-ICL salesman had 
something to do with the Harrods account while with ICL.

The Debenham's store in Taunton, Somerset, (Chapman's) had a nice 
restaurant on the top floor overlooking the River Tone. My wife used to 
take our very well behaved two there occasionally for lunch. The 
waitresses loved their politeness.

My wife had never seen Nick Drescher until he came into the store for 
lunch, accompanied by the senior accountants. She recognised him 
immediately by the size of his feet !

At 6ft I take a UK size 10, size 11 over here. Nick Perhaps 6' 2" or 6' 
3", can't truly remember, must have taken a size 12. That would 
translate to a size 13 or 14 over here, if they make boats that big.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

PS: I lie, I lie ! I started out at 6' and 1/4". I was horrified when 
the family doctor measured me, and found I had shrunk. Still, being  a 
mere two years short of being an octogenarian, them's the breaks.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Don't know when, but then Dodi's dad bought it. Rumor has it there is a
sphinx model inside/outside Harrods with his fisog on the sphinx :-)
> 
> On the other hand... I can quote, line-by-line, certain sections of the 
> film 'The Wizard of Oz' (1939), I don't even have to wait to pull it in 
> off the cerebral archive-pack.  Funny thing, this memory-stuff, eh?
> 
On quoting. Eldest granddaughter, tiny mite at the time, hospitalized and
visited by her sister, granddaughter # 2. They were both watching 'The
Witches, (1990 Anjelica Houston and Mai Zetterling, the latter well 
known in UK movies), which they had already seen umpteen times before. 
Time to go home, a real bawling session, "Why can't I stay and watch ? 
Boo-hooo....".

"OK," says granddad, "I promise, I promise, we will stop at Blockbuster 
on the way home and get the movie". It was bucketing down with rain, 
literally; somebody was throwing the buckets as well as the rain. Got 
absolutely soaked getting her the few feet from the car into the store 
entrance. Explained situation to the store manager and he whisked off, 
returning with a smile and the VCR.

We get home and granddad dutifully fed the VCR to the machine. Now this 
young lady, Erin, would make those sports narrators look complete 
amateurs in their coverage of a game. "Oh granddad, This is where X..... 
comes in and says.....", seconds before it happened on the screen. And 
that continued for the whole movie. She has grown up into a pretty blond 
young lady and I still pull her leg about 'The Witches'.

Some quotes for your knapsack from Warren Buffett, Bill G's buddy.

1. The Fannie and Freddie mortgage fiasco :

When others are greedy, be fearful.
When others are fearful, be greedy.

2. Trying to cure current international financial disaster, bearing in 
mind he is one of the experts Obama is free to call upon. (It ain't 
gonna happen in five minute. There are far two many imponderables, and 
the complexity of intertwined economics per country).

A woman can't have a baby in a month.
And it doesn't help by making 9 women pregnant.

The latter is not necessarily word-for-word, but you'll get the meaning.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
It's easy to come up with "fixes" for such fiascos, but I've read some
interesting articles that indicate that bubble psychology will remain
with us.   As long as people have expectations that current growth
rates will continue when they are high, we will have bubbles.   And
bubbles burst.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CODASYL DB

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** HansJ <hans.igel@googlemail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-13T00:53:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4aea1d60-b2f1-4c06-b1ab-f17f28c68eb5@n4g2000vba.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<Zhe6m.53682$Xs4.7569@newsfe11.iad> <h3cauf$gq5$1@news-01.bur.connect.com.au> <2Rp6m.345854$4p1.5438@en-nntp-03.dc1.easynews.com> <77e77$4a5a6b12$d066afd4$10188@FUSE.NET> <SFz6m.5658$3b7.759@newsfe08.iad>`

```
On 13 Jul., 07:29, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> Univac defintitely implemented it under OS/1100, because that's where we
> used it.  This would be about 1980 which I think is pre-IDMS.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Unisys had implemented DMS under the long gone OS/3 and OS 1100 and
now the current DMS 2200 operating system.

The documentation states:

"Data Management System (DMS)
The Unisys database management software product that conforms to the
CODASYL
(network) data model and enables data definition, manipulation, and
maintenance in mass
storage database files."

The "network" means that the set relationships allow to be defined in
a "network" like structure and not hierarchical.

As far as my memory goes, IDMS and DMS have the same roots.

Regards Hans
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CODASYL DB

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** CG <Carl.Gehr.ButNoSPAMStuff5@MCGCG.Com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-13T11:02:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39762$4a5b4c7f$d066afd4$24980@FUSE.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<SFz6m.5658$3b7.759@newsfe08.iad>`
- **References:** `<Zhe6m.53682$Xs4.7569@newsfe11.iad> <h3cauf$gq5$1@news-01.bur.connect.com.au> <2Rp6m.345854$4p1.5438@en-nntp-03.dc1.easynews.com> <77e77$4a5a6b12$d066afd4$10188@FUSE.NET> <SFz6m.5658$3b7.759@newsfe08.iad>`

```
Maybe it was just CA that described IDMS as a network, but they also 
claimed CODASYL conformance.

As I recall the way CA used to describe IDMS, it [network] was supposed 
to be a superset of hierarchical and could, therefore use IDMS views to 
process an IMS DB.  I'm not sure it ever really worked that way.

Carl
```

```
On Sun, 12 Jul 2009 17:40:26 +0800, "Richard Maher"
<maher_rj@hotspamnotmail.com> wrote:

>I'd say "Network" but that's could be just me.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>> but I can't remember what was the descriptive phrase!  "Hierarchical"?

Cullinane used the word "network" to indicate that it's database was
more than just an improved type of "Hierarchical".   (IBM's database
didn't allow for multiple owners).   But it was basically
hierarchical, and most people in IS had a different definition for
"network".
```

## Thread: Why COBOL is losing the POWER struggle — 1 this month / 122 total

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

_Thread started [2009-06](2009-06.md#t-43fdea805c)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why COBOL is losing the POWER struggle

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-14T15:07:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3il970bv2@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<7ao5pmF1vsqmeU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7a4k6jF1tog9jU1@mid.individual.net> <7a5fgmF1tcqi6U1@mid.individual.net> <4ebbf249-1c38-4408-9885-a23dce1d6879@d19g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <7a886lF1s84o2U1@mid.individual.net> <h1o946$2s5$1@reader1.panix.com> <7aaojaF1v66vqU1@mid.individual.net> <h1r8450i0@news7.newsguy.com> <7ad7jgF1ubcp7U1@mid.individual.net> <h23dm60cpt@news2.newsguy.com> <7ao5pmF1vsqmeU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
[Sorry to take so long to respond - I'm on vacation and haven't been
reading Usenet for a while. This was in response to my post about how
a programmer's training and interests shape the mental models that
programmer uses, and condition which details are important and which
can be abstracted away.]

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> 
> Do you think it is possible that programmer "interests" change over time as 
> well?

Definitely. When I first began to write code, in BASIC on the
Commodore PET (an early personal computer), my main concerns was
control flow. I was writing little experimental programs that didn't
do anything significant in terms of data storage or real processing.

One interesting consequence: I thought of variables essentially as
names for pieces of data that once assigned didn't change during a run
of the program. I still remember a key insight I had very early on
when learning to program. A friend and I were in some sort of
"computer camp" (we must have been in junior high at the time),
writing a program to display the simple Fibonacci sequence in BASIC.
We started with something like:

10 LET A=1
20 LET B=1
30 LET C=A+B
40 PRINT A
50 PRINT B
60 PRINT C

As we were trying to figure out how to go on to the next number in the
sequence in a general way that would let us compute N terms, we
realized that *we no longer needed A, and we could assign it the
current value of C*. So rather than have A, B, and C be the first
three terms of the sequence, they could be the two most recently
computed terms and their sum.

I think that was the first time I thought of programs in terms of
functions, rather than in terms of a recipe - that is, as a list of
ingredients and steps for putting those ingredients together.

> There was a time when I loved playing with bytes (even bits, I have a strong 
> affinity for Boolean Algebra and Propositional Calculus, and once wrote an 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> big crossword puzzle and there for my entertainment... :-))

I think that's how lots of programmers learn to stretch their mental
models - by treating it as a game and giving themselves challenges.

> As time went by I become more subject to the pressures of actually producing 
> stuff. When you're a junior programmer, you are pretty much cannon fodder; 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> can for the system under development.

That's often true, though sometimes senior folk are given
responsibility to research some new area, and get to do some of that
playing around. (In the Scrum methodology, that's called a "spike" - a
prototype for a complex new piece of functionality that's isn't
understood well enough yet to break into functional pieces for
scheduling.)

> Contract programmers and Consultants 
> are perceived as expensive resources and expected to deliver, or they won't 
> be around very long. All of this has a subtle effect on a person's approach 
> to programming.

Sure. So can other constraints, such as security and legal compliance,
which can add to the cost of trying new things.

> I like the levels of abstraction which enable me to work at a "higher 
> level", and that is probably why I have taken to OO, where I can deal with 
> objects and behaviours rather than the mind numbing details of how those 
> behaviours are produced (even if I actually wrote the methods myself 
> originally).

Yes, and this tension - between needing to know implementation
details, and working at higher levels of abstraction - exists in lots
of fields. If I'm in industrial logistics, say, I want to know about
relative costs of different shipping methods for various kinds of
cargo and routes. But I don't need to know if one of my subcontractors
uses Volvo trucks or Mitsubishi ones, as long as they can provide a
certain known level of service with good probability.

If I'm a woodcarver, I might want to know what kind of steel my
chisels are made from - or maybe I just know that such-and-such a
brand is a reliable product, and I don't care about carbon content and
that sort of thing.

Agile development methodologies try to help people determine the
appropriate level of abstraction, with principles like "good enough",
rapid releases, and incremental improvement.
```

## Thread: Parameters in Cobol — 3 msgs

### Parameters in Cobol

- **From:** Ed <nymako@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-14T13:36:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<507fd9a3-e947-45de-83ed-562286090b09@n11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>`

```
I am planning on adding a parm to a Cobol program. In a new job, that
uses the program, I will modify the program to say if the parm is set,
do something. The program is used in MANY other jobs. So, I do not
want to modify all of these other jobs. If I do not set the parm in
the JCL, and the program is executed that checks the parm, will a non-
set JCL parm be considered blank in the program?
```

- **Date:** 2009-07-14T21:30:58+00:00

- **References:** `<507fd9a3-e947-45de-83ed-562286090b09@n11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>`

```

Ed  <nymako@gmail.com> wrote:
>I am planning on adding a parm to a Cobol program. In a new job, that
>uses the program, I will modify the program to say if the parm is set,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>set JCL parm be considered blank in the program?

I'm not quite sure I understand what you are asking... could you post some

(if you can't... please do your own homework)
```

#### ↳ Re: Parameters in Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-14T18:03:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%887m.332503$Xo1.199423@en-nntp-07.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<507fd9a3-e947-45de-83ed-562286090b09@n11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Assumptions (mine)
 - you are working with IBM z/OS
 - Possibly using Enterprise COBOL (but also possibly an earlier compiler)
 - You have a COBOL "main" program that is activated (in batch) by JCL
 - currently the program does NOT expect *ANY* parameters to be passed in the 
JCL "PARM="

If this is true (and you now want to add a "parm" option in JCL)

Some answers:

1) NO, these will NOT show up as "spaces" to programs where the PARM is not 
passed
2) When you use "PARM=" in JCL to pass to a COBOL main, you can ONLY pass one 
parameter.  If you use commas in the PARM= string, it will still come in as one 
parameter to the COBOL main program (the "length" word at the beginning tells 
you the length of the entire PARM string

If you are actually talking about a COBOL *sub* program (one called by another 
COBOL or Assembler or whatever main program) then the way the COBOL subprogram 
handles "passed parameters" is VERY different from what happens with JCL PARM= 
strings.

 * * * *

Clarify what you are actually currently doing and what you want to change and we 
may be able to help more.
```

## Thread: 23 quadrillion dollar mistake — 15 msgs

### 23 quadrillion dollar mistake

- **From:** e p chandler <epc8@juno.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-15T18:36:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cdb023d3-9f95-456e-a008-8dd0e18563a6@a26g2000yqn.googlegroups.com>`

```
Erroneous bills were sent to Visa customers as reported here

http://www.cnn.com/2009/US/07/15/quadrillion.dollar.glitch/index.html.

The faulty amount was  $23,148,855,308,184,500.

When multiplied by 100 and converted to hex, it becomes 20 20 20 20 20
20 12 50. [almost reported that way on Slashdot.]

Of course hex 20 is an ASCII space not an EBCDIC space (40) but it is
plausible to see how this could have happened in COBOL, or in some
other language through a buffer over-run or an array access out of
bounds.

Ouch.

Customers were also charged over-limit fees. Eventually these charges
were rescinded by the bank.

-- e
```

#### ↳ Re: 23 quadrillion dollar mistake

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T01:42:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_zv7m.8169$cl4.7902@flpi150.ffdc.sbc.com>`
- **References:** `<cdb023d3-9f95-456e-a008-8dd0e18563a6@a26g2000yqn.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <cdb023d3-9f95-456e-a008-8dd0e18563a6@a26g2000yqn.googlegroups.com>, e p chandler <epc8@juno.com> wrote:
>Erroneous bills were sent to Visa customers as reported here
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>20 12 50. [almost reported that way on Slashdot.]

Very likely, it was 20 20 20 20 20 20 20 20 (eight spaces), the actual number 
was $23,[...],535.36, and the news services simply rounded it to 
$23,[...],500.
```

#### ↳ Re: 23 quadrillion dollar mistake

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T14:15:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3ncmo$dld$4@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cdb023d3-9f95-456e-a008-8dd0e18563a6@a26g2000yqn.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <cdb023d3-9f95-456e-a008-8dd0e18563a6@a26g2000yqn.googlegroups.com>,
e p chandler  <epc8@juno.com> wrote:
>Erroneous bills were sent to Visa customers as reported here
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>bounds.

Much simpler explanation: MOVE SPACES TO RECORD-AREA (or 
RECORD-AREA-PORTION) where said area contains a field defined as COMP 
(binary).

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 23 quadrillion dollar mistake

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T17:12:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3nn2m$2c8$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cdb023d3-9f95-456e-a008-8dd0e18563a6@a26g2000yqn.googlegroups.com> <h3ncmo$dld$4@reader1.panix.com> <0JI7m.3577$eS5.1954@newsfe25.iad>`

```
In article <0JI7m.3577$eS5.1954@newsfe25.iad>, tlmfru <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>But surely this wasn't a new program?

There's no such thing as a 'new program'; all programs are copies of other 
programs and cut-n-pasted into a slightly different configuration... and 
don't call me Shirley!

(reference to film 'Airplane!', US 1980, see 
http://www.imdb.com/title/tt0080339/quotes and search for "shirley" (no " 
needed)

>Upgraded, perhaps, but it boggles the
>mind to think that such a logical error would persist beyond the first ten
>minutes of testing!  It looks like invalid data got there somehow - perhaps
>from some other program.

It walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, Mr Lacey... and Ockham's 
Razor got applied.  I've seen errors such as the one I described before 
and this one seems to fit it to a 't', spaces moved to an area which 
contains a COMP field.  To add to the force of the argument ('Why MOVE 
SPACES when they can INITIALIZE?') this is credit-card processing and 
speed is paramount (Mr Trembley should be able to support that assertion; 
these folks deal with a billion transactions a day)... and I'll bet 
dollars to Navy beans the program in question (or the original that was 
used as a template for this particular copy) was probably running long 
before 1985.

As for testing... who needs testing?  Our programmers are top-notch, they 
get it right the first time, testing costs money and we need it in Prod 
now Now NOW!  I recall a few years back getting bills from American 
Express where obviously a record-format had been changed from MMDDYYYY to 
DDMMYYYY and my April statement was showing transactions from The Future:

Transaction was actually made on 9 Mar 2003 (03092003)
Statement showed transaction as 3 Sep 2003 (09032003)

Don't ask me how it dealt with days greater than the 12th... and I don't 
know how it made it into Prod, either.

Oh, wait, I know... a consultant/contractor/hired gun did it!  When in 
doubt, blame the consultant, especially the one who left just recently... 
how could I have forgotten?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 23 quadrillion dollar mistake

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T20:56:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
(Previous info snipped)

By pure chance, I happen to be re-listening to Heinlein's "The moon is a harsh 
mistress" - just when this thread began.

The first chapter deal's with Mike's (HOLMES IV computer's) "sense of humor" and 
the fact that he issued a paycheck to an employee of the authority for the 
correct value PLUS 10 to some large power.

Anyone know if the Visa computers have a (new) sense of humor AND are 
self-aware? <G>

I can't remember is Arnold from MasterCard or from Visa?  I wonder what he can 
(and can't) say about all of this?
```

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

I saw the headline earlier but I had not read the entire story:
http://www.nbcwashington.com/news/weird/Wow-Quadrillion-Dollar-Credit-Card-Bill.html

Your suggestion that a MOVE of SPACES to a numeric field might have 
caused this sounds pretty reasonable to me, but it could be more 
complicated that that.  It might be embarrassing for Visa, but I would 
expect them to fix it very courteously so there are no penalties for the 
cardholders.  I would not expect them to explain publicly exactly how it 
happened.

Fortunately, this is not my problem.  I don't work for Visa and I don't 
work with Billing.  I haven't had to appear in any corner offices to 
explain it.
```

```
[snip]

>Your suggestion that a MOVE of SPACES to a numeric field might have 
>caused this sounds pretty reasonable to me, but it could be more

Readily acknowledged that anything can be anything else... including this 
statement, of course, but when it comes to troubleshooting one starts with 
the simple and move upwards from there.

[snip]

>I would not expect them to explain publicly exactly how it 
>happened.

Oh, Everyone Knows how it happened... it was a Computer Error.

(that's how it may wind up getting reported to the public... back on the 
cubicle-ranch it will be the Programmer's Fault, as usual)

>Fortunately, this is not my problem.  I don't work for Visa and I don't 
>work with Billing.  I haven't had to appear in any corner offices to 
>explain it.

I have seen otherwise, Mr Trembley, at other times and in other places: a 
competitor's error makes the News and the Corner Office Idiot calls whom 
he thinks might know something about such matters into a meeting to ask 
'How do we know this will Not Happen Here?'... and is usually not 
satisfied with 'You've just asked us to prove a logical negative and 
that's a bit dicey.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 23 quadrillion dollar mistake

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@att.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-18T02:21:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FfudnZY2XsBx6vzXnZ2dnUVZ_rKdnZ2d@giganews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<h3puqm$pi7$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cdb023d3-9f95-456e-a008-8dd0e18563a6@a26g2000yqn.googlegroups.com> <ETQ7m.390329$Tp1.344622@en-nntp-01.dc1.easynews.com> <h3ooaq$p8a$2@reader1.panix.com> <4A6024D4.9050604@att.net> <h3puqm$pi7$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <4A6024D4.9050604@att.net>,
> Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@att.net> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I have seen incidents like your example before, which is why I 
considered myself so fortunate not to experience it this time.

We do just as good a job as anybody at making our own mistakes, and an 
excellent job of cleaning up our own messes.  Almost all of the urgent 
production problems I actually work with are in a domain that is 
completely invisible to the general public.  If a problem is fixed 
before you get your bill or use your card, you will never know about it. 
  That's what makes the 23 quadrillion dollar error so newsworthy.

Now, if you actually need to prove a logical negative to your manager, 
you could try the inductive fallacy:  "It's never happened before 
because we would never make such a mistake.  We are much smarter and 
more careful than those other guys."

What they don't want to hear is: "It is impossible to prevent a 
completely unpredictable event."  And if that doesn't shake them up you 
can say, "Sometimes it's not even possible to prevent a completely 
predictable event."

Kind regards,
```

```
your shirt-cuffs and have done with it?

('I forgot... what's my name?  Oh, these initials might remind me!')

>completely unpredictable event."  And if that doesn't shake them up you

>predictable event."

Truth is a Powerful Weapon and must be wielded carefully, aye... and what 
it might be *too* tempting to follow the above up with '... and that is a 
Very Good Thing, too, else someone in a Bigger Corner Office might realise 
that there's no need for *you* here.'

(what was the Dilbertism... a Corner Office Idiot sends out a global email 
to the effect of 'All system or production emergencies must be scheduled 
and cleared through my office first'... or something like that)

DD
```

```
<arnold.trembley@att.net> wrote:

>Now, if you actually need to prove a logical negative to your manager, 
>you could try the inductive fallacy:  "It's never happened before 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>predictable event."

True enough.    But stuff happens.    

Even though most crimes don't get solved, even the best criminal is
subject to "stuff happens".    And when someone runs a red light and
plows into his getaway car from the perfect crime - he goes to jail.

The best we can do is follow the philosophy of sidewalk makers.   Try
to pre-design cracks where we want them, so that the points of
failures can be handled.
```

```
On Fri, 17 Jul 2009 02:14:28 -0500, Arnold Trembley
<arnold.trembley@att.net> wrote:

>Your suggestion that a MOVE of SPACES to a numeric field might have 
>caused this sounds pretty reasonable to me, but it could be more 
>complicated that that. 

Sometimes this can happen with a program that runs a long time - a
change in the process has somewhere down the line passing spaces
instead of zeros.   And "down the line" doesn't even have to be in the
same company.
```

<a id="t-3781d084fb"></a>
## Thread: Old versus New — 16 msgs

### Old versus New

- **From:** foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T07:12:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<788cd870-ef08-47ab-86d3-b39433450e21@o7g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hellough everyone:

As some know, I happily use my ancient 16-bit compiler and can say
that I
haven't learned anything new in 20 years (other than html and web
stuff).
Still, my 16-bit compiler and ancient version of SP2 still provide me
with
the tools to do the things I want to do.

I recently discovered on-line Scrabble and found myself writing a
Scrabble 'dictionary' to help my play.

The official Scrabble 'TWL98' dictionary contains 182k words and is
used
to validate play.

So I wrote a program to help me play according to the TWL98
dictionary.

The dictionary source file is TWL98.TXT containing 182,853 words
(Official
TWL98 dictionary).

The program creates the ISAM file:

        SCRABIND.DAT is  7,862,664 bytes
        SCRABIND.IDX is 18,742,272 bytes

The pertinent file description is:

       01  SCRABX-REC.
           02 SCRABX-KEY PIC X(20).
           02 FILLER REDEFINES SCRABX-KEY.
              03  SCRAB-1ST PIC X(01).
              03  SCRAB-C   OCCURS 19 TIMES PIC X(01).
           02 SCRAB-SIZE.         (alternate key)
              03  SCRAB-A  PIC 9(02).
              03  SCRAB-B  PIC X(20).

        (SCRABX-KEY and SCRAB-B are the words from the TWL98 file.)

I ran the program to create the ISAM file four different ways with
the results as follows:

Desktop Pentium D 3.7ghz 4gb RAM - 8gb memory stick. XP -  150
seconds.

Desktop Pentium D 3.7ghz 4gb RAM - 70gb hard drive. XP -  141 seconds.

Netbook Intel Atom 1.6ghz 1gb RAM 160gb hard drive. XP - 665(!)
seconds.

Laptop Celeron 1.6ghz 2gb RAM 70gb hard drive. Vista - 166 seconds.

I am curious as to how the speed of a my ancient 16-bit compiler and
ISAM
would compare to something written using modern stuff (even other
than
COBOL) such as Java, .NET, SQL or whatever.

If interested persons would write a simple program using their chosen
software to produce the equivalent of the ISAM file using TWL98.TXT
and post the resulting times, it would be good.
It would be even better if one chose to write the functional
equivalent of my COBOL program.

You can download the TWL98 text file and my COBOL program from
foodman123.com/scrab.htm.

Has the hardware become so fast that efficient software (in terms of
size and speed)
is not necessary?

I wonder, how much faster would the modern software be?

How big would the downloadable executable be in the more modern
language/access method?

How big would the equivalent of the ISAM file be?

thank you

tony dilworth
```

#### ↳ Re: Old versus New

- **From:** riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T14:43:47-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7b462f0e-58c4-470d-b07a-908354221776@b25g2000prb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Fujitsu COBOL for Linux on 7 year old AMD 2000 (1.6MHz) 750Mb RAM

Fri Jul 17 09:35:50 NZST 2009
TWL Starting
+00182929
+00178723
Fri Jul 17 09:35:57 NZST 2009

7 seconds, and that included reading back the file and counting the
records.  There were 4000 duplicates including spaces.

File size 8,880,128 and this includes the index. Fujitsu files use
compressed data.


> How big would the downloadable executable be in the more modern
> language/access method?
>
> How big would the equivalent of the ISAM file be?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Old versus New

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-18T13:00:00+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ccl4hF2706gaU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<788cd870-ef08-47ab-86d3-b39433450e21@o7g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <7b462f0e-58c4-470d-b07a-908354221776@b25g2000prb.googlegroups.com>`

```
riplin wrote:
> On Jul 17, 2:12 am, foodman <foodman...@aol.com> wrote:
>
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
>> How big would the equivalent of the ISAM file be?

Good job, Richard.

I was tempted to do this, but by the time I saw it, you already had an 
excellent solution.

I was interested to see use of SQLite. Although I have it, I haven't used it 
and have often wondered whether it is any good or not. Looks like it is.. 
:-)

The other question I had to consider was WHY anyone would write a computer 
program to give them an edge in what is supposed to be a "game"? (I guess 
this would be OK if you made the program available to ALL the players, but 
then you might just as well sit back and let the computers play...)

I can understand someone using a "crossword finisher" when they have given 
it their best shot and just want to know the answer, but that only involves 
ONE person and is a personal choice. I guess winning is more important than 
playing for some people.

Based on past experience, I can't see this result causing a revolution on 
Planet Dilworth, but of course, you came up with the "wrong" answer. The 
question was designed to show that old 16 bit technology is way better than 
modern stuff, that any change or innovation in the IT field is pointless, 
and we are all wasting our time using it...

I wonder if Tony ever gets sick of being wrong all the time... :-)

Pete.
```

```
> a 386.

The point that I was making is that the 3.7GHz 4MB machine with your
software is hardly faster than a 386 would be running modern software.

> My business community is exclusively Windows, so what else would I
> use?

Cobol is a language designed to be portable. My Cobol systems (and
others) can run on various operating systems with no changes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Old versus New

- **From:** riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<788cd870-ef08-47ab-86d3-b39433450e21@o7g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <7b462f0e-58c4-470d-b07a-908354221776@b25g2000prb.googlegroups.com> <7ccl4hF2706gaU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Jul 18, 1:00 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> riplin wrote:

> I was interested to see use of SQLite. Although I have it, I haven't used it
> and have often wondered whether it is any good or not. Looks like it is..
> :-)

It is good. I developed a small system for some on the road sales reps
in Python, Glade* and SQLite and it runs on Nokia N8x0 pocket
computers, ASUS EEE Linux Netbooks and Windows Laptops with no code
changes.


*Glade is a GUI designer that writes the GTK+ GUI design out as an XML
file and then the program just references the file and screen to get
it drawn. I understand that .NET 3.x is just catching up to do that.
```

```
on

question was designed to show that old 16 bit technology is way better
than
modern stuff, that any change or innovation in the IT field is
pointless,
and we are all wasting our time using it...

>>You really ought to take a reading comprehension course, Dashwood.

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> admitting it.

Ah, that explains why you need a computer program and a dictionary... :-)
>
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> I want to do.".  Please read that literally, if you can.

OK, I get it. (I got it the first time...) I understand... you don't want to 
DO much...

Now, if you were being honest, just suppose that Richard's test had shown it 
ran 25 times SLOWER than your results (instead of around 25 times FASTER.)

You wouldn't have crowed about how the old stuff is the best, how clever you 
are for changing nothing in this Millennium, and what an all round brilliant 
IT guy you are for finding the place and staying there?

You'll pardon my scepticism.

>Nowhere in
>>> the
>>> post does it claim that old stuff is "Better" than new stuff.

I never said it did. (Maybe it isn't I who needs help with reading...) I 
stated what _I_ believe the question was designed to show. It is more subtle 
than what you actually said. I don't believe your question was prompted by 
honest curiosity; I believe you were hoping that the leaner 16 bit offering 
would outperform today's technology significantly. (I believe the term for 
this is "hidden agenda")

>>   I
>>> emphasize "the things I want to do".  My stuff has served me well
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> TO?

Gee, I dunno... Because you could do the things YOU WANT TO DO (emphasis 
original) quicker and easier, maybe?

>>> That has alway been my point.
>
>>> Remember, as I may have told you in the past, that sarcasm
>>> is the lowest form of wit.

No, get away... well I never... YOU said that?  (Note use of sarcasm...)

Now see if you can find some elsewhere in my post. You won't.  There isn't 
any.

I am tempted to observe that even a low form of wit would be out of place on 
Planet Dilworth, but such a comment would go over your head, so I won't.



>>>You owe me an apology for your stupid,
>>> sarcastic
>>> and inaccurate comments.

Tony, I don't like you and you don't like me. I don't like you because I 
found you in the past to be dishonourable, and you called me a liar. (That 
would be fine had I been lying, but I wasn't, and I even sent you private 
mail showing that I wasn't, so as not to humiliate or embarrass you. You 
never withdrew your statement or apologised, publicly or privately.)

Unlike you, I never demand apologies from people.  Can't see the point. If 
people are sorry, they'll tell you; if they're not... well who would want a 
phoney apology? I reckon you behaved badly and have continued to do so. On 
every encounter you have come off worst... you were scathing about a coding 
style and suggested we let the people in the forum decide. You even promised 
to stand naked in a shop window in New York if my code was preferred to 
yours. It was a landslide, against you. Did you keep your word? Did you even 
concede gracefully? No. Dishonourable.

A lot of time has gone by and I should really let it go, but what the Hell, 
you, to me, are a red rag to a bull.

So I would admit my comments are hostile, but that, apparently, isn't your 
beef...

Stupid? Not for me to say.

Sarcastic?  Definitely not.

Inaccurate?  I'm not infallible, but I call 'em like I see 'em... I see you 
as a cowardly, nasty little man. I'll withdraw that and apologise when you 
can stand within reach and THEN call me a liar...

So just exactly what am I supposed to apologise for? (And, by the way, I 
NEVER apologise unless I am genuinely sorry.)

If my post upset you to the point where you need to shout and yell, I'm 
glad... Time well spent. You deserve aggravation from me and I'm not such a 
Saint that I won't indulge in some mischief occasionally.

>
>>> My post stated "I am curious as to how the speed of a my ancient
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> It did not remotely suggest that old is better than new.

I believe you hoped it would be so.

>>> More than 20 years have elapsed since my compiler was introduced and
>>> only a dolt would think that there would not have been dramatic
>>> improvements in that interval, which is the case. Not being a dolt,
>>> I know things are 'better' and faster but I have no need for them.
>

So why embark on a pointless benchmark?


>
>>> Finally, I looked upon my post as a legitimate opportunity for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> no curiosity in such comparisons?

No, not really. Nevertheless, I was going to do your little exercise because 
I know what your hidden agenda is and I was pretty confident you'd crash. 
Richard did a better job of it than I would have, but my results would have 
been similar.


>>> I would like to see more results
>>> from other
>>> people and would very much like to know how fast it would be on a
>>> modern mainframe.

So you can not buy one, because you don't need to?

>>> After all "now that you can do anything" you should be interested
>>> in showing us
>>> how good the stuff you use is.

That would only be true if your opinion mattered to me.

It doesn't.

>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think that's called "delusional"...

>

>

I just re-read the above and decided it is unworthy of me. I was going to

Congratulations, you really bring out the worst in me...

Pete.
```

## Thread: DD concatenation — 14 msgs

### DD concatenation

- **From:** Jessica Colman <jessica.colman@augustakom.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T19:44:43+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3nouc$fca$1@svr7.m-online.net>`

```
Hi,

is there a way to check, if a DD concatenation contains a certain dataset?

In my program (COBOL, PL/I or even Assembler) I want to know, if the 
STEPLIB contains a certain dataset.

Thanks

Jessica
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DD concatenation

- **From:** Jessica Colman <jessica.colman@augustakom.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T22:10:38+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<h3nouc$fca$1@svr7.m-online.net> <h3npbm$gcs$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
Hi,

I think I did not describe my problem clearly enough, so I try to be as 
precise as possible:

I have this kind of JCL on z/OS:

//X6APGM JOB 'Account',etc
//*
//MYPGM EXEC PGM=MYPGM
//STEPLIB DD DISP=SHR,DSN=HLQ.PODS1
//        DD DISP=SHR,DSN=HLQ.PODS2
//        DD DISP=SHR,DSN=HLQ.PODS3
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
//etc

I want to know in MYPGM, which is written in PL/I or COBOL, the DSN of 
the first, the second and the third dataset in the STEPLIB 
concatenation. I do not think, this is possible with PL/I or COBOL. Does 
anyone have a sample for this in assembler?

Thanks

Jessica



docdwarf@panix.com schrieb:
> In article <h3nouc$fca$1@svr7.m-online.net>,
> Jessica Colman  <jessica.colman@augustakom.net> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DD concatenation

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T12:47:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3prtp$6hg$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<h3nouc$fca$1@svr7.m-online.net> <h3o1fv$uo5$1@svr7.m-online.net> <h3oaoa$q3$2@reader1.panix.com> <h3p13b$lnj$1@svr7.m-online.net>`

```
In article <h3p13b$lnj$1@svr7.m-online.net>,
Jessica Colman  <jessica.colman@augustakom.net> wrote:
>Hm, I just cannot imagine what the text-editor has to do with this, but 
>okay: I'm using IBM's Rational Developer for z and TSO/ISPF.

Well, it helps to know little things like the platform you're working on 
and the tools you have available to work with... and since you neglected 
to mention such things knowing what text-editor you're working with might 
help someone deduce this information.

I asked my question in response to your question of 'is there a way to 
check, if a DD concatenation contains a certain dataset?'; using the 
standard ISPF editor one issues the line command of 'f <ddname> 3' while 
editing (or browsing) the JCL would bring you right where you need to be.

Your question seems to have changed, though, to 'I want to know in MYPGM, 
which is written in PL/I or COBOL, the DSN of the first, the second and 
the third dataset in the STEPLIB'... and that's a different matter, 
entirely.

Since English may not be your native language (als Sie auf meine Aufgabe 
reagierten Ihr 'newsreader' den Text 'docdwarf@panix.com schrieb:' 
addierte... peut-etre vous parlez Francais?) I am not certain what you 
intend when you say 'in MYPGM'; do you want to know this from the JCL 
which runs MYPGM (which can be checked by editor or browser, as mentioned 
above) or during actual run-time?

DD

(depsite my usual habit of deleting such the bottom-posting below is 
included to provide context and an example... nothing added to it)

>docdwarf@panix.com schrieb:
>> In article <h3o1fv$uo5$1@svr7.m-online.net>,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

#### ↳ Re: DD concatenation

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T13:38:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<upvu55hqt28gthkok01eulogroj86cl94g@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<h3nouc$fca$1@svr7.m-online.net>`

```
On Thu, 16 Jul 2009 19:44:43 +0200, Jessica Colman
<jessica.colman@augustakom.net> wrote:

>Hi,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>STEPLIB contains a certain dataset.

From within a program?   Yes, but it not standard programming.   I use
the following to determine which CV an IDMS job is running in
(checking the JCL):

000010  CBL XREF,MAP
000100 CBL NODYNAM,NOOPT,NOSSRANGE
000110*NODMLIST
000200 TITLE 'RETRIEVE JOB/STEP NAME PLUS DATASET INFO.'
000300 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000400
000500 PROGRAM-ID.  DDNAME.
000600*---------------------------------------------------------------*
000700* *
000800*  USING POINTERS, RETRIEVE OPERATING-SYSTEM INFORMATION AND *
000900*  RETURN IT TO THE CALLER. *
001000* *
001010*  IT ONLY LOOKS AT THE DDNAME ASSOCIATED WITH THIS JOB STEP *
001020* *
001030*  IT DOES NOT YET HANDLE CONCATENATED DATA SETS. *
001040* *
001100*---------------------------------------------------------------*
001200     EJECT
001300 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
001400 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001500
001600 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
001700 FILE-CONTROL.
001800
001900 DATA DIVISION.
002000 FILE SECTION.
002100
002200*---------------------------------------------------------------*
002300* BEGINNING OF WORKING STORAGE
002400*---------------------------------------------------------------*
002500 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
002600
002700*---------------------------------------------------------------*
002800* DDN-SUBSCRIPTS-POINTERS
002900*---------------------------------------------------------------*
003000
003100 01  FILLER PIC X(016) VALUE 'DDN-SUBS-PNTRS**'.
003200
003300 01  DDN-SUBSCRIPTS-POINTERS.
003400     10  DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER-X.
003500         15  DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER       POINTER.
003600
003700     10  DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER           POINTER.
003800     10  DDN-TIOT-BINARY  REDEFINES  DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
003900                                        PIC S9(09)  COMP.
004000
004100     10  DDN-JFCB-POINTER               POINTER.
004200     10  DDN-JFCB-X       REDEFINES  DDN-JFCB-POINTER.
004300         15  FILLER                     PIC X(01).
004400         15  DDN-JFCB-LOWER-3           PIC X(03).
004500     EJECT
004600
004700*---------------------------------------------------------------*
004800* WORK-MISCELLANEOUS
004900*---------------------------------------------------------------*
005000
005100 01  FILLER PIC X(016) VALUE '**WORK-MISC*****'.
005200
005300 01  WORK-MISCELLANEOUS.
005400     10  WORK-BINARY-2               PIC S9(04) COMP VALUE ZERO.
005500     10  FILLER  REDEFINES WORK-BINARY-2.
005600         15  WORK-BINARY-2L          PIC X(01).
005700         15  WORK-BINARY-2R          PIC X(01).
005710
005800 01  SWITCHES.
005810     05  SW-MAIN-SWITCH              PIC X(01)  VALUE 'N'.
005820     88  SW-MAIN                                VALUE 'Y'.
005900     EJECT
006000*---------------------------------------------------------------*
006100*  LINKAGE AREA
006200*---------------------------------------------------------------*
006300 LINKAGE SECTION.
006400
006500 01  LINK-COMM-AREA.
006600     10  LINK-JOB-NAME           PIC X(08).
006700     10  LINK-STEP-NAME          PIC X(08).
006800     10  LINK-DD-NAME            PIC X(08).
006900     10  LINK-DS-NAME            PIC X(44).
007000     10  LINK-GDG-OR-MEMBER      PIC X(08).
007100     10  LINK-CATALOG-IND        PIC X(01).
007200         88  LINK-FILE-IS-CATALOGED  VALUE 'Y'.
007300     10  LINK-FILE-TYPE          PIC X(01).
007400         88  LINK-FILE-IS-GDG        VALUE 'G'.
007500         88  LINK-FILE-IS-JES        VALUE 'J'.
007600         88  LINK-FILE-IS-PDS        VALUE 'P'.
007700         88  LINK-FILE-IS-REGULAR    VALUE 'R'.
007800     10  LINK-VOLUME-COUNT       PIC 9(01).
007900     10  LINK-VOLUMES            PIC X(30).
007910 01  PASSED-RECORD REDEFINES LINK-COMM-AREA.
007920     05  PASSED-LENGTH     PIC 9(4)  COMP.
007930     05  PASSED-UPDATE-PARM.
007940         10  PASSED-LABEL  PIC X(14).
007950***      10  FILLER        PIC X(14) VALUE 'PASSED DDNAME='
007960         10  PASSED-DDNAME PIC X(08) VALUE 'NAME=='.
007970     05  FILLER            PIC X(99).
008000
008100 01  L02-TCB-POINTER                 POINTER.
008200
008300 01  L03-TCB.
008400     10  FILLER                      PIC X(012).
008500     10  L03-TIOT-POINTER            POINTER.
008600
008700 01  L04-TIOT.
008800     10  L04-JOB-NAME                PIC X(08).
008900     10  L04-STEP-NAME               PIC X(08).
009000
009100 01  L04-TIOT-START                  PIC X(24).
009200
009300 01  L04-TIOT-SEGMENT.
009400     10  L04-TIOT-LENGTH             PIC X(01).
009500     10  FILLER                      PIC X(03).
009600     10  L04-DD-NAME                 PIC X(08).
009700     10  L04-JFCB-ADDRESS            PIC X(03).
009800
009900 01  L05-JFCB.
010000     10  FILLER                      PIC X(16).
010100     10  L05-DS-NAME                 PIC X(44).
010200     10  L05-GDG-OR-MEMBER           PIC X(08).
010300     10  L05-DMI-BYTE                PIC X(01).
010400     10  FILLER                      PIC X(33).
010500     10  L05-DSTYPE-BYTE             PIC X(01).
010600     10  FILLER                      PIC X(30).
010700     10  L05-VOLUME-COUNT-BYTE       PIC X(01).
010800     10  L05-VOLUME-LIST             PIC X(30).
010900     EJECT
011000 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LINK-COMM-AREA.
011100
011120     IF  PASSED-LABEL  = 'PASSED DDNAME='
011121         MOVE SPACES                 TO PASSED-LABEL
011130         SET SW-MAIN                 TO TRUE
011140     END-IF
011200     MOVE X'0000021C'                TO DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER-X
011300     SET ADDRESS OF L02-TCB-POINTER  TO DDN-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
011400     SET ADDRESS OF L03-TCB          TO L02-TCB-POINTER.
011500     SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT         TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.
011600
011700     MOVE L04-JOB-NAME               TO LINK-JOB-NAME.
011800     MOVE L04-STEP-NAME              TO LINK-STEP-NAME.
011900     INITIALIZE LINK-DS-NAME  LINK-GDG-OR-MEMBER
LINK-CATALOG-IND
012000                LINK-FILE-TYPE LINK-VOLUME-COUNT LINK-VOLUMES.
012100
012200****  IF THEY DID NOT SUPPLY A DD NAME, EXIT STAGE LEFT.
012300
012400     IF LINK-DD-NAME < 'A'
012500        GOBACK
012600     END-IF.
012700
012800     MOVE LOW-VALUES TO DDN-JFCB-X.
012900     SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-START   TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.
013000     SET DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER        TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.
013100     ADD 24                          TO DDN-TIOT-BINARY.
013200     SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-SEGMENT TO DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER.
013300
013400****  LOOP THROUGH ALL POINTERS TO THE DD NAMES.
013500****  WHEN WE GET A MATCH, MOVE IN ALL THE FILE DATA AND EXIT.
013600
013610****  PERFORM UNTIL DD-NAME IS BLANK
013700     PERFORM UNTIL L04-TIOT-LENGTH = LOW-VALUES
013800        MOVE L04-JFCB-ADDRESS           TO DDN-JFCB-LOWER-3
013900        SET ADDRESS OF L05-JFCB         TO DDN-JFCB-POINTER
014000        IF L04-DD-NAME = LINK-DD-NAME
014100           MOVE L04-DD-NAME             TO LINK-DD-NAME
014200           MOVE L05-DS-NAME             TO LINK-DS-NAME
014300           MOVE L05-GDG-OR-MEMBER       TO LINK-GDG-OR-MEMBER
014400           IF L05-DMI-BYTE > X'79' AND < X'90'
014500              MOVE 'Y'                  TO LINK-CATALOG-IND
014600           ELSE
014700              MOVE 'N'                  TO LINK-CATALOG-IND
014800           END-IF
014900           IF L05-DMI-BYTE > X'19' AND < X'30'
015000              MOVE 'J'                  TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
015100           ELSE
015200              IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'00'
015300                 MOVE 'R'               TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
015400              ELSE
015500                 IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'01'
015600                    MOVE 'P'            TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
015700                 ELSE
015800                    IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'02'
015900                       MOVE 'G'         TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
016000                    ELSE
016100                       MOVE '?'         TO LINK-FILE-TYPE
016200                    END-IF
016300                 END-IF
016400              END-IF
016500           END-IF
016600           MOVE L05-VOLUME-COUNT-BYTE   TO WORK-BINARY-2R
016700           MOVE WORK-BINARY-2           TO LINK-VOLUME-COUNT
016800           MOVE L05-VOLUME-LIST         TO LINK-VOLUMES
016810           IF SW-MAIN
016811               DISPLAY
016812 'JJJJJJJJSSSSSSSSDDDDDDDDNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNN'
016813               'NNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNMMMMMMMMCTCVVVVVVVVVVVVVVV'
016814               'VVVVVVVVVVVVVVV'
016815               DISPLAY LINK-COMM-AREA
016820           END-IF
016900           GOBACK
017000        ELSE
017100           ADD 20                    TO DDN-TIOT-BINARY
017200           SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-SEGMENT
017300                                     TO DDN-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
017400        END-IF
017500     END-PERFORM.
017501
017510     IF SW-MAIN
017520         DISPLAY
017530         'JJJJJJJJSSSSSSSSDDDDDDDDNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNN'
017540         'NNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNMMMMMMMMCTC'
017560         DISPLAY LINK-COMM-AREA
017570     END-IF.
017600
017700     GOBACK.
017800*------------ END OF SUBROUTINE DDNAME
-------------------------*
```

```
wrote:
> Hi,

The code I have is an assembler program callable from REXX that
returns a list of DSNAMEs for a particular DDNAME.  If you know
assembler it's probably pretty easy to change it to be callable from
PL/I, COBOL, etc.  If you like I'll send it to you.
```

```
"Jessica Colman" <jessica.colman@augustakom.net> wrote in message 
news:h3nouc$fca$1@svr7.m-online.net...
> Hi,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jessica

Look at paragraph JFCB-INFO in the program below:

       ID DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.   COBPTR.
       AUTHOR.       CHARLIE HOTTEL.
       DATE-WRITTEN. 08/22/01.
       DATE-COMPILED.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    VS COBOL II POINTER FEATURE EXAMPLE                        *
      *    USING MVS CONTROL BLOCKS                                   *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    BASED ON ARTICLE "THE POINTER FEATURE OF VS COBOL II" BY   *
      *    ANNE PETICOLAS IN ENTERPRISE SYSTEMS JOURNAL, MARCH 1991.  *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT TEST-FILE ASSIGN TO DD1.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  TEST-FILE
           LABEL RECORDS STANDARD
           RECORDING MODE IS F
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
           DATA RECORD IS TEST-REC.
       01  TEST-REC PIC X(80).
      /
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01  CUSTOMER-NUMBER                     PIC X(6).
       01  SQL-STATEMENT.
           49  SQL-STATEMENT-LENGTH           PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE +320.
           49  SQL-STATEMENT-TEXT             PIC X(320).

TEST***01  WS-TEST                            PIC S9(9) COMP VALUE ZERO.
       01  WS-WORK                             PIC S9(9) COMP.
       01  WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT                  PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-LEN                              PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-LENGTH REDEFINES WS-LEN          PIC X(02).
       01  WS-FLAG                             PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-FLAG1 REDEFINES WS-FLAG          PIC X(02).
       01  PRIME-CANDIDATE             PIC 9(10) COMP VALUE 2113148651.
       01  I                           PIC 9(10) COMP.
       01  WS-QUOTIENT                 PIC 9(10) COMP.
       01  WS-REMAINDER                PIC 9(10) COMP.

       01  WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-TIOT-SEG-POINT.
           05  WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT REDEFINES WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.

       01  WS-JFCB-POINT.
           05  WS-JFCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-JFCB-PTR REDEFINES WS-JFCB-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-JFCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES  WS-JFCB-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-JFCB-LOW-3               PIC X(3).

       01  WS-POINT.
           05  WS-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-PTR REDEFINES WS-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-LOW-3                    PIC X(3).

       01  WS-SWA-POINT.
           05  WS-SWA-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-SWA-PTR REDEFINES WS-SWA-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-SWA-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-SWA-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-SWA-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-QMAT-POINT.
           05  WS-QMAT-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-QMAT-PTR REDEFINES WS-QMAT-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
      /
       01  WS-UCB-POINT.
           05  WS-UCB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-UCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-UCB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-UCB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-DEB-POINT.
           05  WS-DEB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-DEB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-DEB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-DEB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-DCB-POINT.
           05  WS-DCB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-DCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-DCB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-DCB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-CVT-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-CVT-ADDR-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-ASCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-ASCB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-ASSB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-ASSB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-JSAB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
       01  F-TEMP PIC S999V9.
       01  C-TEMP PIC S999V9.
       01  EF-TEMP PIC -ZZ9.9.
       01  EC-TEMP PIC -ZZ9.9.

       01  M-DIST PIC S999V9.
```
