```
       01  EK-DIST PIC -ZZ9.9.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * PUT THE STATEMENTS BELOW IN AN ASSEMBLER PROGRAM TO LOCATE    *
      * THE DISPLACEMENTS OF THE SYMBOLS.  THESE LINES WERE SHIFTED   *
      * TO THE RIGHT TO MAKE COBOL COMMENTS.  SHIFT THEM BACK TO THE  *
      * LEFT SO THAT THE WORD 'TITLE' IS IN COLUMN 10.                *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *              TITLE 'PREFIX STORAGE AREA'                      *
      *              IHAPSA LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'TASK CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *              IKJTCB LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'SECONDARY TASK CONTROL BLOCK'             *
      *              IHASTCB LIST=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'DATA EXTENT BLOCK'                        *
      *              IEZDEB LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'DATA CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *     DCBDS    DSECT                                            *
      *              DCBD  DSORG=PS                                   *
      *              TITLE 'UNIT CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *     UCBDS    DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFUCBOB                                         *
      *              TITLE 'TASK I/O TABLE'                           *
      *     TIOTDS   DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFTIOT1                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JOB FILE CONTROL BLOCK'                   *
      *     JFCBDS   DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFJFCBN LIST=YES                                *
      *              TITLE 'COMMUNICATION VECTOR TABLE'               *
      *              CVT DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                           *
      *              TITLE 'SUBSYSTEM COMMUNICATION VECTOR TABLE'     *
      *              IEFJSCVT                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JOB STEP CONTROL BLOCK'                   *
      *              IEZJSCB                                          *
      *              TITLE 'SWA QUEUE AREA'                           *
      *              IEFQMNGR                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JES COMMUNICATION TABLE'                  *
      *              IEFJESCT                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JSAB'                                     *
      *              IAZJSAB DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                       *
      *              TITLE 'ASCB'                                     *
      *              IHAASCB DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                       *
      *              TITLE 'ASSB'                                     *
      *              IHAASSB LIST=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'ASXB'                                     *
      *              IHAASXB                                          *
      *              TITLE 'ACEE'                                     *
      *              IHAACEE                                          *
      *              TITLE 'VSAM ACB'                                 *
      *              IFGACB DSECT=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'REMOTE AREAS'                             *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      /
       LINKAGE SECTION.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAPSA)  X'21C' = DECIMAL 540                  *
      *                         X'224' = DECIMAL 548                  *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  PSA.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(540).
           05  TCB-PTR                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(04).
           05  ASCB-PTR                     USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  PSA-ASM REDEFINES PSA.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(540).
           05  PSATOLD                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(04).
           05  PSAAOLD                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IKJTCB)  HAS A 32 BYTE PREFIX AREA             *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  TCB-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  TCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DEB-ADDR                     USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  TIOT-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(164).
           05  JSCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(128).
           05  STCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  TCB-ASM REDEFINES TCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  TCBDEB                       USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  TCBTIO                       USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(164).
           05  TCBJSCB                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(128).
           05  TCBSTCB                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(CVT)                                           *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  CVT-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  CVT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(296).
           05  JESCT-POINTER                USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  CVT-ASM REDEFINES CVT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(296).
           05  CVTJESCT                     USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJESCT)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JESCT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  JESSSCVT-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  JESCT-ASM REDEFINES JESCT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  JESSSCT                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJSCVT)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  SSCVT.
           05  SSCVT-EYE-CATCHER  PIC X(04).
           05  NEXT-SSCVT                   USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  SUBSYSTEM-NAME     PIC X(04).
       01  SSCVT-ASM  REDEFINES SSCVT.
           05  SSCTID             PIC X(04).
           05  SSCTSCTA                     USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  SSCTSNAM           PIC X(04).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFTIOT1)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  TIOT.
           05  JOB-NAME           PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-PROC           PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-STEP           PIC X(08).
       01  TIOT-ASM REDEFINES TIOT.
           05  TIOCSTPN           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOCPSTN           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOCSJSTN          PIC X(08).

       01  TIOT-SEG.
           05  TIO-LEN            PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(03).
           05  DD-NAME            PIC X(08).
           05  SWA-V-ADDR         PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  UCB-ADDR           PIC X(03).
       01  TIOENTRY REDEFINES TIOT-SEG.
           05  TIOELNGH           PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(03).
           05  TIOEDDNM           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOEJFCB           PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  TIOEFSRT           PIC X(03).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJFCBN)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JFCB.
           05  DS-NAME            PIC X(44).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(74).
           05  VOL-SER            PIC X(06).
       01  JFCB-ASM REDEFINES JFCB.
           05  JFCBDSNM           PIC X(44).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(74).
           05  JFCBVOLS           PIC X(06).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEZDEB)  DEB HAS A 36 BYTE PREFIX AREA         *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  DEB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  NEXT-DEB-ADDR      PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DCB-ADDR           PIC X(03).
       01  DEB-ASM REDEFINES DEB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  DEBDEBB            PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DEBDCB             PIC X(03).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(DCBD)                                          *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  DCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DEVICE-TYPE        PIC X.
               88  DISK-3380-X2E  VALUE X'2E'.
               88  DISK-3390-X2F  VALUE X'2F'.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DSORG              PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  RECFM              PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  DDNAME             PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(14).
           05  BLKSIZE            PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(18).
           05  LRECL              PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  DCB-ASM REDEFINES DCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DCBDEVT            PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DCBDSORG           PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DCBRECFM           PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  DCBDDNAM           PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(14).
           05  DCBBLKSI           PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(18).
           05  DCBLRECL           PIC S9(4) COMP.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEZJSCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JSCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(244).
           05  QMPL-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  JSCB-ASM REDEFINES JSCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(244).
           05  JSCBQMPI           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFQMNGR)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  QMPL.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  QMAT-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  QMPL-ASM REDEFINES QMPL.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  QMADD              USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  QMAT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(12).
           05  QMAT-NEXT-POINTER  USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  SWA.
           05  JFCB-ADDR          USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MODGEN(IHASTCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  STCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(188).
           05  JSAB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  STCB-ASM REDEFINES STCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(188).
           05  STCBJSAB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IAZJSAB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JSAB.
           05  JSAB-EYE-CATCHER   PIC X(04).
           05  JSAB-NEXT-PTR      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  JSAB-FLAG1         PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  COMPONENT          PIC X(04).
           05  JOB-ID             PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-NBR            PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  USERID             PIC X(08).
       01  JSAB-ASM REDEFINES JSAB.
           05  JSABID             PIC X(04).
           05  JSABNEXT           USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  JSABFLG1           PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  JSABSCID           PIC X(04).
           05  JSABJBID           PIC X(08).
           05  JSABJBNM           PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  JSABUSID           PIC X(08).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAASCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  ASCB-POINTER                     USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(108).
           05  ASXB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(224).
           05  ASSB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASCB-ASM REDEFINES ASCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(336).
           05  ASCBASSB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAASSB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  ASSB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(168).
           05  JSAB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASSB-ASM REDEFINES ASSB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(168).
           05  ASSBJSAB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MODGEN(IHAASXB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  ASXB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(200).
           05  ACEE-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAACEE)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  ACEE.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(021).
           05  ACEEUSRI           PIC X(008).
      /
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           MOVE SPACE TO SQL-STATEMENT-TEXT.
           MOVE '123456' TO CUSTOMER-NUMBER.
           STRING 'DELETE FROM MM01.CUSTOMER '
                  'WHERE CUSTNO = ''' CUSTOMER-NUMBER ''''
                  DELIMITED BY SIZE
                  INTO SQL-STATEMENT-TEXT
           END-STRING.
           DISPLAY SQL-STATEMENT-TEXT.

           PERFORM JOB-STEP-NAME.
           PERFORM SUBSYSTEM-NAMES.
           PERFORM JFCB-INFO.
           PERFORM DCB-INFO.
           PERFORM JSAB-INFO.
           PERFORM DEGREE-TABLE.
           PERFORM DISTANCE-TABLE.
           MOVE 2113148651 TO PRIME-CANDIDATE.
           PERFORM PRIME-CHECK THRU PRIME-EXIT.
           MOVE 41 TO PRIME-CANDIDATE.
           PERFORM PRIME-CHECK THRU PRIME-EXIT.
           MOVE 51540211 TO PRIME-CANDIDATE.
           PERFORM PRIME-CHECK THRU PRIME-EXIT.

           GOBACK.
      /
       JOB-STEP-NAME.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    JOB NAME AND STEP NAME                                     *
      *      PSA + X'21C' -> TCB -> TIOT                              *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT TO TIOT-POINTER.
           DISPLAY 'JOB NAME=' JOB-NAME
                   '  JOB PROC=' JOB-PROC
                   '  JOB STEP=' JOB-STEP.
           DISPLAY '         '.
      /
       SUBSYSTEM-NAMES.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY SUBSYSTEM NAMES FROM SSCVT CHAIN                   *
      *    CVT -> JSECT -> SSCVT                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'00000010' TO WS-CVT-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF CVT-POINTER TO WS-CVT-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF CVT TO CVT-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF JESCT TO JESCT-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF SSCVT TO JESSSCVT-POINTER.
           DISPLAY 'SUBSYSTEM NAME=' SUBSYSTEM-NAME.
           PERFORM UNTIL NEXT-SSCVT IS = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF SSCVT TO NEXT-SSCVT
               DISPLAY 'SUBSYSTEM NAME=' SUBSYSTEM-NAME
           END-PERFORM.
           DISPLAY '         '.
      /
       JFCB-INFO.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------
      *    FIND DDNAMES AND ASSOCIATED DSNAMES
      *    PSA+X'21C' -> TCB -> TIOT -> TIOT SEG -> SWAREQ(SVA) -> JFCB
      *---------------------------------------------------------------
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT TO TIOT-POINTER.
           SET WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER TO TIOT-POINTER.
           ADD 24 TO WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER.
           PERFORM UNTIL TIO-LEN = LOW-VALUES
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-POINT
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-JFCB-POINT
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-SWA-POINT
               MOVE SWA-V-ADDR TO WS-SWA-LOW-3
               PERFORM SWAREQ
               SET ADDRESS OF JFCB TO  WS-POINTER
               DISPLAY 'DDNAME=' DD-NAME
               DISPLAY 'DSNAME=' DS-NAME
               DISPLAY 'VOL=SER=' VOL-SER
               DISPLAY '********************************************'
               MOVE ZERO TO WS-LEN
               MOVE TIO-LEN TO WS-LENGTH(2:1)
               ADD WS-LEN TO WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT
               SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
           END-PERFORM.
      /
       SWAREQ.
           DIVIDE WS-SWA-PTR BY 16
               GIVING WS-WORK
               REMAINDER WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT.

           IF WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT NOT = 15
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR + 16
           ELSE
               MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF JSCB TO JSCB-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF QMPL TO QMPL-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF QMAT TO QMAT-POINTER
               SET WS-QMAT-POINTER TO QMAT-POINTER
               PERFORM UNTIL WS-SWA-PTR <= 65536
                   SET WS-QMAT-POINTER TO QMAT-NEXT-POINTER
                   SET ADDRESS OF QMAT TO QMAT-NEXT-POINTER
                   COMPUTE WS-SWA-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR - 65536
               END-PERFORM
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR + WS-QMAT-PTR + 1
               SET ADDRESS OF SWA TO WS-POINTER
               SET WS-POINTER TO JFCB-ADDR
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-PTR + 16
            END-IF.
      /
       DCB-INFO.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY DCB INFORMATION                                    *
      *    PSA+X'21C' - > TCB -> DEB -> DCB                           *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           OPEN INPUT TEST-FILE.
           MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-DEB-POINT.
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET WS-DEB-POINTER TO DEB-ADDR.
           PERFORM UNTIL WS-DEB-POINTER IS = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF DEB TO WS-DEB-POINTER
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-DCB-POINT
               MOVE DCB-ADDR TO WS-DCB-LOW-3
               SET ADDRESS OF DCB TO WS-DCB-POINTER
TEST***********DISPLAY 'DCB-PTR=' WS-DCB-POINTER
               DISPLAY ' DDNAME=' DDNAME
               DISPLAY '  DSORG=' DSORG
               DISPLAY '  RECFM=' RECFM
               DISPLAY 'BLKSIZE=' BLKSIZE
               DISPLAY '  LRECL=' LRECL
               IF DISK-3380-X2E
                   DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=3380'
                   DISPLAY '**************************************'
               ELSE IF DISK-3390-X2F
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=3390'
                       DISPLAY '**************************************'
                    ELSE
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=????'
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=' DEVICE-TYPE
                       DISPLAY '**************************************'
                    END-IF
               END-IF
TEST***********DIVIDE WS-LEN BY WS-TEST GIVING WS-WORK
               MOVE NEXT-DEB-ADDR TO WS-DEB-LOW-3
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE TEST-FILE.
      /
       JSAB-INFO.
           SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO NULL.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY JSAB INFORMATION                                   *
      *    PSA+X'21C' - > TCB -> STCB -> JSAB                         *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF STCB TO STCB-POINTER.
           IF JSAB-POINTER OF STCB IS NOT = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO JSAB-POINTER OF STCB
               SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-POINTER OF STCB
           END-IF.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    PSA+X'224' - > ASCB -> ASSB -> JSAB                        *
      *                        -> ASXB -> ACEE                        *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL
               MOVE X'00000224' TO WS-ASCB-ADDRESS-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF ASCB-POINTER TO WS-ASCB-ADDR-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF ASCB TO ASCB-POINTER
               IF ASXB-POINTER IS NOT = NULL
                   SET ADDRESS OF ASXB TO ASXB-POINTER
                   SET ADDRESS OF ACEE TO ACEE-POINTER
                   DISPLAY 'USERID FROM ACEE = ' ACEEUSRI
                   DISPLAY '**************************************'
               END-IF
               IF ASSB-POINTER IS NOT = NULL
                   SET ADDRESS OF ASSB TO ASSB-POINTER
                   SET WS-ASSB-ADDR-POINTER TO ASSB-POINTER
                   IF JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB IS NOT = NULL
                       SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB
                       SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB
                   END-IF
               END-IF
           END-IF.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    IF JSAB-FLAG1 > 127 THE JSAB IS INVALID                    *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL
               CONTINUE
           ELSE
               MOVE ZERO TO WS-FLAG
               MOVE JSAB-FLAG1 TO WS-FLAG1(2:1)
               PERFORM UNTIL WS-FLAG <= 127 OR
                             WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL OR
                             JSAB-EYE-CATCHER NOT = 'JSAB'
                   SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-NEXT-PTR
                   SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER
                   MOVE ZERO TO WS-FLAG
                   MOVE JSAB-FLAG1 TO WS-FLAG1(2:1)
               END-PERFORM
           END-IF.

           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS NOT = NULL
               DISPLAY 'EYE-CATCHER=' JSAB-EYE-CATCHER
               DISPLAY '  COMPONENT=' COMPONENT
               DISPLAY '     JOB ID=' JOB-ID
               DISPLAY ' JOB NUMBER=' JOB-ID
               DISPLAY '     USERID=' USERID
           ELSE
               DISPLAY 'JSAB INFORMATION NOT FOUND'
           END-IF.
      /
       DEGREE-TABLE.
           DISPLAY '**************************************'
           DISPLAY 'FARENHEIT    CENTIGRADE'
           PERFORM VARYING F-TEMP FROM -20  BY 1
                   UNTIL F-TEMP > 105
               COMPUTE C-TEMP = (5 * (F-TEMP - 32)) / 9
               MOVE F-TEMP TO EF-TEMP
               MOVE C-TEMP TO EC-TEMP
               DISPLAY EF-TEMP '       ' EC-TEMP
           END-PERFORM.
      /
       DISTANCE-TABLE.
           DISPLAY '**************************************'
           DISPLAY 'MILES        KILOMETERS'
           PERFORM VARYING M-DIST FROM 1  BY 1
                   UNTIL M-DIST > 500
               COMPUTE K-DIST = M-DIST * 1.609
               MOVE M-DIST TO EM-DIST
               MOVE K-DIST TO EK-DIST
               DISPLAY EM-DIST '       ' EK-DIST
           END-PERFORM.
      /
       PRIME-CHECK.
           DIVIDE PRIME-CANDIDATE BY 2
               GIVING WS-QUOTIENT
               REMAINDER WS-REMAINDER
           END-DIVIDE.
           IF WS-REMAINDER = ZERO
               DISPLAY '**************************************'
               DISPLAY PRIME-CANDIDATE '  IS NOT PRIME (DIVISIBLE BY 2)'
               GO TO PRIME-EXIT
           END-IF.

           PERFORM VARYING I FROM 3  BY 2
                   UNTIL I > PRIME-CANDIDATE
               DIVIDE PRIME-CANDIDATE BY I
                   GIVING WS-QUOTIENT
                   REMAINDER WS-REMAINDER
               IF WS-REMAINDER = ZERO AND
                  WS-QUOTIENT IS = 1
                   GO TO PRIME-IS-PRIME
               END-IF
               IF WS-REMAINDER = ZERO AND
                  WS-QUOTIENT IS NOT = 1
                   DISPLAY '**************************************'
                   DISPLAY PRIME-CANDIDATE '  IS NOT PRIME'
                   DISPLAY '        I = ' I
                   DISPLAY ' QUOTIENT = ' WS-QUOTIENT
                   DISPLAY 'REMAINDER = ' WS-REMAINDER
                   GO TO PRIME-EXIT
               END-IF
           END-PERFORM.
       PRIME-IS-PRIME.
           DISPLAY '**************************************'.
           DISPLAY PRIME-CANDIDATE '  IS  PRIME'.
       PRIME-EXIT.
           EXIT.


```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DD concatenation

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T17:07:52-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ndKdnR7n3bqHdf3XnZ2dnUVZ_q-dnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<h3nouc$fca$1@svr7.m-online.net> <D7Gdnd49g7bsQ_3XnZ2dnUVZ_uSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:D7Gdnd49g7bsQ_3XnZ2dnUVZ_uSdnZ2d@earthlink.com...
>
> "Jessica Colman" <jessica.colman@augustakom.net> wrote in message 
…[717 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I forgot to mention that this kind of code easily breaks when controls 
blocks change.  Over the years since the original article this has happened 
a couple of times.  You should think long and hard before you use this kind 
of thing in a production program.  While I expanded upon the original 
published program over the years, I see it mainly as a learning experience 
and an interesting challenge, but I have never put this kind of thing into a 
production program.  I am not saying you should not, I am just advising you 
to weigh the pros and cons very carefully.  This advice is surly worth every 
cent you have paid for it.
```

---

<a id="t-738dce2e7c"></a>
## Thread: Converting Cobol programs to pl/sql procedures or pakages — 18 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Converting Cobol programs to pl/sql procedures or pakages

- **From:** jmoore <jmoore207@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T11:31:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<43915d44-2d96-45d9-889a-f5b15f9ee463@y7g2000yqa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Does anyone have any example code of Cobol programs that were
converted to PL/sql procedures/packages? Our company is trying to
convert programs to pl/sql and they really haven't addressed many of
the challenges. First being batch programs that display/accept data
from the user. Our software runs thru a .net front end or batch
programs can be ran from a unix prompt. I have limited time to learn
this and other than reading books, I cuold use some great examples
that I could see what the Cobol program looks like and what the pl/sql
program lloks like. Even if it is a Cobol program that calls a
procedure would be great Any help would be greatly appreciated.
```

```
>from the user. Our software runs thru a .net front end or batch
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Let me get this straight... your company has given you an assignment to do 
something that you have not yet learned and must complete in a very 
limited time?

I would start by polishing up my resume and preparing to find another 
employer.  Without knowledge of both the (from) side and the (to) side the 
likelihood of success is, in my experience, diminished.

DD
```

```
The exercise is not necessarily ridiculous; a few of the folks posting 
here, I believe, have some experience moving from a file-based system to a 
table-based one.  It is not a trivial thing to do and there are companies 
which employ very experienced people to make sure the transition is 
successful.

To entrust it to someone who knows little about it indicates - at least 
according to my minimal experience - that Someone Important wants to see 
it fail.  Try not to take it personally.

DD
```

```
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> in here to be a jerk, just looking for some help that's all.

jmoore,

Not sure if I have read you correctly, but do you know little or nothing 
about SQL ?

Like any new activity in this game, you have got to do some reading. SQL 
((R)DBMS or DBMS) is adequately covered by paperback texts; of course 
you can always shell out for the expensive hard-cover tomes. Perhaps 
some of those familiar can recommend texts that you should look at.

As regards syntax, go googling and you'll find interesting examples 
which may appeal to your particular requirement. Surprisingly, a 
PC-user, I found that the IBM site(s) have very good examples of SQL 
syntax using COBOL. Granted it probably is all based on DB2 and some 
'VERBs' you may not be able to use with your particular SQL Preprocessor 
- nevertheless you can probably do a fair amount of 'cut and paste' from 
their examples, obviously changing COBOL variable names.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting Cobol programs to pl/sql procedures or pakages

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-29T20:47:50+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7daglpF2as6eiU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<43915d44-2d96-45d9-889a-f5b15f9ee463@y7g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <e299e138-392b-4659-9d37-d76fcc6a340a@z34g2000vbl.googlegroups.com> <h4n3tg$rte$1@reader1.panix.com> <4a1e8a66-23ba-4b04-94ad-0c33015e291b@q35g2000vbi.googlegroups.com> <h4nc9g$3kp$1@reader1.panix.com> <27ac8c36-9fa8-4382-8433-dee2a2fa0a66@z28g2000vbl.googlegroups.com>`

```
jmoore wrote:
> On Jul 28, 1:25 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> in here to be a jerk, just looking for some help that's all.

Fair enough. But please understand that there is a time and a place for free 
advice.

The problem here is that you have been asked to do something that your 
company should be paying for, and some of the people in this forum are the 
people who would be paid by them. Even with the kindest hearts in the world, 
this one goes against the grain...:-)

Nevertheless, moved by your situation, I'll try and help.

If you know absolutely nothing about SQL you can learn it pretty quickly 
from: http://www.w3schools.com/SQl/default.asp

By "quickly" I mean "in an afternoon" (Compared to other "languages", SQL 
has a limited command set and derives its power from being able to be 
recursively structured (that's what the "S" stands for in the name...)

PL/SQL is SQL on steroids.  Once you have mastered the basic SQL, given you 
have a programming background, the loop control and evaluations available in 
PL/SQL shouldn't be too difficult.

In the same way any computer program can be decomposed into "sequence", 
"evaluate" and "iterate" so PL/SQL has constructs to support all of these 
functions.

Why not take a small COBOL program (with a simple record layout) and analyse 
it along those lines, then apply the equivalent PL/SQL constructs and see 
what you end up with? You said you want to get experience at this, I can see 
no better way. If you tell your Boss you're doing a pilot "proof of concept" 
that should get him off your back long enough for you to successfully 
complete your first conversion.

Understand that there is a difference between the way in which flat files 
and COBOL perceive data and the way the Relational Database (ORACLE or any 
other) does. For your first cut, try and avoid a record layout that contains 
OCCURS and REDEFINES.  Fields defined as group levels in COBOL may or may 
not be needed on the database, depending on whether they are referenced 
frequently in code.

I have written at length about all of this and there are free articles you 
can browse from the cobol21 web site. The ISAM to RDB page is still 
incomplete, but there is a wealth of information on the PRIMA Toolset page.
(http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21/PRIMAToolset.aspx)

The "Pathway to Relational Database" document (click on the link just below 
the image of the Arctic winter...) has full explanations and an appendix 
that explains DB Normalization in very simple terms. I came to grips with 
all of these problems when I was building tools to automate the conversion 
from flat files to RDB, but whether you use the tools or not, the 
explanations will, hopefully, be useful. (You might at least get enough 
ammunition to persuade your employers that they need someone knowledgable to 
assist with this, even if for a few days...) Once you have a fair grasp of 
what is involved generally, conversion to PL/SQL becomes just a "special 
case", where the RDB is "smarter" than would normally be the case.

Starting with no knowledge of RDB or SQL is not a good position to be in but 
the task is not impossible. Give yourself a few days for "education" and 
read avidly. There is a great deal of material available and part of the 
difficulty is deciding what is relevant and what is not, for your particular 
purposes.

I tried a quick GOOGLE using "COBOL to PL/SQL" and received over a million 
hits, some of which looked very pertinent. There are companies who have 
automated this process (much as I have done with standard RDB) and I'm sure 
you can get the assistance you need, if your company is prepared to pay for 
it.

Good Luck!

Pete.
```

```
On Jul 29, 4:56 am, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> "I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."

I will check them all out! Thanks everyone for your help. We have been
embedded sql in alot of our cobol programs(Pro*Cobol), I think the
issue is how to do multiple cursors for or while loops etc. We have an
example program that does a sort and returns a report. The sort
basically takes table 1 reads until eof and then uses the mbrno to
read table 2 to get a few fields, then table 3 to get a few fields
releases the sort-rec and goes back to table 1. When done The return
reads sort and creates a report. We have many batch programs that do
this. I am keeping the cobol part to accept/display. I need to pass
the answers as variables to throw records away I do not need. I am
trying to figure out how to combine all 3 in the cursors and order by
the sort-key which is circuit and station then create the report all
inside a package or procedure. I have been reading pl/sql manuals
trying to figure out the best way to accomplish this. They seem to not
wnat us to use any temporary tables here. I know oracle has the utl-
file package and wasn't sure if I can use that. Maybe combine all the
cursors and do a hash group by. There are quite a few infastructure
issues that need to be worked out as well. Let me finish by saying I
really appreciate everyone's guidance here. I totally understand that
all of you have worked hard to attain your knowledge and to part with
it for free doesn't do you any favors. Unfortunately, with the job
market the way it is, I have to learn this fast. Without going into
detail the VP of development here has "farmed" out the pl/sql to India
and all of the sudden now the owner wants us to know it. So our whole
department, which has been promised to get training is now on its own.
There are alot of us who only know cobol here and I believe they
definitely want to get rid of Cobol. SO, DD you were right somebody
does want "us" to fail. But the "us" is the Cobol department. Pete,
James, DD thanks for your input! I wish I could go into more detail
about what is going on, but it wouldn't be politically correct.
```

- **References:** `<43915d44-2d96-45d9-889a-f5b15f9ee463@y7g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <e299e138-392b-4659-9d37-d76fcc6a340a@z34g2000vbl.googlegroups.com> <h4n3tg$rte$1@reader1.panix.com> <4a1e8a66-23ba-4b04-94ad-0c33015e291b@q35g2000vbi.googlegroups.com> <h4nc9g$3kp$1@reader1.panix.com> <27ac8c36-9fa8-4382-8433-dee2a2fa0a66@z28g2000vbl.googlegroups.com> <7daglpF2as6eiU1@mid.individual.net> <7dah6oF2bmbjiU1@mid.individual.net> <725ac0a7-14c7-488b-8e32-f96f1edc498e@h21g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <7db0dcF2ai115U1@mid.individual.net>`

```

Moving COBOL with inline Oracle Pro*COBOL to PL/SQL is really
esoteric.

Since you and your company seem so lost, take what you know already
(Java, C#, C++, Ruby, ...) and make a proof of concept and change
"boss" minds.

Batch applications work best with COBOL, C++, Java, C# and PL/SQL is a
centralized resource or API for applications. How do you take a ISAM
file in PL/SQL and do operations with it?
How do you read/write BCD (decimal) in PL/SQL?
Is your PL/SQL code portable? What will you do when Oracle 12 breaks
your code?

Have you evaluated converting you COBOL code to Java packages inside
Oracle Database? That would make more sense to me.

Good luck
```

---

### How to change Print to Print to File on MF Cobol on DOS 6.2 under VirtualBox on Win XP Box

- **From:** Mel_3 <myemaillist@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T14:19:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>`

```
STATUS:
- I'm trying to resurrect an old custom COBOL ap that ran on an old
DOS 6.2 PC.
- Loaded Sun VirtualBox onto a Win XP machine & installed DOS 6.2 - it
worked!
- Ported old system files over and got the Application to come up and
work... WOW !

BUT... the application wants to render most of the "reports" to the
printer... and not to the screen !

AND... VirtualBox does not support the parallel printer port (LPT1)...

I even found an old DOS TSR utility LPT2file.com that should redirect
from LPT1 to a file... it seemed to install fine.

BUT, the Print Jobs just go into a Cue and never get saved as files.

I've been rooting around the COBOL source (.CBL) files thinking that
it would be nice to find where the command to print is and modify that
to save the query results as a text file...

But I'm no COBOL programmer so even if I located it in the source
code... I wouldn't know how to modify it... much less recompile it.

Anybody got any idea's?

Thanks for any help
```

```
> STATUS:
> - I'm trying to resurrect an old custom COBOL ap that ran on an old
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for any help

VirtualBox does support serial ports. Try setting up COM1 as a raw
file and redirect LPT1 to COM1 in MS-DOS.

mode lpt1=com1
```

```
Riplin said...

"VirtualBox does support serial ports. Try setting up COM1 as a raw
file and redirect LPT1 to COM1 in MS-DOS. "

I got this to work and the raw data file is written to the host... not
the guest... but...
1 - The raw data file terminates at about 1k of data regardless of
size of the report we are printing to the raw data file
2 - After I print one report... and attempt to print a second
report... it won't overwrite the raw data file.
3 - I think I have it working because I can go to the command line and
enter PRINT AUTOEXEC.BAT and the text from that file shows up in the
raw data file.
4 - There seems no way to print additional raw data files after the
first as VirtualBox makes you enter the path and file name in the
serial port setup window... unless I'm doing it wrong.

Fred said...

"Can you post the SELECT statement (in the INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION) of
that
file ? There you can change the ASSIGN TO and ORGANIZATION of the
file. "

--- snippet start
    SELECT SORT-WORK-1
        ASSIGN TO "INTERLST.SRT".
    SELECT PRINT-FILE
        ASSIGN TO PRINTER.
DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.
    COPY "\STUFF\COPYBOOK\CONTROL.CPY".
    COPY ...
etc
--- snippet end

Thanks for any help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to change Print to Print to File on MF Cobol on DOS 6.2 under VirtualBox on Win XP Box

- **From:** Fred Mobach <fred@mobach.nl>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T22:16:12+02:00

```
Mel_3 wrote:

> Fred said...
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>         ASSIGN TO PRINTER.

You see where the printfile is assigned, you can change that to
something like :
000260     SELECT PRINT-FILE
000270         ASSIGN TO PRINTFILE
000280         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

(The preceding linenumbers can be omitted, depending upon the compiler.
I just copied from an old source file here).

> DATA DIVISION.
> FILE SECTION.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> --- snippet end

Please post the content of that copy file \STUFF\COPYBOOK\CONTROL.CPY,
so some one might post an interesting remark.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to change Print to Print to File on MF Cobol on DOS 6.2 under VirtualBox on Win XP Box

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T08:07:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>`

```
Mel_3 wrote:
> STATUS:
> - I'm trying to resurrect an old custom COBOL ap that ran on an old
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for any help

This is pretty much the same problem that DOS programs have in writing to 
USB printers.

First, why a virtual box? Most old DOS programs run well under XP's command 
prompt.

Second, an excellent fix for the issue is RPG (Report Program Generator). It 
intercepts writes to LPT1 and stashes them in a file. Meanwhile another part 
of RPG is polling the file's location looking for any activity. When a file 
is found, RPG sends the file to the printer of your choice.

Third, you can redirect printing to any printer via pooling a shared printer 
port. The technique is explained at

http://members.shaw.ca/bsanders/printfromdos.htm

Forth, its "queue" not "cue."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: English how he/she is written

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T13:09:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org>`

```
Doug Miller wrote:
> In article <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com>, "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> <g>

I picked up on that, was going to write a message, then thought Nah.... 
Anyway seeing yours ......

Are you a Brit Doug ?

My Concise OECD confirms what you've got above. As George Bernard Shaw 
wrote, "America and England are two great nations separated by a common 
language".

But even in 'English' English it can be confusing, 40 is "Forty" not 
"Fourty" and it's the 'Roaring Forties", etc., etc. Perchance there are 
different rules in the States ? (Using the English/United States spell 
checker it just queried my use of 'Fourty' suggesting 'Forty' instead).

Reading Canadian papers you could never determine which way was up and 
which spelling was kosher. Same with date formats, written or used with 
computers. We had a gentleman, Canadian born, called Conrad Black, now 
aka Lord Black of Cold Harbour, (pretty picture of him in his Gilbert 
and Sullivan dress-up kit, with wife Barbara Amiel, (a columnist), first 
time in the Lords with a smiling Maggie and Kissinger in attendance). He 
  managed to buy most of the Canadian newspapers and owned the Telegraph 
in London. He was an arrogant SOB, using 'our' newspaper columns to 
express his views on how Canada should function; the Anglophile was 
ensconced in London.

He did do one good thing for us - insisted that the papers in Canada 
should use 'English' English, or more accurately 'Canadian' English. The 
official spelling is 'colour' as opposed to 'color', although the latter 
is acceptable. But the English word 'plough' kinda rhymes with 'How now 
brown cow' - but N. Americans would probably pronounce that "pluff". So 
Canada's official word is the American "plow".

Hey, try and get somebody to pronounce 'Worcester(shire) Sauce" 
correctly. They get in right in the New England states because of the 
historical roots to old Albion. And "Dr. Foster went to Gloucester" is 
pronounced "Dr. Foster went to Gloster".

I know looking at Microsoft stuff, that there are separate 
'dictionaries' - strictly speaking 'spell checkers' - for each country 
with former English roots. So down in the Antipodes, have you adapted to 
Americanisms, e.g. "winterize" ? I realise that you will also have Maori 
words in your version of English.

The one that made me really laugh years ago came from Steve Biggs, M/F 
Newbury, Berkshire (pronounced BARK-SHEER in UK). Can't recall the 
topic, but let's say it made reference to 'Dialogs', not 'dialogues'. 
Putting on his inventive cap he came up with something like :-

	"Well in this particular situation you would 'dialogize' it."

I jokingly congratulated him on reshaping the English language. I'm sure 
he had a laugh when he realised what he had done.

Another confusing thing about English/English - check above and you will 
see I type words like 'realise, and the spellchecker wants 'realize'. I 
believe the official current English is 'realize' and deprecates the use 
of words with the "..ise". But the "ise" is how my generation learned to 
spell, and very often the 'ise' appears in French words which are the 
'ancestors' to English spellings.

PS: Hooray ! - after a long-dragged out case over two Hollinger 
newspaper companies, based in USA and Canada, the arrogant SOB, Black 
above, is doing 6 years jail time in the States, appealing it, to the 
Supreme Court I think, and meanwhile he would like a 'Get out of Jail 
Free card', so he can be doing his own thing until the Supreme Court 
judgment. The latest - 'No dice' from the female judge who took his case 
in the first place. Of course his defence offers all sorts of excuses - 
but then there is video of the SOB discretely removing sensitive files 
from his own office ...... ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T14:23:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6vm1651kddvdgmlb81smdegjnobifvmd3p@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad>`

```
On Fri, 17 Jul 2009 13:09:56 -0600, "James J. Gavan"
<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

>He did do one good thing for us - insisted that the papers in Canada 
>should use 'English' English, or more accurately 'Canadian' English. The 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Canada's official word is the American "plow".

Now this American read it as "plow".   I also like to spell theatre,
but for the most part, I use American spelling and pronunciation.

>Hey, try and get somebody to pronounce 'Worcester(shire) Sauce" 
>correctly. They get in right in the New England states because of the 
>historical roots to old Albion. And "Dr. Foster went to Gloucester" is 
>pronounced "Dr. Foster went to Gloster".

I went to high school in New England, so I pronounce the sauce like
the city.    

But typically American English is more phonetic than British English.
I assume that is because most Americans had ancestors who were
literate before they spoke English, while most Brits had ancestors who
spoke English before they were literate.

We still have oddities.    We have a state named "snowfall" in
Spanish, but we don't pronounce it the Spanish way.   Old timers in
Pueblo, Colorado pronounce the city in an Americanized way which is
dying out as more people expect it to be pronounced the Spanish way.

I expect far fewer USAmericans speak French than Brits do, so French
words are often mis-pronounced.   But more of us speak Spanish.

I have read that France is the only place where we can find CoBOL that
doesn't use English key words.    And the only international airport
which doesn't use English is Montreal.  
```

```
Howard Brazee  <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

[snip]

>I went to high school in New England, so I pronounce the sauce like
>the city.

New England, last I looked, encompasses six states and a variety of 
accents; consider that less than 100 miles separates The Peabody Museum of 
Natural History in New Haven, Connecticut and the city pf Peabody, 
Massachusetts

In New Haven it is pronounced 'PEE-body' and the latter is rendered as 
'PIH-bih-dee'.

On the other hand... in Switzerland you can change from German to French 
to Italian by the mere crossing of a few streets.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-18T13:03:58-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3t0np02e1h@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<h3r2hf$i9j$3@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad> <6vm1651kddvdgmlb81smdegjnobifvmd3p@4ax.com> <h3r2hf$i9j$3@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
> New England, last I looked, encompasses six states and a variety of 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'PIH-bih-dee'.

You needn't go nearly that far. There's a Peabody Museum in Salem,
Massachusetts, the name of which is usually pronounced "PEE-Boh-dee",
with a more open vowel and secondary stress for the second syllable,
while the name of the city of Peabody is generally rendered by locals
as, as you wrote, "PIH-bih-dee" or sometimes "PEE-bih-dee". And Salem
and Peabody share a border.

For that matter, there's little agreement among residents of the
Boston area on how to pronounce "Dorchester". On the other hand, while
most folks in the Worcester, Massachusetts area agree how to pronounce
"Marlborough" (of DEC fame), there's no consensus on how to spell it.
```

```
You sure, Jimmy?  This transplanted Brit, now a Canadian citizen, has only
seen it as "plow" in news stories/book/etc. that are American in origin -
either physically, like books, or in news service feeds.

Sometimes stories are "translated" from American to Canadian here.  Amusing
mistakes occur, particularly when changing imperial measurements to metric.
My favourite is the story from Florida about a golfer whose ball landed
uncomfortably close to an alligator.on the fairway.  The golfer, perhaps
energized by adrenalin, blasted his ball 950 metres away.  With a nine iron!

PL
```

```
tlmfru wrote:
> Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
> news:6vm1651kddvdgmlb81smdegjnobifvmd3p@4ax.com...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

Pierre mon ami,

Just to let 'em know we are supposed to be bilingual up in the Great 
White North.

Winston Canadian Dictionary. Plough - as you might expect. Plow "same as 
Plough". But first picked up on it in the Calgary Herald with the 
spelling 'plow' - so took that to be definitive. It's another one like 
'colour' and 'color'; both acceptable in Caanda.

My new daughter-in-law, a country lass NE of Red Deer - freely admits 
she wasn't into the intellectual stuff at school. I'd bet $1,000 she 
would spell it 'plow'.

My deceased mother-in-law, lovely lady, but no formal education; we have 
a government form being a contract where she went into service, probably 
around 13 or 14, as a maid. She came over here, and saw the locals used 
phonetic spelling. She really liked 'New band on stage TONITE'.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T21:14:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3qpj6$9k2$1@news.eternal-september.org>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad>`

```
In article <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad>, jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca wrote:
>Doug Miller wrote:
>> In article <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com>, "HeyBub"
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Are you a Brit Doug ?

Nope. American. Why do you ask?
>
>My Concise OECD confirms what you've got above. As George Bernard Shaw 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>checker it just queried my use of 'Fourty' suggesting 'Forty' instead).

There *are* spelling variations between U.S. English and that used in most of 
the rest of the world. E.g. we write "color" "valor" "flavor" "labor" etc. 
whereas in Britain and Canada, at least, those words end with -our; we drop 
the -ue at the end of catalog and dialog; we spell the name of the chemical 
element Al as "aluminum" whereas in Britain it's "aluminium"; and so on.

But as far as I know, all of the English-speaking world writes four, fourth, 
fourteen... and forty. "Forth" and "fourth" are entirely different words, not 
variant spellings of the same word as are "color" and "colour".

>
>Reading Canadian papers you could never determine which way was up and 
>which spelling was kosher. Same with date formats, written or used with 
>computers. We had a gentleman, Canadian born, called Conrad Black, now 
>aka Lord Black of Cold Harbour,

.. Harbor, to me. :-)

> (pretty picture of him in his Gilbert 
>and Sullivan dress-up kit, with wife Barbara Amiel, (a columnist), first 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>brown cow' - but N. Americans would probably pronounce that "pluff". 

Or "ploff" to rhyme with cough.
Or "plew" to rhyme with through.
Or "ploe" to rhyme with though.

Ain't English spelling grand?

>So 
>Canada's official word is the American "plow".
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>pronounced "Dr. Foster went to Gloster".

I've always heard it pronounced "woostersher" or "woostasher". Hopefully 
that's at least close to correct....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T23:36:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3r1u6$i9j$2@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad>`

```
In article <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad>,
James J. Gavan <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:

[snip]

>But the English word 'plough' kinda rhymes with 'How now 
>brown cow' - but N. Americans would probably pronounce that "pluff".

I am an American, born of two Americans and I have spent most of my life 
in the United States of America.  In my limited experience I have *never* 
heard anyone pronounce 'plough' as 'pluff'... I have heard it pronounced 
to rhyme with 'what's *that* word?', true.

I've also noticed that in the United States of American most native 
speakers can differentiate between the pronounciations of though, through, 
cough and couch (yes, I had to reach for that last one but all of them 
contain a diphthong-biconsonant 'ou(consonant)h' combination).

How someone not raised in the language learns it I will never know.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T21:31:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fua8m.293427$op1.120678@en-nntp-05.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad> <h3r1u6$i9j$2@reader1.panix.com>`

```
For "plough" UK vs US, isn't the real answer that "plow" is the preferred 
spelling (for both the noun and the verb) in the US?  With this spelling, there 
is no question of the pronunciation.

For a "document review" project that I am currently doing, I am using
   http://www3.telus.net/linguisticsissues/BritishCanadianAmerican.htm

as a fairly complete (if not absolutely so) list of spelling differences.

The one that interested me is "cancelled" vs "canceled"

My spell-checker accepts both and I don't remember ever running into this 
(before) as a UK/US difference.
```

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VNr8m.23946$Qg6.268@newsfe14.iad>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad> <h3r1u6$i9j$2@reader1.panix.com>`

```
Here's a question:

when is "meter" correct and when is "metre"?

There is a very specific and rational answer.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2009-07-19T03:13:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3u3cm$rja$2@news.eternal-september.org>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad> <h3r1u6$i9j$2@reader1.panix.com> <VNr8m.23946$Qg6.268@newsfe14.iad>`

```
In article <VNr8m.23946$Qg6.268@newsfe14.iad>, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>Here's a question:
>
>when is "meter" correct and when is "metre"?
>
>There is a very specific and rational answer.

The specific and rational answer in the U.S. is that "meter" is correct when 
referring to
- poetry
- a machine for measuring (e.g. voltmeter, water meter, etc.)
- a unit of length equal to one thousand millimeters or 39.37 inches
and "metre" is correct -- never. It's a misspelling of "meter". <g>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-20T00:04:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iYS8m.15553$O23.2788@newsfe11.iad>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad> <h3r1u6$i9j$2@reader1.panix.com> <VNr8m.23946$Qg6.268@newsfe14.iad> <h3u3cm$rja$2@news.eternal-september.org>`

```
Good answer but not quite there.  A 'meter' is indeed a machine for
measuring: also refers to poetry, although since poetry these days seems to
be mostly haikus and blank, rhythmless verse, it may no longer apply.  But
"metre" is the correct spelling for the 39.37" 1,000 mm distance as it's a
French word which has been taken into English unchanged.  I suppose it boils
down in real life to national useage; although since I was lectured sternly
on the subject by a professional engineer I haven't dared make mistakes
again!

PL


Doug Miller <spambait@milmac.com> wrote in message
news:h3u3cm$rja$2@news.eternal-september.org...

when
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-20T17:29:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h43cqj01p49@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<iYS8m.15553$O23.2788@newsfe11.iad>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad> <h3r1u6$i9j$2@reader1.panix.com> <VNr8m.23946$Qg6.268@newsfe14.iad> <h3u3cm$rja$2@news.eternal-september.org> <iYS8m.15553$O23.2788@newsfe11.iad>`

```
tlmfru wrote:
> But
> "metre" is the correct spelling

Referring to any spelling of an English word (even if it's a French
loan-word) as "correct" with no further qualification is meaningless,
because there is no broadly-recognized authority to establish
"correct" English orthography. There are popular spellings and
less-common variants; there are spellings which have been endorsed by
various self-appointed authorities (such as the publishers of
dictionaries); there are spellings which are older and those that are
newer. But there are no "correct" spellings in an absolute sense.

> for the 39.37" 1,000 mm distance as it's a
> French word which has been taken into English unchanged.

Clearly, if it is spelled "meter", then it has been changed; and so
this argument is false.

In any case, French orthography does not dictate English orthography.

>  I suppose it boils
> down in real life to national useage; although since I was lectured sternly
> on the subject by a professional engineer I haven't dared make mistakes
> again!

Professional engineers are not the arbiters of English spelling. I
wouldn't let a professional engineer tell me how to cook just because
many recipes specify ingredients in grams.
```

```
  You sound a wee bit huffy about this!  I'm surprised that you're taking a
stiff line given your designation at the bottom of the post.

Still, there's a case to be made for "metres".  The adjective is "metric",
not "meteric".

Engineers may not be spelling experts (although some of them are very good!)
or cooking coaches (although they ought to be able to translate grams for
you) but they do know whereof they speak in their own fields. (Well, they
better !)  And sometimes the disticntion is very important.  Fuse or Fuze?

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-28T12:46:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h4nh5m05j0@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<Jwn9m.23072$O23.9857@newsfe11.iad>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad> <h3r1u6$i9j$2@reader1.panix.com> <VNr8m.23946$Qg6.268@newsfe14.iad> <h3u3cm$rja$2@news.eternal-september.org> <iYS8m.15553$O23.2788@newsfe11.iad> <h43cqj01p49@news3.newsguy.com> <Jwn9m.23072$O23.9857@newsfe11.iad>`

```
tlmfru wrote:
>   You sound a wee bit huffy about this!  I'm surprised that you're taking a
> stiff line given your designation at the bottom of the post.

I don't know why a rhetorician would be any less annoyed by false
pedantry than anyone else.

In matters of English usage, one may be a descriptivist or
prescriptivist, and to varying degrees. But prescriptivism that
appeals to arbitrary rules with no justification in the actual
development of the English language - like your engineer's "unchanged
from the French" - is nothing more than one person's idle (and often
uninformed) opinion. Prescriptivists should at least make
well-supported arguments; and they should demonstrate they're aware
that prescriptivism is only ever advice.

> Still, there's a case to be made for "metres".  The adjective is "metric",
> not "meteric".

You can make an argument for any claim; but as English spelling is
irregular, I'm afraid I don't find that one very convincing.

> Engineers may not be spelling experts (although some of them are very good!)
> or cooking coaches (although they ought to be able to translate grams for
> you) but they do know whereof they speak in their own fields. (Well, they
> better !)  And sometimes the disticntion is very important.  Fuse or Fuze?

Engineers often know their own fields - though I'd hardly go so far as
to say that's universally true. (And I've known some PEs who are far
more skeptical about the value of a professional license than you seem
to be.) But even if we believe them to be authorities on systems of
measurement, that doesn't imply any special expertise on English usage.

"Fuse" and "fuze", in the appropriate context, are terms of art, and
so there are grounds for prescribing the distinct spellings (though I
doubt there are many real cases where it would remove any ambiguity).
The same can't be said of "meter" and "metre" in any context I know of.
```

```
Michael Wojcik  <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:

[snip]

>(And I've known some PEs who are far
>more skeptical about the value of a professional license than you seem
>to be.)

Decades on back I was working with some Europeans who were slightly taken 
aback by my refusal to be impressed by credentials; my response was 'I 
have known men who sweep floors who were very wise and PhDs in offices 
with framed papers all over their walls and seemed to have nothing between 
their ears... I would have trouble believing that this experience is 
unique to me.'

(they also were amused by my assertion of 'I am an American... don't tell 
me where or who you are from, show me how you think.')

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2009-07-21T12:43:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h44d63$vjd$1@news.eternal-september.org>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad> <h3r1u6$i9j$2@reader1.panix.com> <VNr8m.23946$Qg6.268@newsfe14.iad> <h3u3cm$rja$2@news.eternal-september.org> <iYS8m.15553$O23.2788@newsfe11.iad>`

```
In article <iYS8m.15553$O23.2788@newsfe11.iad>, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>Good answer but not quite there.  A 'meter' is indeed a machine for
>measuring: also refers to poetry, although since poetry these days seems to
>be mostly haikus and blank, rhythmless verse, it may no longer apply.  But
>"metre" is the correct spelling for the 39.37" 1,000 mm distance

Not in the U.S., it's not. American dictionaries list "metre" as a "variant of 
meter (chiefly British)" or words to that effect.

> as it's a French word which has been taken into English unchanged. 

Unchanged in the U.K., perhaps -- but not in the U.S., it's not.

The British write centre, metre, litre, etc. 
In America, we write center, meter, liter, etc.

> I suppose it boils
>down in real life to national useage; although since I was lectured sternly
>on the subject by a professional engineer I haven't dared make mistakes
>again!

A P.E. is pretty nearly the last person I'd choose as an authority on 
spelling. Most engineers I've met can't spell worth a damn.
```

```
Believe it or don't, Doc, after fifty-plus years in Canada I still blink
sometimes when I see "tire" instead of "tyre".  "The car tires ...": poor
thing, it must have been up all night/nite  "... were completely worn out".
Oh.

Although it's pointless to argue against widespread use of an "incorrect"
spelling, at times incorrect uses boggle the mind: I read recently about
women who had "birthed themselves" on emergency flights from the back of
beyond.  Hmm: infinite regress here.

PL
```

```
trivial of things causing endless back-and-forth... look, there's the 
universe in a grain of sand!

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Doug Miller)
- **Date:** 2009-07-22T21:07:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h47ver$a0g$1@news.eternal-september.org>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <h44d63$vjd$1@news.eternal-september.org> <h44lug$mtj$1@reader1.panix.com> <6nn9m.23070$O23.13669@newsfe11.iad> <h45c5n$fh1$2@reader1.panix.com> <jj6e65181en6ngrjlieltu8i6vgcqeb4il@4ax.com>`

```
In article <jj6e65181en6ngrjlieltu8i6vgcqeb4il@4ax.com>, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>On Tue, 21 Jul 2009 21:32:39 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>inflamable.

I'm not sure why "Dead End" is no longer in widespread use as a road sign, but 
I for one lament its passing.

We used to live at the end of a dead-end road. There was a sign some 20 yards 
from the last intersection saying "Dead End." After we'd lived there about 
five years, the county highway department replaced the sign with one saying 
"No Outlet." 

Within a week or so, we noticed a dramatic increase in the number of drivers 
that would come all the way down to the end of the road, turn around in our 
driveway, and leave -- from maybe three or four a year, to two or three a 
*week*.

I finally called the highway department, explained the situation, and asked 
them to put the old "Dead End" sign back. Eventually, they did. And the 
traffic returned to its former levels.

The part that really baffles me is that:
a) the distance from the sign to the end of the road is only about 300 yards;
b) the road is straight, flat, and unobstructed for the entire distance;
c) athwart the road at the end is a guardrail, clearly visible from the sign;
d) behind the guardrail is a fence, likewise clearly visible;
e) behind the fence is a hedge, likewise clearly visible;
f) beyond the hedge is Interstate 69; the traffic is not only clearly visible 
from 300 yards, but *audible* as well.

How *anyone* could think, even *without* a sign, that that road went anywhere 
besides to my house, is completely beyond my comprehension.

I was also astonished at the number of times I had this conversation, giving 
someone directions to my house: "... turn left at the four-way stop. The road 
dead-ends in about 300 yards. We're at the dead end, and it's the only house 
there."  --- "Which side of the road is it on?"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: English how he/she is written

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-18T12:36:19+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ccjo4F27cfssU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<db1ab610-f312-4745-8ecd-f1ec5d95ddff@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3pur0$ehn$2@news.eternal-september.org> <6048m.17995$Qg6.4992@newsfe14.iad>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Doug Miller wrote:
>> In article <fPOdnd5yPJc46v3XnZ2dnUVZ_radnZ2d@earthlink.com>,
>> "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
<snip>
>
> Hey, try and get somebody to pronounce 'Worcester(shire) Sauce"
> correctly.

When I was living in Hampshire it amused the locals that I would request 
"Worcestershire" sauce. They always referred to it as "Worcester" sauce.

I finally lost patience on one occasion and suggested they read the label on 
the bottle... after that no more problem.

Best of the lot was in Madrid.

You couldn't reasoably expect a Spaniard to get his tongue round such a 
difficult foreign word. They came up with what I thought was a very good 
solution: "Salsa Perrins" (Lea and Perrins are the original makers of the 
definitive Worcestershire sauce).

I spent some time working in North Birmingham (England, not Alabama). When 
the wind was right you could smell the Lea and Perrins factory several miles 
away.


>They get in right in the New England states because of the
> historical roots to old Albion. And "Dr. Foster went to Gloucester" is
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
We are increasingly seeing Maori infiltrate the language as awareness of 
Maori culture is raised. Personally, I think it is a good thing, even though 
the application of it is limited to the local scene. As with most languages, 
the nuances of translation can sometimes make a "foreign" word more apposite 
that the equivalent English one... words like "Schadenfreude" in German, 
which translates as "glee" but is better translated as "joy at another's 
discomfort", "whanau" in Maori, which translates as "family" but is better 
translated as "the ties that bind our extended family by blood and our sense 
of being one unit", "mana" in Maori, which translates as "prestige" but is 
better translated as "the honour and prestige which empowers this person or 
organization and is worthy of respect".

We generally tend not to turn nouns into verbs so "winterize" is not 
something you are likely to hear here. However, there are some acquired 
Americanisms creeping in (and not ALL of them are "bad"...). As a lover of 
the English language I try not to get unwrapped when I hear things like: 
"Wassup?". The primary purpose of language is to communicate...

I wrote a guest Editorial for the local newspaper here and it was published 
yesterday. I had a few phone calls, the majority supportive, and the Editor 
of the paper has asked me if she can syndicate the piece to other papers 
owned by the same group. Apparently, it isn't so much what I said, as the 
way that I said it. It has touched a chord with a number of people who are 
pissed off about the same issue I was, but terrified to say anything for 
fear of being labelled "politically incorrect".  (I have no such fear 
because I'm NOT politically incorrect, and as always, call 'em like I see 
'em...) Sometimes, the way you phrase things (especially on sensitive 
issues) can determine the reaction. I never cease to be amazed by the power 
of language. I'm hopeful that some real good will come from the piece I 
wrote.


> The one that made me really laugh years ago came from Steve Biggs, M/F
> Newbury, Berkshire (pronounced BARK-SHEER in UK). Can't recall the
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> French words which are the 'ancestors' to English spellings.

Both spellings should be "acceptable".
>
> PS: Hooray ! - after a long-dragged out case over two Hollinger
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> sensitive files from his own office ...... ?

"discretely" in the sense he moved them one at a time...? :-)

Pete.
```
