<a id="t-ff100c604c"></a>
## Thread: Reading JCL as data — 5 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Reading JCL as data

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T12:41:46+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7c9vmaF26sg3rU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In Oldene Tymes when prehistoric IBM mainframes stalked the corridors of 
Corporate, and spinning platters were starting to provide random access to 
data as well as sequential, there was an Operating System call "DOS".

This OS had logical names for certain devices.

For example, a distinction was made for reading "data" (from a device called 
SYSRDR) and  reading "control information" (from a device called SYSIPT). 
Sometimes two distinct card readers were used to accommodate these streams, 
but, hardware being extremely expensive, sometimes the same physical device 
had to serve both purposes and it was then logically assigned to SYSIN.

Seeing a post here that required to look at elements of the JCL in a running 
job reminded me that in older (simpler?) times, this was not a problem. You 
could read a jobstream just like you could read anything else (although the 
operator might have to reload the card deck if they were required to be read 
again...normally you read it and saved it somewhere.)

I was wondering if, in today's modern magical world it was still possible to 
open and read a JCL stream, from an application program? If it is, then 
surely there is no need for dropping intoAssembler or writing convoluted 
COBOL to address OS control blocks?

Any comments?

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading JCL as data

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T22:25:46-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4kv5592tlgme2r71kivpp1hk4caiso6k8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7c9vmaF26sg3rU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Fri, 17 Jul 2009 12:41:46 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>In Oldene Tymes when prehistoric IBM mainframes stalked the corridors of 
>Corporate, and spinning platters were starting to provide random access to 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any comments?
I'm not certain how it works in VSE (was DOS then DOS/VS then ...) but
in z/OS and predecessors JCL can be read with a //ddname DD DATA card
but that would not be the JCL that is executing the program in
question but some JCL that the submitter wants read.  What the program
in this case is probably going to try to is find out where it is being
executed from.  This means chasing the //STEPLIB concatenation, if
any, then the //JOBLIB concatenation, if any, then the system list of
executable libraries.  This is not for the faint of heart.  The
overhead for doing so might be low or not so low because the directory
of each library pointed to by the DD statement would have to be read.
The program would have to be authorized to read the directories in
addition to being authorized to execute programs from them.  The task
is cute in assembler and may not be doable in higher level languages.
I suspect the job is equally interesting in Windows depending on PATH
statements etc. 

>
>Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reading JCL as data

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-16T21:03:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e_Q7m.379647$jp1.54962@en-nntp-06.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<7c9vmaF26sg3rU1@mid.individual.net> <b4kv5592tlgme2r71kivpp1hk4caiso6k8@4ax.com>`

```
I agree with DD.  There are times that  COBOL (or other programs) want to "send" 
JCL to the internal reader (to start another program), but I don't think there 
are ways to "read" the "currently executing" JCL.

As far as chasing z/OS control blocks (in COBOL), Gilber Saint-Flour has done 
all the work for you (anyone interested in doing this).  Check out:

 COB2JOB   5kbSample COBOL program to retrieve and display job-related 
information.
COB2SYS5kbSample COBOL program to retrieve and display system-related 
information.

at
  http://gsf-soft.com/Freeware/
```

```
For example... we have jobs that generate WYLBUR control-cards and send 
these off as email... and WYLBUR would generate its own From: card with 
and invalid return address (e.g., NORETURN@MAINFRAME.OURSITE.BIZ).  I used 
Mr St Flour's work (suitably parsed down, of course) to make the username 
the sender's UserID... USERID01@BIGIRON.OURCITE.BIZ.

DD
```

```
While the control blocks can be chased and would give the libraries in
the concatenation, based on my knowledge and what I have read on the
subject on bit.listserv.ibm-main, the program would have to read the
directories of each library in question to find out if the program is
there.  It gets even trickier if the program is invoked by other than
EXEC PGM= (for example called by the batch terminal monitor program of
TSO as are most DB2 programs) because it could then have come from a
TASKLIB.  There may be a LE service (dubious) or assembler MACRO that
involves a program management service that would execute a search and
say where a module was found.  This issue has been the subject of
SHARE (user group for IBM 360/370/390/z series computer) requirements.
>
> COB2JOB   5kbSample COBOL program to retrieve and display job-related 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
```

<a id="t-658bb2ce69"></a>
## Thread: LINAGE question — 2 msgs

### LINAGE question

- **From:** "Roger While" <simrw@sim-basis.de>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T21:48:12+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3qkht$pr9$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Consider a file that has a LINAGE clause.
When a WRITE is made we have the option
to specify [NOT] AT EOP
(or END-OF-PAGE).
EOP is specified as a non-fatal error and therefore
in absence of an EOP clause one should recive
a success status. However, in absence of an EOP clause
AND with a FILE STATUS clause it would appear that
it is not possible to detect this condition.
ie. There is no FILE STATUS 0n for this and gives 00.
(With compilers that I have access to).
Yes, I know that 2002 has the exception code and can be tested
but this is about FILE STATUS.
What do your compilers do?
Oversight in the standard?

Roger
```

#### ↳ Re: LINAGE question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T18:06:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tt78m.377449$4p1.263024@en-nntp-03.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<h3qkht$pr9$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Roger,
  I would expect a "00" status code and to NOT be able to tell when/if "EOP" is 
reached.  Why should you (your program - or in your case, the compiler/run-time) 
care?

This is successful write with the expectation that the next write should also be 
successful.
```

## Thread: Article on COBOL in Federal Computer Week — 10 msgs

### Article on COBOL in Federal Computer Week

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T16:38:23-04:00

#### ↳ Re: Article on COBOL in Federal Computer Week

- **From:** billg999@cs.uofs.edu (Bill Gunshannon)
- **Date:** 2009-07-18T13:38:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ce1ioF26cr6bU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<9-adne6RU52sfP3XnZ2dnUVZ_vSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
In article <9-adne6RU52sfP3XnZ2dnUVZ_vSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>,
	"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> writes:
> http://fcw.com/articles/2009/07/13/tech-cobol-turns-50.aspx
> 
> Talks about some strengths and weaknesss of COBOL vis-a-vis converting to 
> other languages. 
> 

I usally read these articles until I hit the logical falacy that invalidates
the whole premise of the article.  In this one it was: "Cobol simply can't
compete when it comes to displaying output in graphical form."  Just what is
it that makes COBOL not able to "compete when it comes to displaying output
in graphical form"?  Just because the current crop of COBOL programmers
don't do it (or just don't know how to do it) doesn't make it a shortcoming
of the language.  There are a number of ways in which COBOL can do GUI
output.  For the most part. the exact same way other languages do it, by
calling routines to create graphical objects.

bill
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Article on COBOL in Federal Computer Week

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-18T13:17:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3t0nq12e1h@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<7ce1ioF26cr6bU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<9-adne6RU52sfP3XnZ2dnUVZ_vSdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7ce1ioF26cr6bU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Bill Gunshannon wrote:
> 
> I usally read these articles until I hit the logical falacy that invalidates
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> calling routines to create graphical objects.

For that matter, a great many applications these days display
graphical output by including an IMG element in the HTML they write to
their standard output stream, which is then delivered to a web
browser, which fetches the referenced data and displays the image.
COBOL certainly has no problem doing that.

It's particularly ironic, then, that Robinson's comment about
graphical output, which you quoted above, comes immediately after a
sentence about "Web-centric languages". There aren't many prominent
"Web-centric languages" - PHP, ASP, and JSP on the server side;
ECMAScript and Java (when used for applets) on the client side. Pretty
much everything else commonly used for web development is a
general-purpose language. This entire paragraph is pretty much nonsense.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Article on COBOL in Federal Computer Week

- **From:** riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-18T14:13:58-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<e3eadaef-b813-4904-98f6-1d244f076285@d9g2000prh.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9-adne6RU52sfP3XnZ2dnUVZ_vSdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7ce1ioF26cr6bU1@mid.individual.net> <h3t0nq12e1h@news3.newsguy.com>`

```
On Jul 19, 5:17 am, Michael Wojcik <mwoj...@newsguy.com> wrote:
> Bill Gunshannon wrote:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> graphical output, which you quoted above, comes immediately after a

I started developing a Cobol Windows based container loading system
with an early release MF Cobol/2 2.5 in 1991. This had graphical
layout of the vessel and each bay, graphical representation of the
tanks and curves for the torsion and bending. It also could represent
terminal layouts so that one could drag-and-drop containers into the
bays. Later it had a 'helm view' perspective over the container load,
taking into account the draft and trim so that the horizon could be
drawn to ensure adequate view. Actually the helm view could be varied
so that a programmed series of these gave a flyby of the vessel.

> sentence about "Web-centric languages". There aren't many prominent
> "Web-centric languages" - PHP, ASP, and JSP on the server side;
> ECMAScript and Java (when used for applets) on the client side. Pretty
> much everything else commonly used for web development is a
> general-purpose language. This entire paragraph is pretty much nonsense.

I have had Cobol CGI programs since OS/2 with IBMs web server using MS
Cobol 4.5. The same code runs on Windows and Linux and is still used.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Article on COBOL in Federal Computer Week

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-21T09:03:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ilb65lv1l3on3gsme8e5na3u70nj3ovr1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<9-adne6RU52sfP3XnZ2dnUVZ_vSdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7ce1ioF26cr6bU1@mid.individual.net> <06t865pss52h21rdiv5nj7bns1gj9ogubn@4ax.com> <h43cqj11p49@news3.newsguy.com>`

```
On Mon, 20 Jul 2009 17:33:39 -0400, Michael Wojcik
<mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:

>> "Just because the current crop of COBOL programmers don't do it" is
>> sufficient reason.  
>
>Sufficient reason for what? It's not sufficient to support the
>argument's claim, as stated. 

If CoBOL programmers don't do it, who would do it in CoBOL?   We can't
ignore the requirement that CoBOL programs need to be programmed by
CoBOL programmers.

>And, in any case, it's not true - there
>are COBOL applications that produce graphical output. (I have one
>running right now on this machine.) And yes, it was written by a COBOL
>programmer who is still working today, which presumably is enough for
>"current crop" status.

Agreed.

While the graphic programs I have written to print a logo out of
letters on band printers doesn't meet the standards used for this
discussion, there are platforms which aren't so limited.

Trouble is - in most (in my limited experience - all), mainframe shops
have CoBOL for the mainframe, and other languages for desktops, where
the graphics are done.    Speculating on the reason for this, I come
up with:

1.   The people with experience with graphics learned with "free"
tools such as Java, on the desktop.
2.   Experimenting with desktop graphics on the cheap involved using
their skills.
3.   Once a pilot program is working, it is easier to continue it than
to buy compilers and train users to use CoBOL.  
4.   There isn't much incentive to switch from Java to CoBOL anyway.

Notice how much of this is about programming and training and how
little of it is about the actual features of the language.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
```

```
> <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> CoBOL programmers.

Agreed, but clearly there's a difference between "COBOL programmers
don't do this today" and "COBOL programmers will never do this". I
don't think the article was that subtle; my reading of it was that the
author assumed that it was simply impossible to build such
applications in COBOL, regardless of what programmers were willing to try.

> Trouble is - in most (in my limited experience - all), mainframe shops
> have CoBOL for the mainframe, and other languages for desktops, where
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> little of it is about the actual features of the language.

Yes. People come to programming through many routes, but since the
rise of personal computers, I suspect relatively few have started with
COBOL. Many of the youngest generation of programmers probably started
with declarative markup (HTML) and ECMAScript (Javascript / JScript /
ActionScript), then learned web-back-end scripting languages like PHP,
ASP, and JSP.
```

<a id="t-0477f99e66"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power — 38 this month / 47 total

### COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T16:57:32-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
A Java programmer and a COBOL programmer  meet in a bar.  The Java 
programmer says : "Java has more than 35,000 methods listed in 4100 classes 
in the current Javadoc library of API's.  What does COBOL have to offer that 
is equivalent to that, that would make me want to use COBOL instead of 
Java?".

If you are the COBOL programmer what would your answer be?

For reference for the above numbers see: http://www.ddj.com/java/218000160

I assume that a .Net programmer could ask a similiar question.

Well anyway the COBOL EVALUATE statement is highly superior to the Java 
'switch' statement ;-)
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T23:56:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3r33f$i9j$4@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```

Charles Hottel <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
>A Java programmer and a COBOL programmer  meet in a bar.  The Java 
>programmer says : "Java has more than 35,000 methods listed in 4100 classes 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>If you are the COBOL programmer what would your answer be?

My answer would be ''He who thinks all fruits ripen at the same time as 
strawberries knows nothing of grapes' was said by Paracelsus in the 16th 
century.'

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-07-17T23:27:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x3d8m.11614$nL7.6305@newsfe18.iad>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<h3r33f$i9j$4@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3r33f$i9j$4@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com>,
> Charles Hottel <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

In answer to the original question I don't know. What follows is 
specifically Net Express V.3.1. We have since gone through V 4, 5 and 
5.1 - so it wouldn't be unreasonable to make an assumption that over 
time thee might be an increase of 10%.  Rough count on following for N/E 
3.1 :-

  - 039 classes for Base
  - 121 classes for GUIs
  - 050 classes for OLE
  - 007 classes for OLE Auto
  - 004 classes for Java Domain
  - 003 classes for Java Support
  - 010 classes for Dialog System
---------
    234

I've got to do a Gartner now, and extrapolate :-). Can't tell you 
without a very exhaustive analysis exactly how many methods. One class 
might have only 10 another perhaps 30. So (hit and miss), going with an 
average of 20 per class :-

234 classes X 20 methods = 4,680.

Who said big was beautiful. (Bear in mind I have the source of those 
4,680 methods, should I want to take a look see and get a handle on 
M/F's approach).

This might seem like a really stupid question, but I have never delved 
into Java. Does Java act as a replacement for Windows, i.e. a 
replacement OS - in which case, sure it would require considerably more 
classes and methods. Now years ago, when a COBOL programmer using Java 
jumped in with, "I don't believe much in inheritance classes", Pete 
Dashwood replied he agreed. Certainly hierarchical classes were central 
to Smalltalk, culminating in Smalltalk 80. Micro Focus paid the 
compliment of copying Smalltalk concepts, possibly dropping some classes 
and adding new ones applicable to COBOL. Hitachi did the same thing I 
believe - but not much help unless you are proficient in Japanese.  I 
think at first stab, IBM may also have followed the Smalltalk model, 
(that I gleaned from Arranga and Coyle with diagrams comparing, at the 
time, Hitachi, IBM and Micro Focus. Along later comes Fujitsu and I 
don't know what their model was.

Now an interesting thing about Java from a Smalltalk programmer. He had 
switched to Java 'cos that's where the money is, but given is druthers 
would much preferred to have continued in Smalltak where he claimed he 
could achieve the same in much more concise coding.

Of course as a total topic, we are completely screwed with the J4/PL22.4 
approach, producing the barest essentials for Collections/Dictionaries - 
and it appears to me a la Fujitsu style, pioneered by Thane and I think 
the current head honcho who replaced M/F's Don Schrickner is also a 
Fujitsu user - but can't be sure. The whole exercise totally ignored the 
fact that the Collection/Dictionary classes are at the bottom rung in an 
hierachial tree, drawing off various forms of arrays, down to bits and 
bytes.

Now as to my own usage I can achieve all I want from the M/F set of OO 
classes, bearing in mind I've never looked at Webbing etc., promising 
myself I would get around to it some day. I can honestly say, with but 
one exception, I can do it all in M/F OO COBOL. The exception - using 
RGB values to get beyond the ugly 16 prime colours in DOS Screen 
Sections. Without go ape over Technicolor, I can generate pastel colours 
for combo dropdown lists. I got a routine from Newbury which invoked 
Windows APIs. Worked fine with only one Dropdown in a Dialog but went 
belly-up when it had two or more Dropdowns, (say the first was pastel 
blue, and the second, pastel yellow (more like 'Wheat').

When it comes to doing your thing with M/F OO - we aren't just talking 
about Debits and Credits or Inventory Control. My application was, 
(dropped it now), Corrosion Testing at oil/gs plants. I would probably 
need to be mad but the classes do allow me to get into pens, drawings, 
shapes (simple methods to draw a circle for instance, then subsequently 
colour it - but definitely, rather than re-invent the wheel invoke a 
separate Graphics package, from people who have put a lot of resources 
into developing it).

Intellisense with dotNet is great and probably with not too much effort 
both F/J and M/F could have done the same thing, building dictionaries 
to cross reference methods. Being seduced by Microsoft, Fujitsu jumped 
in with both feet and to be competitive, M/F had to do the same. Not 
much point them duplicating Intellisense.

Now I'll ask Charlie a question. As a result of a comment you made in 
another thread about Java, and it appeared to me, Pete concurred, were 
you making the point that Java is a dead duck. A Wiki entry appeared to 
confirm what I thought you were saying.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-18T10:48:24-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S4idnY3QSfY1ffzXnZ2dnUVZ_tWdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3r33f$i9j$4@reader1.panix.com> <x3d8m.11614$nL7.6305@newsfe18.iad>`

```
See comments interspersed below.

"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message 
news:x3d8m.11614$nL7.6305@newsfe18.iad...
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com>,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Doc you really should try drinking, it might make a normal man out of you. 
One whose communication I might have a hope of understanding ;-)

> In answer to the original question I don't know. What follows is 
> specifically Net Express V.3.1. We have since gone through V 4, 5 and 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The Java source code is also available.

> This might seem like a really stupid question, but I have never delved 
> into Java. Does Java act as a replacement for Windows, i.e. a replacement 
> OS - in which case, sure it would require considerably more classes and 
> methods.

The Java virtual machine (JVM)  that runs byte code is a separate entity 
from the Java language/compiler.  The classes and methods referred to are a 
huge resource available to anyone coding in Java.  I do not know if the JVM 
or parts of it are written in Java.  At the lowest level when communication 
with the operating system is required I believe the Java Native Interface 
(JNI) is used probably calling C or C++ programs.

Now years ago, when a COBOL programmer using Java
> jumped in with, "I don't believe much in inheritance classes", Pete 
> Dashwood replied he agreed. Certainly hierarchical classes were central to 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Java supports inheritance.  Each class can 'extend' a single class.  This is 
referred to as implementation inheritance. Also each class can impement as 
many interfaces as necessary.  This is referred to as interface inheritance. 
Java does not support mutiple implementation inheritance in the way that C++ 
does, but you can do a lot of the same things with less of the problems of 
multiple inheritance.  In addition to the above every Java class implicitly 
inherits from the Object class.

Inheritance can be useful but it can also be overused.  Inheritance breaks 
encapsulation.  One of the general rules in the book "Effective Java" is to 
favor composition over inheritance where possible.  It kind of boils down to 
the ideas of "is a" and "has a".


> Now an interesting thing about Java from a Smalltalk programmer. He had 
> switched to Java 'cos that's where the money is, but given is druthers 
> would much preferred to have continued in Smalltak where he claimed he 
> could achieve the same in much more concise coding.
>

Everyone has their preferences.  I am biased toward COBOL and enjoy coding 
it much more than Java, but from what I have seen of OOCOBOL it is much too 
wordy compared to Java.  The idea of perceived power of course is a whole 
different thing.

> Of course as a total topic, we are completely screwed with the J4/PL22.4 
> approach, producing the barest essentials for Collections/Dictionaries - 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well many of the business applcations today are all about the web. The 
question is not whether OOCOBOL's "all" is equivalent to Java's "all".  It 
certainly is possible to code classes equivalent to Java in OOCOBOL, but the 
point is the Java programmer already has this huge, powerful library at his 
disposal.  Someone already used to that will look at COBOL and see it as 
very far down the power curve.  Not saying it is true but it is a 
perception.

> When it comes to doing your thing with M/F OO - we aren't just talking 
> about Debits and Credits or Inventory Control. My application was, 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

Java is far from being a dead duck.  It is however not a silver bullet. 
Recoding COBOL functionality manually in Java is a great opportunity for 
introducing new bugs.  Also being somewhatt base upon C syntax Java is 
harder to read and maintain than COBOL, at least in my experience.  Of 
course I have much more experience with COBOL than with Java.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-19T06:13:25+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cehltF27c4g4U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3r33f$i9j$4@reader1.panix.com> <x3d8m.11614$nL7.6305@newsfe18.iad> <S4idnY3QSfY1ffzXnZ2dnUVZ_tWdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> See comments interspersed below.
>
…[89 more quoted lines elided]…
> possible.  It kind of boils down to the ideas of "is a" and "has a".

I don't think inheritance breaks encapsulation, necessarily. The base class 
is still encapsulated. Extending it makes its methods "visible" but that 
doesn't,of itself, violate encapsulation. Overriding a super class method in 
a subordinate class doesn't violate encapsulation in either of the two 
classes, because both the current override and the super class are still 
intact and available for legitimate use.

I've never really thought about that.It seems to me more academic than 
important. Even if you're right and encapsulation is technically broken by 
inheritance, I don't think it is important enough to make me NOT inherit, if 
inheriting was the obvious best path... I agree that inheritance can be 
overused, but there are times when it is obviously the right way to go, too.

>
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think that's fair comment. We do all have preferences. I too enjoy coding 
COBOL, but I have come to enjoy coding C# more.  The fact that COBOL can 
support OO is one of the things keeping it alive at the moment. For around a 
decade I coded OO COBOL and found it very useful. BUT, the other OO 
languages do it better (and slicker, leaner, and meaner...) so it makes it 
hard to justify using OO COBOL, once the decision is taken ot move to OO and 
knowing that the language for OO Environments like .NET is really pretty 
irrelevant. If the source code doesn't matter, it is sensible to choose 
whatever is quickest and most comfortable to write. If I can write 300 lines 
instead of 2000, then that's a no-brainer (at least as far as I'm 
concerned). (I believe this is a major factor in the success of scripting 
languages like PHP and Python. They are quick to write and they are 
powerful.)


>> Of course as a total topic, we are completely screwed with the
>> J4/PL22.4 approach, producing the barest essentials for
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The number of classes available is only one measure of power. .NET 3.5 
offers around 80,000 classes. They certainly do make life much easier and 
increase productivity. You can bet that whatever you want to do there is 
probably a class that does it.

>> When it comes to doing your thing with M/F OO - we aren't just
>> talking about Debits and Credits or Inventory Control. My
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> with Java.

That is the general reaction when COBOL people encounter Java. It really 
comes down to practice and learning curve. As experience grows, so does 
facility and what once seemed "hard" becomes second nature. I agree that 
there is not much point in recoding COBOL in Java. The two fundamental 
paradigms are different. If you are committed to OO and therefore using 
Java, it would still make sense to refactor your COBOL code into OO COBOL 
rather than Java. (it is simpler to keep the COBOL than to translate it, 
although there are some very good programs that can convert COBOL to Java.)

Encapsulate it, wrap it and forget about it. Carry on with Java development.

In the component based world, once a component is wrapped, the language it 
is written in is really irrelevant. I'm generating a Data Access Layer 
comprised of OO COBOL components for someone at the moment.  These 
components will be used to manage and provide separation for a DB with a 
couple of hundred tables on it. The interesting thing is that the DAL 
components (in OO COBOL) will be used by SQL stored procedures and by VB 
application code, and possibly, by COBOL. Client knows the components are 
written (generated) in COBOL and has no problem with it. He doesn't care, 
and neither should he. The components expose methods  and functionality 
which he needs, and those are invocable from any language which implements 
the COM interface. VB does, so that's all right then... :-)

I do plan to have the Toolset generate this code in C# at some future date, 
but right now, it generates around 3000 lines of COBOL which encapsulates 
all possible access ( backward and forward sequential and skip-sequential, 
and random, for GET, and Insert, Update, and Delete for PUT)  to a given set 
of related, normalized tables. (A component is generated to support and 
manage each given Tableset. These components do construction and 
deconstruction of application buffers, type conversions between database 
types and COBOL (especially dates...), handle database connections, and 
manage multiple instances of themselves running GET and PUT threads... it is 
all really good fun... :-)

I don't really think arguments about programming languages mean much any 
more.

It is all about functionality, and encapsulating it so it can be deployed 
anywhere, and used by anything.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

```
<snip>

> I don't think inheritance breaks encapsulation, necessarily. The base 
> class is still encapsulated. Extending it makes its methods "visible" but 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
From "Effective Java" 1st edition by Joshua Bloch, item 14:

"Inheritance is a powerful way to achieve code reuse, but it is not always 
the best tool for the job.  Used inappropriayely, it leads to fragile 
software.  It is safe to use inheritance within a package, where the 
subclass and the superclass implementation are under the control of the same 
programmers.  It is also safe to use inheritance when extending classes 
specifically designed and documented for extension.  Inheriting from 
ordinary concrete classes across package boundaries, however, is dangerous. 
As a reminder this book uses the word "inheritance" to mean implementation 
inheritance (when one class extends another).  The problems discussed in 
this item do not apply to interface inheritance (when a class implements an 
interface or where one interface extends another).

Unlike method invocation, inheritance breaks encapsulation [Snyder86].  In 
other words, a subclass depends on the implementation details of its 
superclass for its proper function.  The superclass's implementation may 
change from release to release, and if it does, the subclass may break, even 
though its code has not been touched.  As a consequence a subcalss must 
evolve in tandem with its superclass, unless the superclass'e authors have 
designed and documented it specifically for the purpose of being extended."

Block follow this with and example.  It doesn't sound academic to me.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-19T16:18:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h402s7020ov@news7.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<WPGdndh7aZ-Crv7XnZ2dnUVZ_sSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3r33f$i9j$4@reader1.panix.com> <x3d8m.11614$nL7.6305@newsfe18.iad> <S4idnY3QSfY1ffzXnZ2dnUVZ_tWdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7cehltF27c4g4U1@mid.individual.net> <WPGdndh7aZ-Crv7XnZ2dnUVZ_sSdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> 
> Unlike method invocation, inheritance breaks encapsulation [Snyder86]. 

The reference is (presumably) to Alan Snyder's talk "Encapsulation and
Inheritance in Object-Oriented Programming Languages" at OOPSLA '86.
While Snyder's thesis is well-known (or at least widely cited) and
important, OO language theory has had more than 20 years to reconsider
it; and there seems to be a widespread (though not universal)
conclusion that while it might be a useful starting point, it's
overstated.

And probably more importantly, in the very next year (1987), Barbara
Liskov (who just received the Turing Award from the ACM) published the
Liskov Substitution Principle, which formalizes how type hierarchies
should be constructed to avoid breaking encapsulation. Arguably, that
alone rendered Snyder's thesis moot, or at least incomplete.

> In
> other words, a subclass depends on the implementation details of its 
> superclass for its proper function.

Since every component of a computer system depends on the correct
functioning of other components, this is a tautology.

While the coupling introduced by implementation inheritance is not
academic, it's also not nearly as simple as offhand references to
Snyder, like the one you quoted here, make it sound. Implementation
inheritance (inheriting from types that are not purely abstract) is an
important option for solving many problems.
```

- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3r33f$i9j$4@reader1.panix.com> <x3d8m.11614$nL7.6305@newsfe18.iad> <S4idnY3QSfY1ffzXnZ2dnUVZ_tWdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7cehltF27c4g4U1@mid.individual.net> <WPGdndh7aZ-Crv7XnZ2dnUVZ_sSdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h402s7020ov@news7.newsguy.com>`

```
Michael Wojcik wrote:
> Charles Hottel wrote:
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That was initially my reaction, too. But I don't think it is important 
enough to argue it at length. That was what I meant by "academic".


> And probably more importantly, in the very next year (1987), Barbara
> Liskov (who just received the Turing Award from the ACM) published the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> functioning of other components, this is a tautology.

Ain't that the truth... :-)

>
> While the coupling introduced by implementation inheritance is not
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> important option for solving many problems.

Thanks Michael. As always, erudite and interesting:-)

This whole discussion has made me think about it more. I tried to remember 
when I last invoked a super class and the answer is "never". But that is not 
to say I wouldn't, if the situation warranted it. Most of the time, the 
stuff I write is small classes which are intended for wrapping as 
components. If a method needs to be added, I would put it into the Class 
rather than extend and inherit the class into something new.

May not be the ideal theoretical way, but it works for me. I should add that 
it is extremely rare for me to need to add a method to a Class 
"retroactively".

I think a lot comes down to design. I like interactive iterative design 
(Agile style...) so methods could be being added to a class, as use cases 
are iteratively evaluated and worked through. However, it is very rare for a 
class to need further amendment once the time box has ended.

I should add the disclaimer that what works for me has been evolved over 
years of OO development. I don't think that what I do should necessarily be 
emulated by everyone else, I don't believe it is the only way, and I'm also 
always trying to improve the approach.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-07-18T16:15:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sQr8m.13473$8B7.3225@newsfe20.iad>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<S4idnY3QSfY1ffzXnZ2dnUVZ_tWdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3r33f$i9j$4@reader1.panix.com> <x3d8m.11614$nL7.6305@newsfe18.iad> <S4idnY3QSfY1ffzXnZ2dnUVZ_tWdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:

> Java supports inheritance.  Each class can 'extend' a single class.  This is 
> referred to as implementation inheritance. Also each class can impement as 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I paused on your use of the word 'extend' which you specifically put in 
quotes. Let's see if we are on the same wavelength.

In COBOLese are you referring to :-

Class A inherits from BASE (with Fujitsu that's FJBase)
-----------------------------------------------------

I found that I could ignore a reference to Base in N/E. However when I 
did some reading of the latest on-line manual for 5.1 I noted that if 
you don't use Base (which is what handles 'Invoke ClassK "new" returning 
os-ClassK, you face the possibility of errors which Base and its sub 
Behavior cover; that 'pair' on a typo on a method name will return 
"Class Q doesn't understand 'Ive-maid-a-typo' - should have been "made" 
not "maid". So scotch that idea, I've added Base back in.).

Is this what you mean by 'extend' or is it something entirely different :-

Class A inherits from Base
Class B inherits from Class A
Class C inherits from Class B
Class D etc....... you can go on and on

Given a method in Class A "DisplayThe Time", each of B, C and D can :-

invoke super "displayTheTime"

Now the word 'extend' as used in N/E V 3.1

We've got those four classes above. This one might have had appeal to 
the gent who doesn't change components but introduces a new one. It 
becomes apparent that you need a new method and you either want to add 
it into Class A or put it in Class B.

Non-standard but N/E has - Class X "extends" Class A - it slots 
immediately between A and B, so D, C and B have automatic access to the 
methods in A, plus the new Class X methods.

I thought that was a somewhat neat feature - forget about it, the syntax 
has been excluded, and doesn't appear in V5 :-)

One other useful thing N/E has, and again non-Standard, is the 
following; I wont quote the specific syntax, (with several options for 
inclusion or exclusion), otherwise it takes too long to write and read, 
but you can have :-

Class A inherits from Base
Class B inherits from Class A WITH DATA
Class C inherits from Class B WITH DATA

Global Working storage used in either Factory or Object/Instance can 
generate copyfiles. You require both, even if one area is empty. To 
achieve this the classes must be compiled separately, top to bottom.

Using the correct syntax when Class A is compiled, there are two copy 
files generated, even if one is empty :-

ClassA.CLS (CLS = Class or Factory)
ClassA.INS (INS = Instance or Object)

If Class B is passing on Global data to C, when B is compiled, then you 
get a repeat :-

ClassB.CLS (CLS = Class or Factory)
ClassB.INS (INS = Instance or Object)

Assuming the original 'Global' in Class A had 10 variables and Class B 
adds its own 5 variables, then the cumulative effect when compiling 
ClassC C with the inclusion files, is that it now has access to the 15 
variables.

Whether a Dialog, COBOL record or row from a DB table, that you are 
editing, then you are ONLY changing the 'record' in the first class that 
specifies it. The inclusion copyfiles are solely there so that the 
sub-classes well compile and wont hiccup when they see a line "Add 10 to 
Customer-Kounter"; the addition of 10 only appears in the "record" in 
Class A, using the example above.

As yo said you can abuse inheritance; nothing to stop you having a 
'chain' if sub-classes from B to Z! I think the best I have achieved is 
perhaps one sub-class inheriting; I might possibly have had two 
sub-classes inheriting, but I can't recall. You really have to put on 
your thinking cap to achieve this. The compiler vendors, they have put 
tremendous thought into their hierarchies, and have skilled staff 
covering different areas/topics to achieve this in a smooth fashion.

For those not comfortable with OO - a little explanation is in order. 
Given an hierarchy, with or without data inheritance. Assume you had 
Classes A through H - you invoke ClassH "new" returning os-ClassH (an 
instance of the object), the lowest in your chain which now has access 
to all methods up through the family tree, and data if you have coded 
"inherits with Data". On particular aspect of the application may not 
want to dig down to the depth of Class H, it only needs to start at 
Class E going upwards - invoke Class E "new" returning os-ClassE - that 
instance now has access to A through D, using only those methods 
relevant to this separate invocation.

I personally only tried it once, but inheritance is a neat way of 
getting at support classes and overriding an existing method in an M/F 
class. Read up on it and was puzzled, couldn't figure it out. But 
nothing could really be easier. Based on the corrosion system I was 
designing at the time, the user as a throwaway remark, said "It would be 
nice if we had a record of the last Vessel/Item that we were working on, 
before we quit for the day".

I really enjoy Treeviews as much as Listviews. So I designed one :-

Level 0 - Oil/Gas Plant (Root)
Level 1 - Vessel (think boilers - supervised by Alberta Boiler's Branch
				 here in Alberta)
Level 2 - Item ( a specific pipe attached to a Vessel - and that's where
			susceptible points on a pipe are paint-marked to
				take corrosion readings).

Whether or not the Treeview is expanded or contracted, what is viewed 
has a ball icon against each displayed element - RED not yet 
inspected/incomplete, or GREEN actioned, (this being applicable to the 
CURRENT inspection).  Now I got it figured out pretty good, but came 
unstuck on how to change from RED to GREEN. Couldn't seem to find an 
appropriate method. So I asked M/F Newbury and either Steve Biggs or 
David Sands produced the answer, with an example. "You have to write 
your own method which overrides the standard existing method to get what 
you want". I checked out the syntax of the M/F GUI method, then writing 
a new separate method and changing the standard syntax where necessary. 
I had what I wanted. So I don't invoke the appropriate Treeview class -
I have :-

	MyTreeviewIcon inherits from (M/F)Treeview

invoke MyTreeviewIcon "new" returning os-MyTreeview

os-MyTreeview still allows access to all the Treeview methods, but it is 
my method that gets the wake-up call to change icons, NOT the routine in 
M/F's Treeview.

It's an interesting topic and one we covered back in softwaresimple.com 
when roughly a dozen of us were discussing OO COBOL - 'Invoking Up and 
Down". Given a set of classes with inheritance, and this philosophy I'm 
sure must apply to all other OO languages :-

a) To point upwards - from the class you are in, say Class K,

		invoke SUPER "doThis"

and the search is upwards through J to A for the method - and you sure 
get an error message if you made a typo on the method name.

b) to point downwards - from the class you are in and it is Classs K,

		invoke SELF "doThis"

which means take a method from within Class K; however, assuming your 
inheritance hierarchy was down to Class P, that invoke would go to the 
lowest, Class P, starting a search and eventually find the method it 
wants in Class K. (Initial reaction it looks klutzy but it is a valid 
approach and works).  Why does it go downwards initially - assume you 
had a method "doThis-2" in Class M. The invoke is a SELF invoke :-

		invoke self "doThis-2"

Down to the bottom Class P and search upwards now looking for "doThis2" 
which it finds in Class M.

So having covered the above, when you specify "extend" are we talking 
apples and apples or apples and a banana. In OO theory we should be 
talking the same language.
> 
> Well many of the business applcations today are all about the web. The 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> perception.

Very true. But as one wise young man said, about five years back, "I'm 
not putting our Payroll on the Web !". Like any other tool the web can 
be abused, with no real justification for going that route. On-line 
banking where I can transfer amounts to utilities, property taxes, pay 
plastic cards, gas, electricity etc, - a real dream. I have only written 
one or two cheques this year.

Reading your comments above, the guy I was with on Corrosion would have 
reacted, "I like it ! I like t ! I must have one !". Really. He is away 
from base a minimum of 7 to 8 hours solid driving from Calgary, anywhere 
in the boonies where there's an oil plant. Operates from the back of a 
van fitted out with a desk, cosy chair, ultrasonic screen one or two 
computers and a generator chugging away outside the van to give him 
electricity. For what purpose would he want the Internet even if it was 
possible for an ISP to cover him - rush the stuff back to base where it 
could be input, update his DB (COBOL files) and produce a summary. a 
one-man band operator with a succession of technicians helping one at a 
time and a secretary. It still has to wait for him, the perfectionist, 
to vet the summaries, do a bit of adjusting, and then print final reports.

He and I parted company after I attended the J4 Meeting as Newbury in 
2000. Having reflected on the above, made me recall Mr. Dashwood the 
Abitrator who has a parchment to prove it. Glorious mis-read of what I 
once told him. But always quick to react to e-mails here, on any topic, 
but certainly those from me, made an ill advised assessment based on the 
shorthand version I had written. Left to the judgment of Mr. Kiwi I 
would not have "passed GO and collect $200". He would have recommended I 
pick up the "Go to Jail card" !

In summary - Very successful company started by four technicians each 
with 25% of the shares. Never agreed, board motions were FOR : 2 AGAINST 
: 2. I programmed in Datapoint walking away having done my thing, not 
knowing the above subsequently happened. (I made good money at a fixed 
price) - well, I thought it was a good price, and certainly beats $0 !).

I had three successive candidates approaching me with the same line, "I 
don't have a computer but you do. How about you program it. I'll go 
contact clients and once we make money you will get paid too". Whoopy 
Doo! I turned the first two down but 'KK' was the third and the 
technical brains behind the operation. Pleasant, I still like him, 
knowledgeable, well respected in his field and innovative when it comes 
to using a computer. But on the downside an absolute German 
perfectionist. Check your memory box Mr. Dashwood, "He had more support 
staff than you have had hot Sunday dinners", it comes up again at the end.

We team up on a verbal agreement - B-I-G  M-I-S-T-A-K-E-. I got handouts 
in sums like $123, $340, $147, no consistency and no regular handouts. 
We parted in anger but got back together again some two years later, 
when he wanted even more changes to the application. He spoke to his 
lawyer first who told him he had been screwing me. This time around, "Do 
want a minority shareholding in my company or a sub-contract ?". Knowing 
the ridiculous way he spent money, new vans new computers etc., at every 
turn - this junior shareholder would have seen $0 for his annual 
dividend. I opted for a monthly figure. He came up with $2K per month - 
again $2K beats $0K and with no contacts had no idea what a fair going 
rate was. Still this bloody idiot, ME, didn't twig get it in WRITING. He 
completely pissed me around between OO in Net Express and the clunky 
RM/COBOL system to make changes which he must have NOW, but preferabnly 
yesterday, for a particular oil company. We were both angry at one 
another, him thinking he was paying unnecessary fees, and again me 
pissed off because I didn't get the $2K a month on a regular basis. I 
was the last in the pile of invoices for his vendors/suppliers. We 
parted in 2000 and not amicably.

Sure, as you suggested he may have used Excel - he would be stubborn 
enough to kill himself making it work, using macros. Chances are he may 
have for a period got in a bright-eyed-bushy-tailed youg Java-ite. The 
latter would eventually have seen the light - there was no future in a 
three-person organization. I have no idea what he did/does but 
eventually tracked him down through the Internet. Somebody in Calgary 
had put out a questionnaire to NDT companies, asking basic questions, 
which they published on the Web. The entry might be five years old, (no 
dates mentioner) but this was KK's entry and the CAPITALIZATION is mine :-

Name of Company : bbbbbbbbb
Owner : KK
Staff : APPROXIMATELY 3
Sales : $500,000 - $1,000,000

See the approximately three - he still has more technicians than you 
have had hot dinners !

So Mr. Abitrator :-

(a) Did I hold this guy back - or pissing around did he hold me back ?
(b) Looking at the sales, whom do you think shafted whom ? (BTW - I'm 
now living off the grand sum of $1,000 per month from pension - Yes, I 
have the comma in the correct position)

My wife will give a very quick answer to both, plus a smart business 
lady I met, walking the dogs. I have Jaydee (= Jimmy's Dog) and as a 
replacement, for Emma my beloved border collie, and chosen by my wife, a 
Cairn terrier. She's cute and smart - but I ask you - a 6 Ft man with a 
leash to something only some six inches above the ground ?????

I hardly knew her but the business lady concluded, "You are too nice. 
And if as you describe he shafted you, there must have been something 
latent in his nature which allowed him to do it". Eileen would agree 
with her - perhaps women are smarter than men :-) . Hadn't thought about 
the "I'm nice bit" - too easy or lenient perhaps.

Nah ! Mr. Abitrator. Pull the parchment off the wall and go take a 
refresher course on arbitration.

Charlie - before I go both you and Pete made the dumbest suggestions to 
Paul about solutions to his COBOL to MS Excel problem. I'll do an update 
on that having spoken to Paul on Monday or Tuesday night to see what he 
has achieved and which route he wants to go. Brifely after the kerfuffel 
in here, I received an e-mail "I'm stuck" - that's how I got into the 
picture at a personal level

Jimmy
```

```
picking up on something negative 30 years from now, as they pore through 
old mail, there's your ego has to be protected. But it will be 
impossible to not make a reference sometimes - so as a get around how 
about I refer to you as Our Resident Expert ? More on that vein will 
follow, apart from an MS Excel explanation.

Do you perhaps get the impression that I'm a little wound up, and not 
just about above topic - your first thoughts on above topic were 
irritating, granted you were unaware of the full story, but not 
insulting, which is the vein you are currently replying in. (Your claws 
are showing ducky ).

But gardening is much more interesting than shooting the crap with you. 
I'll get back in due course.
```

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
> news:xMGdnUGlQs5KqP7XnZ2dnUVZ_hmdnZ2d@earthlink.com...
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
OK didn't bother me you used 'Tree' as opposed to 'Iheritance 
Hierarchy'; in fact I missed it but as a description either would uffice.

So within Java the Java Class 'Object' serves perhaps the same purpose 
as M/F's Base, but you don't have to explicitly specify the 'Base'. As I 
indicated don't use a reference to 'Base' and you have additiional work 
to do to capture errors terminating 'upwards' if Class A is the senior 
or super class. I can't think of an occasioin where you would want to 
break the rules, (i.e. not use Base), but you never know....

Just for Info :

Base Class Methods
-------------------
initializeclass
isMetaClass Returning lnkBool

Object/Instance Methods
-----------------------
Methods
addSpy Using lnkObject lnkMethod
addSpyCallback Using lnkCallback
basicCopy Returning result
becomes Using lnkObject
class Returning result
className Returning aString
CompatibilityError Returning lnkResult
connect Using lnkOutputComponent lnkInputComponent lnkSocketId
connectTo Using lnkInputComponent lnkSocketId
containsCharacters Returning lnkbool
containsDBCSCharacters Returning lnkbool
copy Returning result
copyContents Using lnkObject
Returning lnkBool
dealloc Using lnkPtr
deepCopy Returning lnkCopy
deepfinalize Returning defaultreturn
deepFinalizeWithDepth Using lnkDepth
Returning defaultreturn
defineInSignal Using lnkSignalId lnkSocketId lnkMethodName
defineInSocket Using lnkSocketId
defineOutSignal Using lnkSignalId
description Returning aString
disconnectSignal Using lnkSignalId lnkComponent
dispatchEvent Using lnkEvent
Returning lnkResult
display
displayRecursion
doesNotUnderstand Using p1 p2 p3 p4 p5 p6 p7 <--- this is 
activated when 						you have a typo on a
						method name - searching,
						it can't find the method
Returning result
doEvent Using lnkEventIndex
Returning lnkResult
doIdleProcessing Returning lnkbool
duplicatesNotOK
duplicatesOK
equal Using anotherstring
Returning lnkresult
finalize Returning result
getSignalHandlers Using lnkSignalId
Returning lnkSignalHandlers
getTraceLevel Returning lnktraceLevel
hash Returning ahashvalue
isBitmap Returning lnkbool
isClass Returning lnkbool
isColor Returning lnkbool
isComp5 Returning lnkBool
isCompX Returning lnkBool
isDBCSCharacterArray Returning lnkbool
isDBCSEnvironment Returning lnkbool
isDesktop Returning Bool
isIcon Returning lnkbool
isInputSignal Using lnkSocketId lnkSignalId
Returning lnkBool
isMenu Returning lnkbool
isMenubar Returning lnkbool
isMenuParent Returning lnkbool
isMetaClass Returning lnkBool
isOutputSignal Using lnkSignalId
Returning lnkBool
isPopUp Returning lnkbool
isPrinter Returning lnkbool
isProcessItem Returning lnkbool
isSequenced Returning lnkbool
isSubMenu Returning lnkbool
isTypeOf Using lnkClass
Returning lnkbool
isWindow Returning lnkbool
makeComponent
malloc Using lnkSize
Returning lnkPtr
prepareToBeSaved
raiseException Using errnumber
Returning result
raiseExceptionWithText Using errnumber lnkstring1 lnkstring2 lnkstring3 
lnkstring4 lnkstring5 lnkstring6
Returning result
raiseExceptionWithTextCollection Using errnumber lnktextcollection
Returning result
raiseExceptionWithTextZ Using errnumber lnkstring1 lnkstring2 lnkstring3 
lnkstring4 lnkstring5 lnkstring6
Returning result
readyForApplicationStart
reallydeepfinalize Returning defaultreturn
registerBaseMessageFile Returning msgfh
removeDelegate
removeSignalDependant Using lnkObject
removeSpy Using lnkComponent
removeSpyCallback Using lnkComponent lnkCallback
setDelegate Using aDelegate
setEvent Using lnkEventIndex lnkEventHandler
setEventTo Using lseventindex lsReceiver lsmethodName lsParameter1 
lsParameter2
setTraceLevel Using lnktraceLevel
signal Using lnkSignalId lnkP1 lnkP2 lnkP3 lnkP4 lnkP5 lnkP6 lnkP7 lnkP8 
lnkP9 lnkP10
Returning lnkSignalReturnValue
signalString Using lnkSignalId lnkP1 lnkP2 lnkP3 lnkP4 lnkP5 lnkP6 lnkP7 
lnkP8 lnkP9 lnkP10
Returning lnkSignalReturnValue
size Returning alength
sizeInBytes Returning acollectionsize
species Returning lnkClass
translateEvent Using lnkEventIndex lnkReceiver lnkTranslatedEvent
translateEventToSignal Using lnkEventId lnksignalObject lnkSignalId 
lnkParameter
whatdidntIUnderstand Returning lnkString

Behavior Class Methods = None
----------------------------
Object/Instance Methods :
------------------------
Instance Methods
The list below does not include methods inherited from Base. For these 
methods, click the button below:

Base

allClasses Returning result
allInstances Returning result
allmethods Returning aMethodList
baseClass Returning classhandle
basicnew Returning result
description Returning lnkString
getFileName Returning aFileName
GetNumberOfObjects Returning lnkNumObjects
handlefromfilename Using lnkFilename
Returning lnkClass
handlefromfilenameAndClassName Using lnkFilename lnkClassName
Returning lnkClass
immediatesubclasses Returning lnkimmediatesubclasses
implements Using lnkMessage
Returning lnkBool
initializeclass
initializeClassExtension
isClass Returning lnkbool
isimmediatesubclass Using lnkclass
Returning lnkbool
isMetaClass Returning lnkBool
isSubclass Using lnkclass
Returning lnkbool
Name Returning aString
new Returning result 	<---- here we go, sub-class Behavior 
has the 						"new" method to create instances
newIndexedInstance Using lnkNewSize
Returning lnkCollection
respondsTo Using lnkMessage
Returning lnkBool
superclass Returning lnkclass

Goes on a bit doesn't it ? Be nice if rather than highlight and getting 
a tediously long list I could paraphrase it. (Note it doesn't show

Jimmy
```

```
I certainly believe in inheritance and use it all the time.



>Certainly hierarchical
> classes were central to Smalltalk, culminating in Smalltalk 80. Micro
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
> you making the point that Java is a dead duck.

Again I don't recall the context. Just to be clear, I do not consider Java 
to be a dead duck. I see Java being around for many years yet.  Certainly 
longer than COBOL.

Java is a true object oriented language and was designed that way from the 
ground up. Although it is generally easy to use, I prefer C# because it 
combines the best features of both Java and C.

Some programmers are moving from Java to C# but it is by no means a 
landslide.

Pete
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

>>This might seem like a really stupid question, but I have never delved
>>into Java. Does Java act as a replacement for Windows, i.e. a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> I certainly believe in inheritance and use it all the time.

Sorry I can't give you the context Pete - I just don't store passing 
interest items to take a crack as somebody later. I just merely remember 
them. Best I can do to put you on track, and you were interested in his 
comments, he went to great lengths to explain the Java syntax "Try and 
Catch" - ring any bells ?
> 
>>Now I'll ask Charlie a question. As a result of a comment you made in
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Now that one was a very recent thread. Obviously I misread Charlie, as 
he has already indicated, and as I thought you were agreeing with him, 
obviously I misread you to.

> Java is a true object oriented language and was designed that way from the 
> ground up. Although it is generally easy to use, I prefer C# because it 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> landslide.

We all misread and put our interpretation into what we read - and at 
times you sure have fun misreading me. Recent example - The services.exe 
problem. I said I was delighted to get an update from Microsoft. From 
your interpretation you immediately informed me that MS don't single out 
individual users to make updates. Well I bloody well knew that ! As a 
result of an MS scheduled update, and possibly my error report triggered 
an investigation, they fixed it quickly and were able to include in the 
latest general update.

I thought I read SP3 was supposed to be the last for XP. I received an 
update within the last week. Perhaps they have to drag it out because 
Vista is such a balls up - waiting for introduction of a smoother OS.

And on that one in general - a thought which has always been ticking in 
the back of my mind over the years. You and I are convinced, totally 
convinced, not partially convinced, that OO is the way to go. Now am I 
again being naive that MS use OO to develop their OS and packages ? 
Granted their's is a HUGE operation, and somethings they have done 
superbly, but so many continuous updates/corrections. Do they chuck the 
old OS away and start from scratch ? Where does the reuse come in ? 
Possibly the new thing has a new philosophy as to how it is conceived 
and assembled - but routines (i.e. methods) have been proven in older 
versions - save and reuse what works and dovetail into the new OS. 
Perhaps they do - but why still consistently updates/corrections ?

Whatever they do is a 'downer' for OO and an easy way for developers to 
say, "I'm not getting into OO. If MS can't get it right, why should I try".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-19T08:15:23+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ceoqjF27belqU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3r33f$i9j$4@reader1.panix.com> <x3d8m.11614$nL7.6305@newsfe18.iad> <7cedhpF27adhkU1@mid.individual.net> <xxo8m.28355$ZU1.26496@newsfe16.iad>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> out individual users to make updates. Well I bloody well knew that !

It read like you thought they had seized upon your error report and 
responded specifically. From contacts I have at MicroSoft I know that is not 
how it works. I didn't mean to offend you or teach you to suck eggs, I 
simply responded to what you wrote. Sorry for the misunderstanding.

> As a result of an MS scheduled update, and possibly my error report
> triggered an investigation, they fixed it quickly and were able to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Vista is such a balls up - waiting for introduction of a smoother OS.

Vista is actually not bad now. Things move on. It WAS a disaster (and it 
still isn't my personal favourite) but the general concensus seems less 
fiery than it was.
>
> And on that one in general - a thought which has always been ticking
> in the back of my mind over the years. You and I are convinced,
> totally convinced, not partially convinced, that OO is the way to go.

Jimmy, I don't know why it is but you consistently seem to take something 
and make a position out of it that just is NOT true for me. It's like you 
are only seeing blacks and whites... here's the reality:

1: I definitely believe OOD and OOP using an iterative and interactive Agile 
style methodology are GENERALLY the most effective way to develop computer 
systems.

2: I PREFER to write OO programs and build components from OO classes than 
to code a procedural solution.

3: There will be some circmstances where OO is NOT the way to go. (Batch 
processing, for example)

So, given the above, how do you arrive at "You and I are convinced, totally 
convinced, not partially convinced, that OO is the way to go." :-)

Please don't include me in your generalizations and confine yourself to 
things I actually said if you want to talk about me...  (I have a feeling 
we've had this conversation before... never mind. :-))

It WOULD be fair to say that I am a "fan" of OO, but I am seldom "totally 
convinced" of anything.


> Now am I again being naive that MS use OO to develop their OS and
> packages ?

Not naive. They certainly do. You only have to use an object browser on the 
windows system directories to see it.


> Granted their's is a HUGE operation, and somethings they
> have done superbly, but so many continuous updates/corrections.

I think you have to look at what is OS and what is Applications like Word, 
the Browser, and so on.


>Do
> they chuck the old OS away and start from scratch ? Where does the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Perhaps they do - but why still consistently updates/corrections ?

I think its a fair question. I don't know the answer. I'm pretty sure they 
DO reuse a good deal of stuff but there are certainly ongoing and (it seems) 
never ending updates, patches, and hot fixes. It doesn't bother me as long 
as they work... :-)
>
> Whatever they do is a 'downer' for OO and an easy way for developers
> to say, "I'm not getting into OO. If MS can't get it right, why
> should I try".

I seriously doubt that.

Developers are NOT looking for excuses to avoid OO (OK, there may be some 
COBOL developers who remain sceptical, but even that is changing); millions 
of them have voted with their feet and that's why everything is about 
classes and objects. ALL of the modern languages support OO. It is the most 
successful programming paradigm ever invented. I honestly don't think it 
matters whether MS are perceived to have programming problems (and it is 
arguable that they even do...). Having developed applications both ways, 
there is no comparison as far as I'm concerned. And the majority of the 
programming going on in the world today is employing that paradigm.

OO is really not the end point though. It is when you take the next step and 
push the classes into components that things get "interesting".

To see the requirement of MS to maintain their software as being a bad 
reflection on OO, is like blaming music because some people sing badly...

Pete
```

```
On Sun, 19 Jul 2009 05:02:57 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>I certainly believe in inheritance and use it all the time.

We have a funny language.   We translate "all the time" automatically
to something less than every instant of eternity.    In the context of
writing a new object, (excluding the built-in inheritance for this
discussion)  I often write it expecting inheritance to be used, if
desired - but whether it is used or not is a different story.
```

```
> programmer says : "Java has more than 35,000 methods listed in 4100
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

less is better than more (an old unix joke, see [1])

In a COBOL environment I'm stuck to my collection of subroutines (COBOL,
Assembler), maybe about 300. Easier to remember than 35,000 methods or
4100 classes.

[1] ?more? is a Unix utility to paginate output, ?less? is a Linux
utility to do likewise but offers more options than 'more'.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-18T13:26:03-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3t0nq22e1h@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> A Java programmer and a COBOL programmer  meet in a bar.  The Java 
> programmer says : "Java has more than 35,000 methods listed in 4100 classes 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> If you are the COBOL programmer what would your answer be?

"If that's your sole, or main, criterion for evaluating a programming
language, there's not much point in continuing this discussion."

COBOL implementations can generally call external libraries. If the
functionality needed to solve a particular problem isn't built into
the language, it's probably available in an external library. If it
isn't, *then* you'd have some grounds for evaluating your language
choice on the basis of available functionality.

Far better would be criteria like "how well do I understand this
language?", "how elegantly can I solve my problem in this language?",
"how difficult will it be to maintain a solution written in this
language?".

The question is even more ridiculous in the .Net case, since .Net
COBOL implementations can use nearly everything in the .Net Framework.

(Incidentally, Javadoc is not a "library". It's a documentation
system. The standard Java classes reside in the Java Runtime Environment.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-19T13:57:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U0K8m.380855$fo6.245771@en-nntp-09.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <h3t0nq22e1h@news3.newsguy.com>`

```
> Charles Hottel wrote:
>> A Java programmer and a COBOL programmer  meet in a bar.  The Java
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

"Language wars" are almost always useless and are often (if not usually) 
comparing apples to flying saucers (not just oranges).  Certainly comparing 
languages based on how many "methods" and "classes" it has, is such a 
comparison.

If I got into such a (useless in my opinion) discussion, I would come back with 
(equally useless) replies as,

1) COBOL has 47 verbs in the 2002 ISO Standard and and 75 Intrinsic Funcitons in 
the same ISO Standard.  How many verbs and how many intrinsic funcitons does the 
latest ISO (or ANSI) Standard for Java have?

2) COBOL supports both dynamic and static CALLs (not invocations of methods). 
Does Java?

3) Native COBOL (wthout any calls, invokes, or other APIs) supports fixed and 
floating editing symbols as well as alphanumeric, national, and numeric editing, 
simply by definiing the correct picture string for the edited item.  Can you do 
this in Java without using any external methods or classes?  (FYI, you can do 
this with PL/I or ADA as well)

4) Standard COBOL provides native language support for creating fixed font 
reports and screens.  Can you do this in Java without using any external APIs, 
methods, or classes?  How about SORT and MERGE of sequential files?  For that 
matter, how well can "native" Java handle indexed files (again, without using 
APIs, methods, or classes)

  * * * *

As stated at the beginning of my rpely, I would *ONLY* give these types of 
replies if I couldn't get the other person to admit that comparing languages 
based on the number of methods and classes is "silly" - unless what you want to 
compare is how much "built-in" OO support a programming language has.  (Even in 
that case,  OO COBOL can - where impltented correctly - invoke all the Java 
methods and classes, so that number doesn't mean much even for this type of 
comparison.)
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-21T16:45:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GFq9m.363053$Lo1.208614@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <5ft865ptgfpuc955c781ef7lv8pg1jd067@4ax.com> <Kv2dnQZ1X8uKjvjXnZ2dnUVZ_o-dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <e8mb65ddgasbr1j73vsi51uvir9n7502r1@4ax.com>`

```
At least for the "IBM Mainframe,' it is NOT required (even though it is 
recommended) that your mixed COBOL/Java applications be run under UNIX System 
Services.  See:
  http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg40/2.3.2.2

The section beginning,
 "In limited circumstances, however, you can run an OO application by using 
standard batch JCL (EXEC PGM=COBPROG) or the TSO/E CALL command."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and Java Relative Perceived Power

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-22T08:09:37-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tPCdndihQIzknPrXnZ2dnUVZ_vqdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<6e6dnUXtGYIweP3XnZ2dnUVZ_r2dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <5ft865ptgfpuc955c781ef7lv8pg1jd067@4ax.com> <Kv2dnQZ1X8uKjvjXnZ2dnUVZ_o-dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <e8mb65ddgasbr1j73vsi51uvir9n7502r1@4ax.com> <GFq9m.363053$Lo1.208614@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com>`

```
Note: in this post USS stands for Unix System Services

A quick reading of the web page indicates that the class files must reside 
in the HFS, the BPXBATCH utility must be used and the INCLUDE staments have 
Uxix style paths in them.  Without USS thres is no HFS.  Programs that start 
with 'BPX' are USS utilities.  Path names specify USS files. Therefore it 
seems to me that without USS you cannot do this.

> recommended) that your mixed COBOL/Java applications be run under UNIX 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **Date:** 2009-07-17T15:27:51-07:00

```
DOS 6.2.

I think everything s on here so I can recompile...
after I change the source to output to the screen and not the
printer...

Questions:

this ver of MF COBOL? (editor, debugger, compiler, linker, etc)
2 - Else I could make the changes to the source in a text editor...
but then how to compile and get all the parts together so the program
worked again? (There are many source files it seems to this project)
3 - After I recompile one I guess I have to run some linking
application to sew them all together... or what? (It's been a long
time since I've coded :)
4 - I would guess that he has a file some where (project file) that
the compiler/linker reads to know what to do upon re-compiling etc...
what would the file ext be for such a file
5 - What would be the file name of the compiler?
6 - is the syntax...
CompilerName SourceFileName
followed maybe some switches?
7 - Or is there some great IDE interface I can bring up that will load
the project and make it a little easier?

Thanks for any help.

PS 1

Here are the changes I hope to make...
Change this...
--- snippet start
    SELECT PRINT-FILE
        ASSIGN TO PRINTER.
--- snippet end

To this...
--- Snippet Start
SELECT PRINT-FILE
     ASSIGN TO PRINTFILE
    ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
--- Snippet End

PS 2

to Fred...
You wanted to see the contents of Control.CPY... here it is...

       FD  CONTROL-MASTER-FILE
           LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
           RECORDING MODE IS F
           DATA RECORD IS CONTROL-MASTER-RECORD.

       01  CONTROL-MASTER-RECORD.
           03  CMR-RECORD-KEY.
               05  CMR-RECORD-KEY-9            PIC 9(4).
           03  CMR-MAIN-CONTROL-RECORD.
               05  CMR-CONTROL-DATA.
                   07  CMR-CONTROL-DATA-PT1    PIC X(32).
                   07  CMR-CONTROL-DATA-PT2    PIC X(32).
                   07  CMR-CONTROL-DATA-PT3    PIC X(32).
               05  CMR-CONTROL-DATA-R REDEFINES CMR-CONTROL-DATA.
                   07  CMR-CONTROL-DATA-AREA-1 PIC X(48).
                   07  CMR-CONTROL-DATA-AREA-2 PIC X(47).
                   07  CMR-CONTROL-record-type PIC X.
           03  SIGNON-CONTROL-RECORD REDEFINES CMR-MAIN-CONTROL-
RECORD.
               05  SCR-PASSWORD                    PIC X(10).
               05  SCR-CLEARANCE-LEVEL             PIC X(1).
               05  FILLER                          PIC X(02).
               05  SCR-SIGNON-TITLE                PIC X(25).
               05  FILLER                          PIC X(03).
               05  SCR-DATE-CHANGE                 PIC X(8).
               05  FILLER                          PIC X(47).
```

```
Here is the long and short of this project...

1 - Getting the data out of this old COBOL data base to a comma
delimited file is the goal.

2 - The data base has about 4,000 customer accounts, payment status,
sales commissions, products purchased, bought by, sent to, etc...

3 - I've found another 486 PC and have it working with their old hard
drive as a patch
    till I can get the data out as comma delimited... or fixed field
length... or whatever

4 - I also have the application running under VirtualBox and now have
the application printing to file.

The Current Question:

How to get the data out of the old COBOL data base so that it can be
ported to a more modern application?

(The programmer did not put an option to export data in the
applications menu system and relied on a backup of the entire hard
disk as a recovery method)

Thanks again for everyone's help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to compile MicroFocus COBOL/2 Ver 1.2.29 L2.2 rev 003 - on DOS 6.2 machine ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-19T12:05:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c5d22b6c-4d75-4250-a26c-c9af14b04738@x6g2000prc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9adbbbf6-6744-4902-ba13-10fdd2d4fb54@r2g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <h3r360$i9j$5@reader1.panix.com> <Arb8m.4003$E61.2812@newsfe09.iad> <d0ef257e-4507-45b8-897a-613221d23568@26g2000yqk.googlegroups.com> <0tidnXTiwtSniP_XnZ2dnUVZ_oqdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
On Jul 19, 6:32 am, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
> Mel_3 wrote:
> > Here is the long and short of this project...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> problem.

You appear to be 'sure' based on a complete lack of any evidence. It
is version 1.2 of Cobol/2 and that puts it at around 1985 or 86.

I don't have manuals for Cobol/2 that early, I went to Cobol/2 with
version 2.1. Version 2.4 does have 'rebuild'. While later versions do
allow an indexed file to be converted to sequential the one in 2.4
does not have this option. And 2.1 does not even have rebuild, the 2.1
manual outlines the method of rebuilding or converting by writing your
own program, which is the same as for Level II Cobol. As 1.2 is
between Level II and 2.1 it is most likely the sames as these.

But even if there was a utility to convert to sequential in that
version, and it was still present with the application, this does not
make the output file "text". It will not convert numeric binary or
packed to alphanumeric, nor even make 'sign separate' and interpret
the negative overpunch.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to compile MicroFocus COBOL/2 Ver 1.2.29 L2.2 rev 003 - on DOS 6.2 machine ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-22T09:04:06-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<geSdnSFQycLKgfrXnZ2dnUVZ_tudnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<9adbbbf6-6744-4902-ba13-10fdd2d4fb54@r2g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <h3r360$i9j$5@reader1.panix.com> <Arb8m.4003$E61.2812@newsfe09.iad> <d0ef257e-4507-45b8-897a-613221d23568@26g2000yqk.googlegroups.com> <0tidnXTiwtSniP_XnZ2dnUVZ_oqdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <c5d22b6c-4d75-4250-a26c-c9af14b04738@x6g2000prc.googlegroups.com>`

```
riplin wrote:
> On Jul 19, 6:32 am, "HeyBub" <hey...@NOSPAMgmail.com> wrote:
>> Mel_3 wrote:
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> is version 1.2 of Cobol/2 and that puts it at around 1985 or 86.

But absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. I've worked with four 
PC-oriented COBOL compilers and they all had back-pocket utility programs to 
both extract and create ISAM files.

>
> I don't have manuals for Cobol/2 that early, I went to Cobol/2 with
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> the negative overpunch.

You make a good point. Instead of creating a "text" file, these utilities 
create sequential files, but they are devoid of indexing and positioning 
information found in the ISAM files; they are data only. How you fiddle with 
the fields in each record is up to you.
```
