- **References:** `<6f5da30c-7a93-4a9b-85fb-31a3c90014b7@m11g2000yqh.googlegroups.com> <7cn9o4F286495U1@mid.individual.net> <6c091e87-cc40-48fb-b62f-1717a66ffa0b@a7g2000yqk.googlegroups.com> <7co7toF1kfbhiU1@mid.individual.net> <h473la$ceu$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
Leaving the subject as "ot" - but

For any IBM mainframe COBOL users, if you still have a copy of the "ANS Version 
4" COBOL LRM, you should look up with "D" and "d" meant when specified in a 
COBOL picture clause (with usage DISPLAY-1).

Interestingly enough, software support was left in with OS/VS COBOL, so if you 
still have an OS/VS COBOL compiler, you can try these out.  However, the OS/VS 
COBOL documentation (post "decimalization") does NOT document these.
```

#### ↳ Re: threaded c calling cobol

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-28T13:13:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h4nh5n25j0@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6f5da30c-7a93-4a9b-85fb-31a3c90014b7@m11g2000yqh.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<6f5da30c-7a93-4a9b-85fb-31a3c90014b7@m11g2000yqh.googlegroups.com>`

```
Adev wrote:
> I have c++ program that creates threads, Each thread is to call
> Microfocus cobol program connecting to Oracle db (using oracleoci). I
> receive varierty of errors on execution when the 2nd thread is
> started. Can this be done? And if so what compiler directives etc are
> required.

Regardless of whether your COBOL routines create threads, they must be
compiled for multithreading if they will be called in a multithreaded
program. That means compiling with the -t option, and setting the
appropriate compiler directives - probably including one of the
REENTRANT directives. I can't tell you what to use specifically
without knowing quite a lot more about your application; I suggest you
refer to the documentation.

Are you following all the instructions in the documentation for
calling COBOL from a non-COBOL program? See the chapter "Interfacing
with C Programs". In particular, are you calling cobinit(), cobtidy(),
and cobthreadtidy() in the appropriate places? Are you using cobcall()
to invoke your COBOL routine?

This type of problem can also arise if the C++ program wasn't
correctly compiled and linked for multithreading.
```

### Experience with "cobol-it" cobol?

Hi,
in the time of crisis I have more time to get to know another cobol's.
I have started with cobol-it because it has an option to aid mf-dialect.
This aid is (my experience) not complete, for example "display" is about 
80%, "accept" and "crt status" much fewer.
Is somebody here who is using this cobol and is interested to interchange
his exoeriences with me.

Thanks
```

Hello Vaclav!

22 Jul 09 15:17, Vaclav Snajdr wrote to All:

 VS> in the time of crisis I have more time to get to know another cobol's.
 VS> I have started with cobol-it because it has an option to aid
 VS> mf-dialect. This aid is (my experience) not complete, for example
 VS> "display" is about 80%, "accept" and "crt status" much fewer. Is
 VS> somebody here who is using this cobol and is interested to interchange
 VS> his exoeriences with me.

You will find that Open Cobol v1,1 (released April 09 or later) supports most 
of display and accept for screen section etc and works well.

For help download info etc go to www.opencobol.org or 
http://add1tocobol.com/tiki-index.php

I should point out that CobolIt comes from a old release of Open Cobol prior 
to a large number of upgrades and fixes to the screen section as well as many 
other areas.


Vince
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Experience with "cobol-it" cobol?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-23T11:12:12-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<zZ%9m.413380$fo6.96986@en-nntp-09.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<h477en$f3j$01$1@news.t-online.com> <1248354943@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn>`

```
One ("the"?) major difference between COBOL-IT and OpenCOBOL, is that COBOL-IT 
comes with built-in "user friendly" debugging facilities that are NOT available 
with OC.  If, however, you are used to "debugging" with C and/or GNU software, 
then going to OC would be the better choice.  If, however, you are used to most 
other COBOL interactive debuggers, you will find OC a "real challenge".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Experience with "cobol-it" cobol?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** ihtfp71 <ihtfp71@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-27T09:03:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<47f57da6-a33b-4c98-9a7b-a5eb3a2a91f6@t13g2000yqt.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<h477en$f3j$01$1@news.t-online.com> <1248354943@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <0585ab70-5f88-42ef-8033-6acb53b07e9a@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <1248450735@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn>`

```
On Jul 24, 7:52 pm, "Vince Coen" <VBCoenDesp...@btconnect.com> wrote:
> Hello riplin!
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Vince

This thread was brought to my attention, and I would like to provide
some perspective.  I work with Micro Focus, Fujitsu, and COBOL-IT for
mainframe re-hosting projects plus some local workstation
programming.  I am reasonably familiar with the business directions of
each of these companies because I have been involved in potential
partnering opportunities with each one on different projects. I
formerly worked with CA-Realia and with AcuCOBOL before the
acquisition.  I have not worked with OpenCOBOL and know of only one
major project done with it here in the US.

COBOL-IT was forked from OpenCOBOL in August of 2008. I am told that
there were a lot of bugs and some performance issues that needed to be
fixed before the compiler could pass third party certification.
Resolving those issues consumed many months, but the compiler is now
certified by Oracle (Tuxedo), Red Hat, MySQL, HTWC (XFrame), Sun, HP,
and Syncsort. Certification projects are in progress with DB2 (UDB)
and Oracle Pro*COBOL; there are no known problems but official
certifications have not been issued yet so far as I know. COBOL-IT has
been successfully tested with UniKix, but without a certificate being
issued.

OpenCOBOL has benefitted from the work of COBOL-IT, as Roger While and
COBOL-IT are regularly in touch and many COBOL-IT bug fixes have been
incorporated into OpenCOBOL. Users of the free Standard version of
COBOL-IT for Windows have benefitted as Windows API calls (STDCALL)
are now supported, Microsoft C compilers are supported, and fully
compiled and linked Windows binaries are available for free download.

The goal of COBOL-IT is to release and maintain an open source
compiler that can be used for mission critical applications by large
scale, high performance users in Windows as well as Linux and Unix
environments. This is why multi-threading was added. Small users
rarely need it, but it is critical for large complex applications.

The biggest difference between COBOL-IT and OpenCOBOL is not technical
but business.  Large organizations cannot use unsupported products,
and OpenCOBOL is not an option.  COBOL-IT is providing professional
support on the MySQL model - a free product with a support forum, and
a professional product that requires a support agreement.

Because of this support orientation, COBOL-IT's development schedule
is being driven primarily by the requirements of large customers,
though this will probably change when the first US distributors are
announced later this year, one of which focuses on smaller users.  The
support for multi-threading was a lot of development work but the
result was relatively invisible to most users.  There has also been a
lot of attention given to Windows users recently.

This is also why COBOL-IT has added a debugger and an Eclipse plug-in
to both the standard and the enterprise versions of the compiler.  The
debugger is basic in its features, but it is being regularly worked
on. There is also an external SORT module and SQL pre-compiler
available for MySQL, PostgreSQL and ODBC, though this is a proprietary
extension to the product and there is no free version.

On a technical basis, Micro Focus COBOL, as the market leader for
commercial COBOL, has been the standard for comparison of features,
not OpenCOBOL. If there are deficiencies between MF and COBOL-IT, you
should report it on the forum at www.cobol-it.com.  There have been
some surprisingly quick fixes issued, even for the free version.

Bottom line: I will use COBOL-IT for serious projects, just as I would
use Micro Focus or Fujitsu.  It's up to my clients which one to
choose.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Experience with "cobol-it" cobol?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-27T14:50:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zynbm.403533$Lo1.274389@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<h477en$f3j$01$1@news.t-online.com> <1248354943@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <0585ab70-5f88-42ef-8033-6acb53b07e9a@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <1248450735@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <47f57da6-a33b-4c98-9a7b-a5eb3a2a91f6@t13g2000yqt.googlegroups.com>`

```
I have NO idea who ihtfp71@gmail.com" is and I certainly don't know how he/she 
works with COBOL-IT, but there is LOTS of inaccurate or misleading or at least 
questionable information (IN MY OPINION), in this note.

The most important ones are:

1)  "OpenCOBOL has benefitted from the work of COBOL-IT, as Roger While and 
COBOL-IT are regularly in touch and many COBOL-IT bug fixes have been 
incorporated into OpenCOBOL."
  From following the OC forums and lists, it appears that the ONLY way that OC 
gets "fixes" from COBOL-IT, is when someone (from the OC side) searches the code 
and lets Roger know.  COBOL-IT, has also (on a number of occasions) copied 
"fixes" from OC that were tentative and, in fact, erroneous.  (Check the OC 
forum and list for a variety of case where this has happened)

2) "On a technical basis, Micro Focus COBOL, as the market leader for commercial 
COBOL, has been the standard for comparison of features, not OpenCOBOL. If there 
are deficiencies between MF and COBOL-IT, you
should report it on the forum at www.cobol-it.com.  There have been some 
surprisingly quick fixes issued, even for the free version."
  I agree that MF has features that OC does not, but all reports that I have 
seen indicate that COBOL-IT is "relatively" slow in delivering compatibility. 
In fact, as far as I can tell, COBOL-IT still doesn't have full X/Open 
conformance (including X/Open Accept/Display support) - which will cause 
problems converting from not only MF, but also RM (Liant), AcuCOBOL, Fujitsu 
(Alchemy) and others).  To the best of my knowledge, COBOL-IT does NOT (yet?) 
have XML support compatibly with either the native language of MF or IBM.  And 
it is worth noting that "IBM compatibility" is often the primary concern of 
those "rehosting" COBOL applications, not MF compatibility.  In other words, it 
is important to include IBM extensions (whether or not they are in MF - although 
MF almost always has such support within a year of when IBM introduces it) - and 
it is "relatively" unimportant to include MF extensions that are not available 
in IBM.  (This is NOT true if your migration is from a PC or Unix commercial 
COBOL to a  "free" compiler.  In those cases MF compatibility is often most 
important - even for conversions from other compilers).

   * * * * * *

As I hope I indicated before,

A) COBOL-IT (and OC) both have a HUGE advantage over MF, in that they are free 
*ND* they require no run-time licenses.  (The same is true for the "functionally 
stabilized" Fujitsu V3 compiler - but that is NOT intended for commercial use - 
and includes no "updates" for many years now).

B) To those familiar with "commercial" COBOL products and NOT "GCC/GNU" 
products, the built-in IDE and debugger for COBOL-IT, is well worth it. 
Development and debugging (and even installation) of OC is a "pain" if you are 
not a GCC-junkie (and familiar with C based development).  It can be done, but I 
wont say it is easy (or even reasonable - IMHO).

C) From a VISIBILITY point of view, OC seems much more open and has a greater 
"team"spirit of developers (and those who can help - online - new and 
experienced users).  I haven't seen anything like the forums and lists that OC 
supports - that exist for COBOL-IT.  They may exist, but I haven't seen them.

D) The notion of "supported software" is a real-world issue for many shops doing 
"rehosting" or "migrations". This is certainly another advantage of COBOL-IT 
over OC.  On the other hand, sites for which this is important, really should 
look at the "cost-benefit" advantages of using Micro Focus.  It is FAR from 
cheep (both for development and for deployment).  However, it will provide 
EXCELLENT cross-platform development and (from its past track record) provides 
the best GUESS for support (and enhancements) in the future.  If I were involved 
in a site doing a migration to Unix today  - that might be to Linux tomorrow - 
and Windows the day after - with AIX somewhere in-between, *AND* I had the 
long-term budget for it, I would certainly go with Micro Focus over any free 
compiler.  lf I were doing a "small" conversion and/or have no budget for a 
commercial product, I would select COBOL-IT if I had primarily "commercial 
COBOL" programmers - while I would select OC if I had primarily GCC/GNU or C 
programmers.

NOTE:
  I suppose that I should mention both zCOBOL and IBM COBOL (for AIX and 
Windows).  zCOBOL should be looked at for IBM mainframe rehosting, but is 
CURRENTLY a long way from "complete" - but does include CICS support. IBM COBOL 
(AIX or Windows) provides the ABSOLUTELY best IBM compatibility (by definition - 
as the front-end is shared and/or developed in common).  However, its cost can 
make Micro Focus look "cheap".  As far as I know, TinyCOBOL is "static" at this 
time, so I would certainly select OC or COBOL-IT over it, but for what it does, 
it certainly can be considered.

 * * * * * * *

Note:
  Concerning my "biases".
I formerly worked for Micro Focus (over a decade ago) and am currently an 
alternate to the PL22.4 (formerly J4) COBOL Standards group - representing Micro 
Focus.  (I am NOT paid by them, nor do I have insider information based on 
this).

I am still the requirement coordinator at SHARE for the project that handles LE 
and COBOL (almost exclusively z/OS not workstation or VSE)

I follow zCOBOL development (and try to be helpful to it on "Standards" matters 
in particular)
Similarly, I follow the OC project and provide any help I can for them 
(including writing a document on how to install OC for the Windows based new 
user).

I think (hope) that the "regulars" in this group will confirm that I *try* to be 
unbiased in my "how do you compare compilers" notes in conp.lang.cobol.  I am 
only writing this note as I felt the ihtfp note would mislead (from my 
experience) other readers.  I am NOT saying that readers shouldn't select 
COBOL-IT over OC; only that they should do so "with all the facts".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Experience with "cobol-it" cobol?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> I have NO idea who ihtfp71@gmail.com" is and I certainly don't know
> how he/she works with COBOL-IT, but there is LOTS of inaccurate or
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> of case where this has happened)

Bill, is the bottom line to this just that there is no love lost between the 
COBOL-IT and OC camps?

If that's true, then it is a pity.

Nevertheless, commercial decisions cannot be concerned with that kind of 
bickering.
>
> 2) "On a technical basis, Micro Focus COBOL, as the market leader for
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> also RM (Liant), AcuCOBOL, Fujitsu (Alchemy) and others).

Dou you think Accept/Display of screen sections is a major show stopper? I 
don't. But it is 25 years since  coded anything along those lines.

>To the
> best of my knowledge, COBOL-IT does NOT (yet?) have XML support
> compatibly with either the native language of MF or IBM.

XML for ANY version of  client/server COBOL, as an innate COBOL function is 
pretty irrelevant. The support provided in Windows is easily called/invoked 
and it is superlative, and I believe, though I haven't used it, Linux 
supports it through the C library functions. I don't think that ones's a big 
deal, but I guess if you were bringing mainframe stuff to client/server, 
then it might be more problematic.

I have to ask though, if anyone is considering bringing mainframe COBOL into 
a client/server environment, for commercial applications, why would they 
even consider using a freeware unsupported compiler? The overall costs of 
such a migration would make the cost of the Fujitsu or Micro Focus compiler 
seem pretty trivial, and both of these compilers support OO and COM. (OK, I 
know the Run Time fees for MF are a consideration, but presumably anyone 
doing such a migration will do their financial planning before embarking on 
it).

Moving COBOL to client server simply as a means of prolonging procedural 
COBOL development use is a pipe dream.

The future is in objects and layers, not in procedural COBOL. As neither of 
the Open offerings support this at the moment, neither of them is a viable 
choice as far as I am concerned. (If and when either of them supports OO or 
ability to wrap COBOL as COM components, then they would definitely need to 
be considered.)

It seems to me that there is a time warp when it comes to COBOL. The rest of 
the world has voted with its feet and is moving  (or has moved) into Obect 
Oriented  development. Many sites are realising that the days of COBOL 
development are numbered and need a strategy to move their existing 
investment into the modern world. That world requires support for objects 
and layers. Any COBOL offering WITHOUT such support has to be considered a 
hobbyist indulgence or the last ditch effort of a reactionary COBOL 
establishment, trying desperately to cling to the past.

It seems to me there are two choices:

1. Scrap all your existing investment and redevelop in a modern environment 
with a modern language.
2. Leverage as much of your existing investment as you can into objects that 
play nicely with objects from other languages in a modern evironment.

Option 1 is just too painful to be considered by most sites (although I know 
one site that has done this, progressively over a period of several years. 
It was still traumatic.)

Option 2 gives you some options. You can continue developing in OO COBOL 
after migration, or you can move to other languages, or you can do both.

Personally, I would LOVE to see both OC and COBOL-IT and any other COBOL 
being viable for the foreseeable future. It isn't going to happen without OO 
support and/or component wrapping. COBOL needs to be able to participate as 
a full member of the new order if it is going to survive. (Both Fujitsu and 
MF COBOL offerings have recognised this.)

I have written at length about why I believe this and the arguments that led 
me to this position. (See http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21 )

Having personally undertaken a migration from COBOL to C#/.NET (salvaging 
more than 80% of existing COBOL investment during that exercise)and assisted 
a number of other people to bring their existing COBOL into the world of 
objects and layers, I KNOW this is a viable route.



> And it is
> worth noting that "IBM compatibility" is often the primary concern of
> those "rehosting" COBOL applications, not MF compatibility.

Yes, and you can understand why.


>In other
> words, it is important to include IBM extensions (whether or not they
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> important - even for conversions from other compilers).

And once you get there, you find that the new environment is different from 
the old one. If you want to get the best from it you need to change some 
thinking. Objects and layers.


>   * * * * * *
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> years now).

It ISN'T a huge advantage if they are strangers in a strange land once you 
get there.

>
> B) To those familiar with "commercial" COBOL products and NOT
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> reasonable - IMHO).

Nevertheless, you managed it and your document on the experience is 
valuable.

>
> C) From a VISIBILITY point of view, OC seems much more open and has a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> exist, but I haven't seen them.

I think you would agree, Bill, that that is a personal perception which may 
or may not be true...

Prospective clients for these products are really not concerned about how 
"gung-ho" the developers are, as long as the products can deliver what is 
needed. My argument is that they currently can't (because I need objects and 
layers). This means they are only viable for experimentation and "emulation" 
of existing COBOL on other platforms. It isn't possible to really get into 
the spirit of the new platform with them. Emulating your existing processing 
on the client server environment may have some attraction as a saving in 
hardware cost, but that is a very short-sighted view. To move to this 
environment properly and obtain all the benefits it offers, you need an OO 
language. That COULD be COBOL (but NOT OC or COBOL-IT currently) but it is 
no wonder that people are moving to Java as an alternative.
>
> D) The notion of "supported software" is a real-world issue for many
> shops doing "rehosting" or "migrations".

Absolutely. Everyone I have spoken to about this sees that as a major 
concern. Any client/server compiler they go with has to support their 
existing stuff AND enable them to move to OO in the new environment. Fujitsu 
is popular because it meets these criteria and has no runtime fees.


>This is certainly another
> advantage of COBOL-IT over OC.

Not for me. If it doesn't provide what is needed long term (objects and 
layers) then I can't use it and I can't suggest it to clients using my tools 
to migrate.

> On the other hand, sites for which
> this is important, really should look at the "cost-benefit"
> advantages of using Micro Focus.

(Or Fujitsu... :-)  Or anybody else who has a client/server COBOL compiler 
that supports objects and layers.)

>It is FAR from cheep (both for
> development and for deployment).

That really depends, Bill. Certainly, the Fujitsu .NET COBOL compiler is not 
cheap (neither is Net Express...)  but if you ALREADY have a MF or Fujitsu 
compiler that can compile Object Classes and wrap objects so they can be 
layered, then you DON'T NEED to invest in a new compiler. The .NET 
environment can run your existing objects in native mode, alongside new 
components developed with CLR code.

It comes down to whether you intend to continue development solely with 
COBOL in the new environment, or whether you are targeting a new language 
like C#, VB.NET, or Java. (All of which are FREE, so you can't quibble about 
the price...)  The days when sites developed in a single language (COBOL or 
PL/1 :-)) are long gone.

The important thing to recognise is that migration is going to incur a cost. 
The cost for a new compiler (if you even need it) is not such a big chunk of 
that cost when viewed in perspective. Neverthless, you can probably save 
several thousand dollars if you DON'T invest in a new COBOL compiler, and it 
makes sense not to spend any more money on COBOL than you HAVE to, if you 
are planning to move away from it anyway, long term.

>However, it will provide EXCELLENT
> cross-platform development and (from its past track record) provides
> the best GUESS for support (and enhancements) in the future.

Cross platform development of what? Perpetuation of what is done now? Sooner 
or later people are going to "get it" there is no viable long term pathway 
for procedural COBOL development. This realization becomes (painfully...) 
apparent when you have moved to say, .NET, and continue trying to do things 
the way you have always done them in the old environment. All you do is 
degrade the new environment and it fails ot deliver the things it promised. 
(Then we'll see posts here about what a crock the .NET environment is... 
When, really, it is a simple failure to understand what it needs (objects 
and layers) and adjust your traditional development thinking accordingly. 
It's a bit like complaining because your Ferrari doesn't perform when you 
run it on whale oil..."But I've ALWAYS powered everything with whale oil... 
it works fine in my hurricane lamps... what a crock those Ferraris are...")


> If I
> were involved in a site doing a migration to Unix today  - that might
> be to Linux tomorrow - and Windows the day after - with AIX somewhere
> in-between, *AND* I had the long-term budget for it, I would
> certainly go with Micro Focus over any free compiler.

I probably would, but I like the fact there are no runtime fees with 
Fujitsu. I think the deciding factor would be what was ALREADY IN PLACE.

MF probably has an edge if AIX is in the mix.



lf I were
> doing a "small" conversion and/or have no budget for a commercial
> product, I would select COBOL-IT if I had primarily "commercial
> COBOL" programmers - while I would select OC if I had primarily
> GCC/GNU or C programmers.

I understand your reasoning, Bill, but I don't share your conclusion.

I wouldn't consider either of the Open solutions because they can't get me 
where I need to go.

The decision on whether another compiler should be obtained depends on what 
is already in use and whether it supports OO and COM, and runs on the target 
platform (presumably client/server)  If it doesn't (either count) then a 
client/server compiler that supports OO and COM would be required. (It 
doesn't have to be .NET, because we can run in .NET through Interop 
Services.)  All things being equal, I'd go Fujitsu. It is an excellent 
product and there are no runtime fees...) I would spend money getting 
existing programmers who know the business, upgraded to OO and probably have 
them experiment with C# or Java, depending on where we were heading. If the 
target is .NET, definitely C#.

<snipped>

Pete..
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ COBOL tools - and wher are you going (was: Experience with "cobol-it" cobol?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-27T22:30:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rhubm.403465$Xo1.222305@en-nntp-07.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<h477en$f3j$01$1@news.t-online.com> <1248354943@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <0585ab70-5f88-42ef-8033-6acb53b07e9a@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <1248450735@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <47f57da6-a33b-4c98-9a7b-a5eb3a2a91f6@t13g2000yqt.googlegroups.com> <zynbm.403533$Lo1.274389@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7d7410F2b64trU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
New subject line and almost all text snipped

In another thread (and my quoting TOTALLY out of context), Pete wrote,

> I wouldn't consider either of the Open solutions because they can't get me 
> where I need to go.

I may be crazy on this, but there is (to me) an interesting question for sites 
wanting to rehost (IBM) mainframe COBOL applications to Unix/Linux/AIX (but I am 
NOT including Windows) for this.

Are you trying to rehost your USER INTERFACE (UI) to Unix/Linux/AIX - or is the 
"business logic" and/or "data layer" that will be there, while your "end users" 
will be on Windows (with or without .NET).

I know that there are "GUI" tools for **ix environments, but it seems to me that 
NOT going with "COMs" or "Objects" is still (relatively?) common for 
applications where the UI *and* the logic rules on **ix.  On the other hand, if 
the UI is on Windows, then I do agree with Pete that most "new" and *most* 
"ported" applications will be using some variation of OO.

I am "out of touch" with much of the IBM mainframe migrations to AIX.  If the UI 
will be there (or even on Linux/Unix) what type of interface (GUI or 
character-based) or end users expecting today?  What type of applications (OO, 
procedural, other) are such migrations using once they get off the mainframe?

I certainly may be totally mistaken on this, but the fact that neither OC nor 
COBOL-IT currently support "native" OO COBOL, doesn't seem (to me) that it would 
be a show stopper for sites that are moving entirely to Unix/Linux/AIX - while 
it might well be so for sites that are moving all (or much) of their 
applications to Windows.

Any comment form the "real world" COBOL migrators out there?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL tools - and wher are you going (was: Experience with "cobol-it" cobol?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-29T00:44:02+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7d8a4eF2abaofU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<h477en$f3j$01$1@news.t-online.com> <1248354943@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <0585ab70-5f88-42ef-8033-6acb53b07e9a@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <1248450735@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <47f57da6-a33b-4c98-9a7b-a5eb3a2a91f6@t13g2000yqt.googlegroups.com> <zynbm.403533$Lo1.274389@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7d7410F2b64trU1@mid.individual.net> <rhubm.403465$Xo1.222305@en-nntp-07.dc1.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> New subject line and almost all text snipped
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> get me where I need to go.

Maybe I need to be clearer on this. I am not (at this time...) talking 
mainframe migration to client/server (although I'll certainly be looking at 
that in the future.) Most mainframe sites are migrating to client server 
because they see savings in hardware costs, ability to use standard tools 
for things like reporting, and better development tools. For this "initial 
migration" it is enough to get stuff running on the new platform. You can 
run existing applications under Windows without problem, but if you want to 
use .NET effectively, you will need object Classes or wrapped COM components 
that make legacy code look like it is an object.

You can write them in OO COBOL or you can use something else, but if you 
have a version of COBOL that doesn't support OO it is about as much use to 
me as roller skates would be to a steeple jack...

I believe the future is in objects and layers. I moved all of my own COBOL 
development into such an environment using .NET and since then I have been 
helping others to make the same transition. Most modern languages support 
objects and layers, but the Open COBOLs don't. (They don't support OO or 
COM. Not yet, anyway...). They are therefore not currently capable of taking 
me (or my clients) where we want to go.

That is pretty much the context of my statement.

>
> I may be crazy on this, but there is (to me) an interesting question
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> while your "end users" will be on Windows (with or without .NET).

I think some of them will be using CICS Migration tools to convert CICS 
applications to a supportable GUI in the new client/server environment.
>
> I know that there are "GUI" tools for **ix environments, but it seems
> to me that NOT going with "COMs" or "Objects" is still (relatively?)
> common for applications where the UI *and* the logic rules on **ix.

Sure. I agree. I have one prospect who is looking at Linux but until he 
commits (or doesn't) I haven't got into what the implications are. My 
personal preference is for Classes and tiers, but I agree it isn't essential 
for this environment. However, I think that will change. The difference 
between what Java (being OO) can leverage from this environment, and what 
COBOL (not being) can do, will make people think twice about staying with 
procedural code. It may take a year or two, but gradually the advantages of 
OO filter through. It isn't just about programming; as we have discussed 
here on a number of occasions, more "agile" ways to develop applications are 
gaining popularity. Iterative approaches lend themselves much more to OO 
than procedural coding.


> On the other hand, if the UI is on Windows, then I do agree with Pete
> that most "new" and *most* "ported" applications will be using some
> variation of OO.

I am working almost exclusively in the Windows and .NET environments, so, 
yes, I am biased that way.

>
> I am "out of touch" with much of the IBM mainframe migrations to AIX.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> sites that are moving all (or much) of their applications to Windows.

I think that is probably fair, Bill. (Although it may not be in another 
couple of years...)

My comment should not be interpreted as being anti-Open COBOL software (I am 
very much in favour of it...), rather it was simply observing what my 
company (and our clients) need to achieve our end goals, and noting the fact 
that neither of the Open offerings under discussion were supporting it. (Not 
a criticism; merely an observation of the fact... I understand that 
resources are limited but I really hope some consideration is being given to 
OO before it is altogether too late for COBOL.)

>
> Any comment form the "real world" COBOL migrators out there?

Hey! the world I live in  and the migration tools I am providing are "real" 
enough, thank you. :-) I have a growing base of clients who consider them 
"real" too... really useful. :-)

Pete.
```

```
>
> Any comment form the "real world" COBOL migrators out there?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com"

On the projects I participated the most common GUI for COBOL
migrations is the Web Browser.
After the migration, of course.
I don't see that the GUI issue is related to OO. Not in my case.

I love Linux and my experience with it has been great. It's very
stable and production ready, as many sites can testify.
Portuguese Air Force runs Linux with migrated Unisys (Univac) COBOL.
We have production code on Linux at the Navy.

Regarding OO: why going for OO COBOL when you can choose other OO
platforms: Java, C#, Ruby, ...
Java gives you run everywhere, no runtime fees, no upfront costs, free
IDE's, the world.
Ruby is not quite there and has no Sun/Oracle elephant pushing it. No
Gartner reports, bla bla bla.
C# has Microsoft. Novell Mono project puts C# on Linux, Windows and
macosx.

No matter what technology you were/are, I do believe the web is the
right interface now.
I believe many companies are struggling with multiple application
human interfaces and choosing one is key to make life easier to all
users.
Maybe it is not the most productive interface for power users, but the
web browser is the best choice these days: zero install, low
maintnance, free upgrades, etc.

Productivity has more to do with simplicity and usability on the UI
and less to do with the technology (terminal, winforms, GTK, web,
etc).

Sorry, no experience with COBOL-IT.

joao.p.ribeiro
AT
pt.fujitsu.com

- **References:** `<h477en$f3j$01$1@news.t-online.com> <1248354943@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <0585ab70-5f88-42ef-8033-6acb53b07e9a@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <1248450735@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <47f57da6-a33b-4c98-9a7b-a5eb3a2a91f6@t13g2000yqt.googlegroups.com> <zynbm.403533$Lo1.274389@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7d7410F2b64trU1@mid.individual.net> <rhubm.403465$Xo1.222305@en-nntp-07.dc1.easynews.com> <e9640895-d8c7-49bc-945c-cbf926cba653@a13g2000yqc.googlegroups.com>`

```
ribjo wrote:
>> Any comment form the "real world" COBOL migrators out there?
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> migrations is the Web Browser.

That's a very good point.

> After the migration, of course.
> I don't see that the GUI issue is related to OO. Not in my case.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> platforms: Java, C#, Ruby, ...

Precisely.

But let's say you are opting for future development in C#, and have a large 
inventory of legacy COBOL code... (as I did a few years back)

Then you need to get this legacy into .NET. You want your new C# code to be 
able to use your existing legacy (where it makes sense to NOT re-invent the 
wheel...) so you need to wrap your legacy as objects. You have 2 choices 
(assuming .NET is the target...)

1.  Buy a .NET (CLR generating)  OO COBOL compiler.

OR...

2.  Use your existing OO COBOL compiler that generates "Native code" and 
create COM objects that can run under Interop Services as .NET  Classes. 
This saves  the need to buy a new .NET OO COBOL compiler when you are 
planning to move off COBOL anyway...

So, in this scenario there is definitely a place for using OO COBOL... :-)

> Java gives you run everywhere, no runtime fees, no upfront costs, free
> IDE's, the world.

Yes, it does. But it isn't really suited to .NET and that is where I want to 
be...

> Ruby is not quite there and has no Sun/Oracle elephant pushing it. No
> Gartner reports, bla bla bla.

Nevertheless, Ruby is making itself felt in Europe...

> C# has Microsoft.

And that is the customer base for me...


>Novell Mono project puts C# on Linux, Windows and
> macosx.
>

But Mono does not provide Interop Services at the moment (at least it didn't 
when last I checked. That was over a year ago and there was talk of it being 
added...)


> No matter what technology you were/are, I do believe the web is the
> right interface now.

I am very sympathetic to your position. I think more and more companies are 
using the Web.

Please visit the cobol21 site (if you haven't already) at : 
http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21

This site has samples of code wrapping procedural COBOL so it can run in 
.NET, along with a good deal of background on the component based OO 
approach for legacy COBOL, and a strategy for moving to .NET.

> I believe many companies are struggling with multiple application
> human interfaces and choosing one is key to make life easier to all
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> maintnance, free upgrades, etc.

I think you have a very good point. Faced with creating a user interface for 
Windows COBOL and given a choice of say, Web Forms, WPF, or the Web Browser, 
I can see using ASP.NET (for example) as a very viable alternative. I'm 
going to give some more thought to this...

>
> Productivity has more to do with simplicity and usability on the UI
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks for a very interesting post, Jo.

Pete.
```

On Wed, 29 Jul 2009 03:57:15 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>But let's say you are opting for future development in C#, and have a large 
>inventory of legacy COBOL code... (as I did a few years back)
>
>Then you need to get this legacy into .NET. 

Except more often than not, (In My Opinion - by far), that isn't an
option taken.  

What I see is companies deciding that either the future development is
for future projects (not replacing the legacy system), or the legacy
system is replaced.    

Quite often this is a good thing.   It's like moving to clean out our
garage, and sell or throw away all of the stuff that won't fit
effectively in the new house.   Being forced to reanalyze the work
needs and start over has its upside.
```

Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Wed, 29 Jul 2009 03:57:15 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> system is replaced.

There are times when that's not an option. It depends too on how difficult 
it is to refactor what you have. (If it's low effort/cost why wouldn't you 
do it? If it can be automated so it is done quickly and cheaply there is 
even better reason to do it.)

I'm not disagreeing with you, Howard; many companies will take the option to 
rebuild everything, but smaller companies (especially) can't always afford 
to, and the business has to run while whatever new solution is being 
developed and put in place. Refactoring can ease the transition to a new 
environment, particularly where the user interface doesn't change.

I see no reason to throw the baby out with the bath water and my garage has 
been re-organised many times with certain boxes still remaining... :-)
>
> Quite often this is a good thing.   It's like moving to clean out our
> garage, and sell or throw away all of the stuff that won't fit
> effectively in the new house.   Being forced to reanalyze the work
> needs and start over has its upside.

Pete.
```

Those who make the decisions don't often think this way.   All changes
cost in the short run.  To justify spending the money, they want to
show significant change.    A safe political choice is to shop around
for the replacement package and spend twice what refactoring would
cost.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Experience with "cobol-it" cobol?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

- **References:** `<1248354943@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <0585ab70-5f88-42ef-8033-6acb53b07e9a@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <1248450735@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <47f57da6-a33b-4c98-9a7b-a5eb3a2a91f6@t13g2000yqt.googlegroups.com> <zynbm.403533$Lo1.274389@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7d7410F2b64trU1@mid.individual.net> <uf4u651ukgk9uvkq927hanlqhl8b34p0t4@4ax.com> <q85u65hme493h07uu1urdfg0ughchu9tbc@4ax.com> <bppu655vr9ntot4ahcbefg0ki1sm11eocb@4ax.com> <7d9rtqF29fp80U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Wed, 29 Jul 2009 14:53:45 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Clark F Morris wrote:
>> On Tue, 28 Jul 2009 09:12:01 -0600, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>the outside world and pass our results around."

There is a certain amount of that.  However in the ENVIRONMENT that
much mainframe code exists (CICS and IMS for on-line) many of the
issues that are handled by OO in the non-z world are handled by
different mechanisms in this environment.  The editing which would
require a class or a method in most languages is native to COBOL.
Indeed, I suspect the first iterations of the classes were awkward by
comparison with what was available in COBOL.  However the
extensibility of some of these solutions changed the flavor of things.
CICS screens are probably still better than the latest web equivalents
if you are doing basically data entry and don't need any help.  NOTE
the qualifications.

In the case of mainframe COBOL and XML, I suspect that the goal is to
use XML in CICS to interface either with other computer systems
through things like MQ series or pass information back and forth to
web servers and get out of screen handling.  Note that I have not been
actively employed at anything for almost 3 years and that gig was on a
legacy system so discount accordingly.  

COBOL with CICS on 3270 screens is decent for internal only
applications and probably gives faster response time.  Just don't
expect decent help or prompting and don't expect any pictures.  For
other applications where better guidance is needed, any of the 3270
screen based methods are a disaster.

The infuriating thing in the mainframe arena is the lack of any
strategic vision.  IBM has 64 bit Java and 64 bit Websphere.  It has
XPLINK to cut interfacing costs.  It has a C/C++ compiler that knows
what to do with 64 bit addresses.  Yet there is no 64 bit Cobol that
can interface with C/C++.  Despite the existence of both hardware
floating point decimal, Java support of it and the Java use of IEEE
binary floating point, IBM is blindly thinking of using the 2002
standard floating point data type for floating point decimal if and
when they support decimal floating point.  Of course the work done on
decimal floating point (pushed within IBM by Mike Cowlishaw, inventor
of REXX) was done primarily for business applications.  

Another issue is the extreme reluctance of some managements to change.
Upgrading from COBOL 74 was a major issue with many shops being
dragged kicking and screaming.  COBOL could become OO and still be
vital in many areas if there was some vision but I suspect that many
managements want to replace their applications with something like SAP
which already has the help and descriptive information built in may
make COBOL an also ran.  Doing Help right and providing useful screens
is a major effort.  Of course conversions to SAP (or enterprise system
of the month) are not cheap and one wonders if there is a better way.
>
>I've stopped worrying about it. People will do whatever they want to until 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pete.
```

- **References:** `<1248354943@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <0585ab70-5f88-42ef-8033-6acb53b07e9a@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <1248450735@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <47f57da6-a33b-4c98-9a7b-a5eb3a2a91f6@t13g2000yqt.googlegroups.com> <zynbm.403533$Lo1.274389@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7d7410F2b64trU1@mid.individual.net> <uf4u651ukgk9uvkq927hanlqhl8b34p0t4@4ax.com> <q85u65hme493h07uu1urdfg0ughchu9tbc@4ax.com> <bppu655vr9ntot4ahcbefg0ki1sm11eocb@4ax.com> <7d9rtqF29fp80U1@mid.individual.net> <s761759e9ngr24pp2sre1u7sfncur9ovad@4ax.com>`

```
Clark F Morris wrote:
> On Wed, 29 Jul 2009 14:53:45 +1200, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[84 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Pete.

Some interesting insights, Clark; thanks for that.

I guess it is always difficult for people to change what they do. I look 
back on 3270 with some affection; in its time it was pretty good.

(Have you used the "Then" and "Now" buttons on 
http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21/S2NTestServer.aspx  ? put some input 
into the black bar halfway down the page and press "Try it" first... )

I remember when colour first became available on 3270. There was great 
scepticism. Who needs it, we already have highlighting if we want to 
emphasise something? And yet ANYTHING that helps the user interface and 
makes things clearer has to be desirable. The very same naysayers 6 months 
later couldn't imagine working without it...

I take your point about lack of strategic vision by management. I think it 
is a spin-off from working in a conservative environment with advice from 
people who are also resistant to change. And sometimes, IBM themselves are 
not providing strategic vision. Their vision is limited to tactical 
considerations like: "How can we sell this hardware/software that is sitting 
in inventory and we know is going to be obsoleted in a few months time..." 
Often exacerbated by bonuses being offered to salespeople if they move the 
stuff slated for replacement. (I don't wish to pick on IBM; most mainframe 
suppliers do this...)

I have worked on some sites where the management were excellent and knew 
where they were going and what their principles would be, but they were 
definitely a minority.

I think one of the reasons for popularity of client/server (amongst 
management) is that if you get it wrong, it is pretty easy to 
replace/reconfigure without incurring horrendous cost. You can deploy 
servers into other areas and they are not "wasted".  Once a mainframe is 
deployed, it stays deployed...

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<h477en$f3j$01$1@news.t-online.com> <1248354943@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <0585ab70-5f88-42ef-8033-6acb53b07e9a@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <47f57da6-a33b-4c98-9a7b-a5eb3a2a91f6@t13g2000yqt.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hello ihtfp71!

27 Jul 09 17:03, ihtfp71 wrote to All:

 i> COBOL-IT was forked from OpenCOBOL in August of 2008. I am told that
 i> there were a lot of bugs and some performance issues that needed to be
 i> fixed before the compiler could pass third party certification.
 i> Resolving those issues consumed many months, but the compiler is now
 i> certified by Oracle (Tuxedo), Red Hat, MySQL, HTWC (XFrame), Sun, HP,
 i> and Syncsort. Certification projects are in progress with DB2 (UDB)
 i> and Oracle Pro*COBOL; there are no known problems but official
 i> certifications have not been issued yet so far as I know. COBOL-IT has
 i> been successfully tested with UniKix, but without a certificate being
 i> issued.

 i> OpenCOBOL has benefitted from the work of COBOL-IT, as Roger While and
 i> COBOL-IT are regularly in touch and many COBOL-IT bug fixes have been
 i> incorporated into OpenCOBOL.


I'm sure Roger will be pleased to hear this as this is NOT the case.

Many and all reported bugs (have been cleared) via the support websites 
devoted to OC where problems and upgrades produced directly by Roger.

To my knowledge COBOL-IT has not provided ANY backflow for any fixes to the 
code as an examination of the source will show.

OC (Open Cobol) has at least over the two or more years that I have been using 
it have passed all of the NIST tests to COBOL85 as well as tests covering to 
2002 and later as indicated within the 20xx standard docs.


Vince

Using OC for prod systems since 2007 and others since 1961 (including MF, IBM, 
Microsoft, ICL, Honeywell, Univac and many others).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Experience with "cobol-it" cobol?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-29T14:59:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gS1cm.368049$2p1.74006@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<h477en$f3j$01$1@news.t-online.com> <1248354943@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn> <0585ab70-5f88-42ef-8033-6acb53b07e9a@h11g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <47f57da6-a33b-4c98-9a7b-a5eb3a2a91f6@t13g2000yqt.googlegroups.com> <1248889611@f1.n250.z2.fidonet.ftn>`

```
Vince,
  Just to clarify,

OC doesn't claim to have all of the required 2002 Standard features in it (and 
there are NO "generally available tests" for that Standard).  However, in most 
(not quite all) cases OC does conform to the '85 Standard, the Intrinsic 
Functions Amendment, the 2002 Standard, and even a few things from the draft 
revision WHERE support is "claimed" for that feature.

Certainly the biggest issue with the '02 Standard (for some users) is the lack 
of Object Orientation.  (However, others may miss Report Writer).  Last that I 
heard, Roger was working on User Defined Functions, but that is certainly NOT in 
the "generally available" tarball.

On the other hand (going back "on topic") COBOL-IT has none of this either.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Experience with "cobol-it" cobol?

- **From:** Vaclav Snajdr <vsn@snajdr.de>
- **Date:** 2009-07-24T12:32:57+02:00

```
Hello Vince,

not all features of accept are included. One example is the question
how to differ between the return-key and the space-key. In case of return
I need something as a exception in a editor application for filling
formulars or edit the cobol source.

 
    CRT STATUS IS IT-CRT-4.

working section .. no screen section

01  IT-CRT-4         PIC 9999 VALUE 0.
01  FUN-KEY          PIC 9999 COMP. *> the value of exception
01  EA1              PIC X.
01  I-Z              PIC 9999 COMP. *> number of line
01  I-P              PIC  999 COMP. *> number of position in line
01  ZBZ              PIC   99 COMP. *> screen line
01  BP               PIC   99 COMP. *> screen column

01  TX-BAU.             *> common editor table for all applications
           02  TX-BO.
             03 TO-OC              OCCURS 9999.
                05 T-EL            PIC X(160).
           02 TX-1                 REDEFINES TX-BO.
             03  TO-1-V            OCCURS 9999.
                05  T-1-H          OCCURS 160.
                   07  T-1         PIC X.

       *  with auto means automatically to next field if
       *  the input field is full 
H-EIN.  MOVE 0            TO       FUN-KEY.
        ACCEPT EA1        AT    LINE ZBZ COL BP WITH AUTO
                  on exception     move it-crt-4        to fun-key
                  display it-crt-4 at 0101
        END-ACCEPT.

        IF   FUN-KEY      =        1003 PERFORM INSERT-NEW-LINE.
        IF   FUN-KEY      =        1012 PERFORM DELETE-CUR-LINE.
        and so on
       * only return - go to next line in the editor table and on the screen
        IF   FUN-KEY      =       "only-return-pressed"
             ADD 01       TO       I-T ZBZ
             MOVE 01      TO       I-P BP
             GO TO                 H-EIN
        END-IF.
       *                          value entry
        IF   FUN-KEY      =        0
             MOVE EA1     TO       T-1 (I-Z,I-P)
        END-IF.
        GO  TO                     H-EIN.
        
# the mf-solution:
        *                            return = naechste zeile
           IF   MF-CRT-STATUS-1 =  0            AND
                MF-CRT-STATUS-2 =  48           AND
                MF-CRT-STATUS-3 =  10
                MOVE 99        TO  FUN-KEY
           END-IF.

        *  there is a way to ask that only return was put


Vince Coen wrote:

> Hello Vaclav!
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Vince
```

#### ↳ Re: Experience with "cobol-it" cobol?

- **From:** Frank King <frankintally@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-27T11:01:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ae290036-45da-4398-becc-c39304bd2502@d9g2000prh.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<h477en$f3j$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
This discussion caught my eye because earlier in the year I had to
research whether it would be possible for our Windows/Web COBOL
product to support OpenCOBOL and COBOL-IT.  At the time we supported
Micro Focus 4.0 and Micro Focus NetExpress and Fujitsu  The folks at
COBOL-IT were anxious to work with us to support the Windows API
calling convention and made considerable changes to support Micro
Focus dialect - including some of the more common compiler directives
that we use.  Their releases for Windows are already built and come
with an installer.  As Bill Klein has pointed out COBOL-IT comes with
an interactive debugger that is not yet as fully featured as the ones
that come with NetExpress and Fujitsu.  It does have the basics and
we've been told it will continue to be improved.  They also provide a
COBOL plug-in for the Eclipse IDE but since our product has its own
IDE interface we did not evaluate it.

It was my experience when researching OpenCOBOL that they have a solid
COBOL compiler but they did not seem to be very interested in the
Windows environment.  Unless something has changed recently you have
to download sources from OpenCOBOL and other places to build the
compiler.  While this is natural in the Linux/UNIX world it is not
that straightforward in the Windows world.  However, the big show
stopper for us was lack of  native support for the Windows' API.

Long story short, we found it easy to work with COBOL-IT to provide
support for their compiler for Windows and Web development.

> --
> Vaclav Snajdr
```

### AIX COBOL ASSIGN error.

- **From:** gaya3 <gayathri.kalyanasundaram@wipro.com>

Hi All,


I have just started writing my first COBOL in AIX environment.

My COBOL code is as follows :

+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
+
FILE-CONTROL.
    SELECT FILECOBV ASSIGN TO FILE1.
--------- Rest of the Code..

I have set the environment variable FILE1 as below.
   export FILE1=filename
I have also tried giving
   export FILE1=~/correct-path/filename

+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
+

I am getting the following error whenever i execute the code:

IWZ200S  Error detected during OPEN for file 'FILECOBV'. File status
is: 35.
         File system indicated: File Not Found.
         Message routine called from offset 0x38 of routine
iwzWriteERRmsg.
         iwzWriteERRmsg called from offset 0x574 of routine
_iwzCallUseProc.
         _iwzCallUseProc called from offset 0x1538 of routine
_iwzSeqOpen.
         _iwzSeqOpen called from offset 0x2ec of routine
_iwzGenericOpen.
         _iwzGenericOpen called from offset 0x458 of routine TESTPGM.
IWZ901S  Program exits due to severe or critical error.

=========================================================================
Could someone please tell me the solution for this..

Thanks in advance..!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: AIX COBOL ASSIGN error.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-23T03:22:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i5V9m.407483$4p1.325977@en-nntp-03.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<8084fdf9-fd42-42dc-b9ce-6fef9dfbc590@s31g2000yqs.googlegroups.com> <2fb4b24b-2bc3-48f1-9bff-283be584f533@z4g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <d8bafbe8-e492-49c0-8ddc-dd51f0163301@v2g2000vbb.googlegroups.com>`

```
The IWZ message makes me think this is an IBM compiler for AIX?  Is that true? 
(There are many other compilers, such as Micro Focus that run on AIX).

If this is an IBM compiler for AIX, have you checked what the IBM Programmers 
Guide says to do - for relating Select/Assign to physical files?  I know about 
IBM COBOL for Windows, but haven't looked at AIX for a long time.  They may well 
have a different compiler option for associated filenames in the ASSIGN clause 
with external physical file names.

If you are following the information in the PG, then you should contact the IBM 
support center.

NOTE:
  To get the current IBM COBOL for AIX documentation, see:
http://www-01.ibm.com/software/awdtools/cobol/aix/library/
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX COBOL ASSIGN error.

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-23T10:04:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eJWdnU9-vs-e4fXXnZ2dnUVZ_oydnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<8084fdf9-fd42-42dc-b9ce-6fef9dfbc590@s31g2000yqs.googlegroups.com>`

```
gaya3 wrote:
> Hi All,
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance..!


The error message says that the specified file does not exist. There are two 
principal causes:

* The file you're trying to open simply does not exist, or
* The file exists, but not in the location you're telling the program to 
look.

Change the OPEN INPUT or OPEN I-O to OPEN OUTPUT, then close the program.

Where is the new file?
```

#### ↳ Re: AIX COBOL ASSIGN error.

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-07-24T03:59:37+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7crfnaF2909gfU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<8084fdf9-fd42-42dc-b9ce-6fef9dfbc590@s31g2000yqs.googlegroups.com>`

```
gaya3 wrote:
> Hi All,
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance..!

Try this (assuming FILECOBV is NOT VSAM...)


select FILECOBV assign EVFILE1

export EVFILE1-STL-EVFILE1= <insert the path and filename here>


If it IS VSAM you must set the export as:  export EVFILE1-VSA-EVFILE1= 
<insert the path and filename here>

Please write it exactly as shown, including the case. If it works, try 
substituting FILE1 for EVFILE1

Let us know how you get on.

Pete.
```

<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

…[58 more quoted lines elided]…

I am getting the following error whenever i specify the Export
variable as you have told..

export EVFILE1-STL-EVFILE1=/home/username1/cobegs/STUDENTS.DAT
bash: export: `EVFILE1-STL-EVFILE1=/home/afnigk1/cobegs/STUDENTS.DAT':
not a valid identifier


I have posted the entire sample program.. I am desparate to know the
solution for this problem.. Please refer this to anyone who has worked
on AIX COBOL platform.

-------------------------------------------------
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  SampleFile.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT StudentFile ASSIGN STL-EVFILE1.
      *       ORGANIZATION IS SEQUENTIAL.
      *       ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD StudentFile.
       01 StudentDetails.
              02  StudentId       PIC 9(7).
              02  StudentName PIC X(10).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       Begin.
              OPEN INPUT StudentFile
              READ StudentFile
               AT END MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO StudentDetails
              END-READ
               PERFORM UNTIL StudentDetails = HIGH-VALUES
                 DISPLAY StudentId SPACE StudentName
                 READ StudentFile
                  AT END MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO StudentDetails
               END-READ
             END-PERFORM
              CLOSE StudentFile
              STOP RUN.
---------------------------------------------------------

Thank you all for the effort taken!!!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: AIX COBOL ASSIGN error.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-24T02:18:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xfdam.419022$4p1.390303@en-nntp-03.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<8084fdf9-fd42-42dc-b9ce-6fef9dfbc590@s31g2000yqs.googlegroups.com> <7crfnaF2909gfU1@mid.individual.net> <b1027787-2571-4ddb-a827-706a76d3fe86@a26g2000yqn.googlegroups.com>`

```
The following is what the IBM AIX PG says (among other things),

Identifying STL files
 To use the standard language file system for a file, identify the file with the 
SELECT clause as shown below.
  SELECT file1 ASSIGN USING 'STL-MyFile'

If the runtime option FILESYS(STL) is in effect, identify the file as follows:
  SELECT file1 ASSIGN TO 'MyFile'

If you have defined the environment variable MYFILE (for example,
   export MYFILE=STL-MYFILE),
identify the file as follows:
   SELECT file1 ASSIGN TO MYFILE"

So what is your setting for the FILESYS compiler option?  Do you want STL 
(Standard  Language filesystem) for this file?  Have you tried using quotes?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: AIX COBOL ASSIGN error.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-25T12:10:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<90Ham.571560$641.245879@en-nntp-10.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<8084fdf9-fd42-42dc-b9ce-6fef9dfbc590@s31g2000yqs.googlegroups.com> <7crfnaF2909gfU1@mid.individual.net> <0a654590-9e5a-45ad-a336-6d7cab5b773c@j32g2000yqh.googlegroups.com> <7ctjeaF28kqv0U1@mid.individual.net> <de4f994d-fdd2-4f1b-a10d-22defc3aa694@d9g2000prh.googlegroups.com>`

```
When you say that  records include CR, do you mean that you have 80 bytes of 
data (already) and - for example - the 49th byte might be a "CR" that should be 
treated AS DATA (possibly in a COMP field).
   OR
are you saying that if the 49th byte is a CR, that you want to treat the first 
48 bytes as data and want to treat the record as those 48 bytes plus (for an 80 
byte record) the next 32 bytes are spaces?

If you have records that ARE 80 bytes but include CR as data within records, 
then you are correct, that you need to define this as "record sequential".

If you currently have LINE SEQUENTIAL (with variable amounts of data in each 
record) and want to "pad" it with spaces, then you can do this with a READ INTO 
with putting it into an 80-byte working-storage item.  The system will "pad" 
each record with spaces.  In this case, you may want to (probably should) define 
your input with the VARYING IN SIZE phrase (as well as defining it as LINE 
SEQUENTIAL).

  ****

I don't think you have told us if you are an experienced COBOL programmer who is 
new to AIX - or if you are new to COBOL and AIX.  Some of your questions sound 
like you may be new to COBOL, but if you aren't, then tell us what compiler and 
Operating System you are used to - and we may be able to help you more.

NOTE:
  You still have NOT confirmed that you are using the IBM compiler on AIX and 
not some other AIX compiler.  You also haven't confirmed that you have the 
Language Reference and Programming Guide for which I previously sent the URL. 
If you don't have those books yet, I would suggest that you get them.  The PG 
really does have pretty useful information on it - especially if you are used to 
COBOL on another platform.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: AIX COBOL ASSIGN error.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-27T14:18:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%3nbm.356198$2p1.170611@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<8084fdf9-fd42-42dc-b9ce-6fef9dfbc590@s31g2000yqs.googlegroups.com> <7crfnaF2909gfU1@mid.individual.net> <0a654590-9e5a-45ad-a336-6d7cab5b773c@j32g2000yqh.googlegroups.com> <7ctjeaF28kqv0U1@mid.individual.net> <de4f994d-fdd2-4f1b-a10d-22defc3aa694@d9g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <90Ham.571560$641.245879@en-nntp-10.dc1.easynews.com> <7fb48fce-09f4-4278-8552-aac0b1f37e35@v36g2000yqv.googlegroups.com> <I8abm.575922$641.566646@en-nntp-10.dc1.easynews.com> <bbd0a0af-e1c5-41a7-a482-3147a165065f@e27g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>`

```
With a name like "studentfile", is there any chance that this is part of a COBOL 
class?  (either an instructor led class, or one from a book)?  If so, you may 
want to follow there instructions.

"Record Sequential" files are pretty much a "COBOL only" type file (on AIX and 
Windows). MOST "sequential" files on AIX are *intended* be available to thinks 
like VI or other "text editors" or AIX utilities and EXPECT to use CR as a 
record delimiter (and to usually include only "displayable" data - not binary or 
packed decimal).  There are ways to get "binary" or "byte" files that do not 
have record delimiters, but these are relatively unusual.

Can you tell me WHY you want this to be a record sequential file?

As I think I said in an earlier note,  If you want to treat the data as 80-byte 
"records" but it is coming in as line sequential (with 1-80 bytes), then do the 
following

In the Select/Assign define it as LINE SEQUENTIAL

In the FD, use the
  Record Varying in Size from 1 to 80 depending on some-ws-item.

then define the record (under the FD) as
 01  MyRec.
      05  Each-Byte occurs 1 to 80 times depending on some-ws-item
                 Pic X.

Then include a FIXED length 80 byte record in WS (as well as some-ws-item)

Then in the procedure division, use
   Read whateverFile INTO 80-byte-ws-rec

This will give you a "fixed" 80-byte record in WS - while "successfully" reading 
your Line Sequential file.  It will "space pad" any records that you read that 
are shorter than 80 bytes.

NOTE:
  If you are getting CR after byte 48 or 49 (or wherever) in a record that you 
THINK has 80 bytes of "real data" (no trailing spaces) on the mainframe, then 
something else is going wrong with your download.  Can you tell us exactly how 
you are getting the file from the mainframe to AIX?  Give us EXACT details, so 
we can see if there may be a problem with your command.  (If the file on the 
mainframe has COMP, COMP_5, COMP_4 or USAGE BINARY data), then this may be 
causing the problem.  It may well have a binary (numeric) fields with X'0A" or 
X'0D' or one of the other values that will cause problems with downloading.  In 
fact, if you have ANY type of "non-character" data in the mainframe file, and 
you are doing "automatic" EBCDIC -> ASCII conversion in the download, you are 
likely to have problems.

  * * * * *
NOTE:
  There is another approach - which would get a "record sequential file" on AIX. 
That is to download the file from the mainframe in "binary".  The problem with 
this, is that it will leave you with EBCDIC data on AIX instead of "converting" 
it to ASCII.  I think that IBM's AIX compiler can handle this, but it is NOT the 
approach that I would suggest.
```

```
On Jul 28, 7:18 am, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
wrote:
> With a name like "studentfile", is there any chance that this is part of a COBOL
> class?  (either an instructor led class, or one from a book)?  If so, you may
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> have record delimiters, but these are relatively unusual.

Note that the standard line terminator varies with the system being
used:

MS-DOS/Windows:    CR+LF   x"0D0A"
Unix/Linux:        LF      x"0A"
Mac:               CR      x"0D"

So, on AIX, a LF (LineFeed) is expected.

Perhaps the problem is that IBM COBOL Line Sequential is reading up to
a LF and the file contains CR/LF. Thus the CR is appearing as a data
character.

A hex dump (hd) of the file should resolve this.
```

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-29T05:11:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0fVbm.491169$6p1.480592@en-nntp-02.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<8084fdf9-fd42-42dc-b9ce-6fef9dfbc590@s31g2000yqs.googlegroups.com> <7crfnaF2909gfU1@mid.individual.net> <0a654590-9e5a-45ad-a336-6d7cab5b773c@j32g2000yqh.googlegroups.com> <7ctjeaF28kqv0U1@mid.individual.net> <de4f994d-fdd2-4f1b-a10d-22defc3aa694@d9g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <90Ham.571560$641.245879@en-nntp-10.dc1.easynews.com> <7fb48fce-09f4-4278-8552-aac0b1f37e35@v36g2000yqv.googlegroups.com> <I8abm.575922$641.566646@en-nntp-10.dc1.easynews.com> <bbd0a0af-e1c5-41a7-a482-3147a165065f@e27g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <%3nbm.356198$2p1.170611@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <7524f053-effd-4604-834b-ca4233199378@j19g2000vbp.googlegroups.com>`

```
How are the 49th and 50th characters defined in your record layout?  Is it a
  USAGE COMP
field?  (This would be my first guess).

If so, you have a serious problem.  You are absolutely going to have to do some 
sort of "conversion" somewhere.

Can you tell us what software you are using to download the file (and exactly 
what command /options you are using).

Downloading a mainframe file that has COMP (or BINARY) data mixed with 
alphanumeric (character) data to AIX in a useable form definitely requires some 
work or some advanced tools somewhere.

Are you doing this at/for work?  Does anyone else work with getting files from 
the mainframe to AIX?

As I asked before, is this a class assignment (if it isn't for work).  If so, 
have you asked your instructor?

I hate to get "personal" in the questions, but to get where you want from where 
you are is (I think) a non-trivial task.

If you know COBOL (well enough) and have access to both the file on the 
mainframe and a COBOL compiler, the easiest thing (probably) would be to write a 
SIMPLE COBOL program on the mainframe to convert all COMP, COMP-3, 
Packed-Decimal, BINARY fields to "usage display" - using the SIGN IS SEPARATE 
clause; then to down load that file to AIX (which will now be fairly easy) and 
then to have another program to convert the impacted numeric fields back.

NOTE WELL:
  There are tools from some vendors that will take a record layout (such as a 
COBOL COPYBOOK) and do an "Intelligent" file transfer. To the best of my 
knowledge IBM COBOL for AIX does NOT include such a tool.  If your record layout 
includes REDEFINES, such conversions become even more difficult.

If you only have a few records with the X'0D' value in it, you could run grep or 
awk (or similar) on AIX to convert those bytes to something specific and have 
your program handle them that way.

You really should do a hex-dump (as suggested by others.  Depending on how you 
did the download, it is relatively likely that you ALSO have "LF" characters 
after the 80th byte of each record and before the next record.

P.S.  You did verify, didn't you, that the X'0D' value does exist in the file 
BEFORE the download (in byte 49)?  If not, then this too indicates some problem 
in how you are doing the download.
```

> On Jul 24, 4:15 pm, "Pete Dashwood"

this and you should take his advice.

need something other than STL when using a Sequential, fixed length dataset.

Good luck!

Pete.

<a id="t-8e515dac57"></a>
## Thread: PIC specs for non arithmetic digit fields — 3 msgs

### PIC specs for non arithmetic digit fields

- **From:** Graham Hobbs <ghobbs@cdpwise.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-23T09:56:00-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1rpg655ajt8lh86pl9o3ic4v55n63s5usc@4ax.com>`

```
Hello,

In a COBOL batch and CICS environment where many data fields are all
digits but never used for arithmetic, I'd be interested in knowing if
there exists any study that shows how installations have defined such
fields, e.g. student, part, vendor, policy, bank account numbers, etc.

Using a six digit field, I can think of:
PIC X(6).
PIC S9(6).
PIC 9(6).
PIC S9(4) COMP-3.
PIC S9(4) COMP.
Is/was/wiill there (be) a prevailing method?

I can think back many years when I often defined a field as one of the
first three above simply because it 'DISPLAY'ed nicely during testing
despite DASD constraints.
Thanks,
Graham Hobbs
```

#### ↳ Re: PIC specs for non arithmetic digit fields

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-07-23T08:17:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vqrg65h1uuntcg868clvup5r45mq8v9vli@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<1rpg655ajt8lh86pl9o3ic4v55n63s5usc@4ax.com>`

```
It is irritating when someone used some datum such as an ID number as
packed decimal just because it is numeric and mandatory.

Things change.   When we are mandated to not use Social Security
Numbers as the Personal ID, the option to use alphanumeric characters
is more expensive now.

At least packed decimal had the advantage of saving space back when.
But now it means we have to translate to decimal before FTPing to an
ANSI machine, and the space savings is not valuable anymore.

But even with PIC 999999999 USAGE DISPLAY, we can have data exceptions
if we decide to make it alphanumeric.    If we're not doing arithmetic
with the ID, make it alphanumeric.   We can do IF NUMERIC for editing.
```

#### ↳ Re: PIC specs for non arithmetic digit fields

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-07-23T09:42:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4d6dnUCuisd66_XXnZ2dnUVZ_o6dnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<1rpg655ajt8lh86pl9o3ic4v55n63s5usc@4ax.com>`

```
Graham Hobbs wrote:
> Hello,
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Graham Hobbs

Back in the olden days, COMP-3 or COMP-5 was often used to conserve space.

Today, in my view, if you can't do arithmetic on it, it's not a number and 
should not be defined as a number. There are a couple of reasons:

* Your example of easy display
* The definition of the field may change. In the U.S., postal codes are 
numeric, but if you have somebody who lives in Canada, where they're not, 
you're a goner.

In fact, I'd expand the definition of using DISPLAY to numeric fields that 
are not totally internal to a program (such as subscripts and indexes). That 
is, master and intermediate files should not render numerics in anything but 
S9(n) or similar.
```

Hi,

I work at a company called MAKE Technologies (http://
www.maketechnologies.com) and we sell a solution to modernize legacy
systems (PL/1, RPG, Natural, PowerBuilder, ...) into modern platforms
like Java and .NET. A while ago Micro Focus created their Cobolator
videos, and we just finished a tongue-in-cheek response that you might
enjoy: a presentation of Agile COBOL 2009, http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ELdYYE2F7F0

Mik
```

Industrial Application (John Ion)

Kang)

Biomaterials: The Intersection of Biology and Materials Science
(Temenoff & Mikos)

Ed., Ratner, Hoffman, Schoen & Lemons)
Introduction to Materials Management (6th Ed., Arnold, Chapman &
Clive)
Fundamentals of Solid State Engineering (2nd Ed., Manijeh Razeghi)
Smart Electronic Materials: Fundamentals and Applications (Jasprit
Singh)

Chawla)
